column22:
One of the coolest projects I’ve built is an AI-powered digital painting platform. The idea was to let users describe what they wanted to draw in natural language, and the system would translate that into actions inside the painting application. For example, a user could type something like “draw a blue circle and fill it,” and the program would interpret that instruction and execute it on the canvas.

I built the application in Java using JavaFX and integrated an LLM API to interpret user prompts and convert them into structured commands. A big part of the project was designing the architecture so that natural language inputs could be reliably mapped to painting actions. It was really interesting working through problems like input validation, command parsing, and making sure the system behaved predictably even when prompts were ambiguous.

I liked this project because it combined creativity with engineering. It felt like building a bridge between human language and a graphical system, and it made me think a lot about how AI can make software interfaces more intuitive.
column23:
My dream project would be building something like a “debugger for the real world.” In software, we have great tools to understand what is happening inside a system. We have logs, traces, and error messages that help us figure out why something broke. In the physical world, we often do not have that kind of visibility until something goes wrong.

I would love to build a platform that collects data from sensors and systems around a city and turns it into something people can actually understand and act on. It could look at things like traffic patterns, transit delays, building energy usage, or even food waste in cafeterias. The goal would be to identify patterns and surface insights like why certain areas consistently get congested or where resources are being wasted.

The interesting part would be making the system interactive. City planners or organizations could test ideas, simulate changes, and see how the system might respond before making real decisions. It would feel similar to debugging a complex piece of software, except the system you are analyzing is a city.

I like this idea because it connects software, data, and real-world problems. Instead of just building apps, it would help people understand and improve the systems that affect everyday life.

One of the coolest projects I’ve built is LegalAssist, an AI-powered legal workflow platform designed to help lawyers manage case information and generate structured documents more efficiently.

The platform uses React, Next.js, and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) to process legal documents and provide grounded responses. It includes features such as structured client intake, case tracking, risk scoring, and AI-assisted document drafting. By organizing information through standardized forms and retrieval pipelines, the system reduces drafting time and helps lawyers prioritize cases more effectively.

The project focuses heavily on traceability and reliability, ensuring that AI-generated responses are grounded in uploaded documents instead of hallucinated outputs. I also designed analytics-ready data structures so the platform could support reporting and future automation.

This project reflects my interest in building practical AI systems that solve real operational problems rather than just demos.

column23:
My dream project would be to build an AI “operations co-pilot” that helps teams make faster and better decisions when things get complex.

In many organizations, the problem isn’t a lack of data, it’s that information lives in too many places. Important context is scattered across dashboards, documents, emails, and internal tools. When a critical decision needs to be made, teams spend a lot of time just trying to gather the right information before they can even start analyzing it.

I would want to build a platform that brings all of that information together and helps people understand what is happening and what their options are. The system would use a combination of large language models, machine learning, and agent-based workflows to gather data, analyze patterns, and generate possible recommendations.

Instead of simply giving answers, the platform would show why it made a recommendation. For example, it could highlight the data sources used, explain the assumptions behind a prediction, and allow users to explore different “what-if” scenarios. The goal would not be to replace human decision-makers, but to help them reach informed conclusions faster.

I’m particularly interested in how a system like this could support fields where decisions really matter, such as healthcare operations, emergency response, climate planning, or large-scale infrastructure management.

For a hackathon version, I would focus on building a small prototype that takes structured data and documents, runs analysis on them, and produces clear recommendations along with the supporting evidence. Even a simple version of this idea could demonstrate how AI can help people turn complex information into actionable insights.

column22: One of the coolest projects I’ve built was a real-time Software-Defined Radio (SDR) system on a Raspberry Pi 4. The goal was to explore whether RF signals could be captured, processed, and demodulated entirely in software on a low-cost embedded platform, rather than relying on traditional hardware-heavy radio architectures. We built a modular DSP pipeline in C++ and Python that handled IQ acquisition, filtering, FFT-based spectral analysis, and real-time demodulation with live visualization. One of the biggest challenges was meeting real-time performance constraints as Python alone wasn’t fast enough, which forced us to migrate our signal processing into optimized C++ and carefully redesign our buffering strategy. We also faced multithreading synchronization bugs that caused dropped samples, which we resolved using properly managed ring buffers and clearer module boundaries. Working in a four-person team using GitHub, we learned to define strict interfaces between components to avoid integration conflicts. This project strengthened my understanding of hardware-software co-design, real-time systems, and collaborative engineering, and it remains one of the most technically rewarding systems I’ve built.
column23: My dream project is to build a modular, open hardware driver-assistance and vehicle telemetry platform that makes advanced automotive safety and performance tools accessible to everyday drivers. Inspired by systems like openpilot and companies like Tesla, I’d want to create a plug-and-play embedded system that connects to a car’s CAN bus, fuses data from cameras and sensors, and runs efficient real-time perception models on edge hardware. The goal wouldn’t just be autonomy, it would be driver insight: detecting fatigue, monitoring battery health, analyzing suspension or brake behavior, and giving drivers actionable feedback in a clean, intuitive interface. From a hardware perspective, it would involve custom PCB design, power regulation for noisy automotive environments, and robust sensor integration. From a software perspective, it would require structured system architecture, embedded firmware, real-time data processing, and a thoughtfully designed UX. What excites me most is the intersection of electrical engineering, embedded systems, and intelligent software, building something physically tangible that also learns, adapts, and improves over time.

My dream AI project is to build a personalized job search assistant designed for students and job applicants. Applying to over 100 positions and tailoring a resume and cover letter each time is extremely time consuming. I personally relate to this since I am actively looking for co-op and the process is really frustrating. But we have to keep going.

So, my idea is to create a platform where users build a detailed profile including their projects, skills, experience, and job preferences. The AI would continuously scan job postings, match them to the user’s profile, and recommend highly relevant roles. If a user selects a specific job, the system would automatically tailor the resume by prioritizing the most relevant skills and projects, generating a customized version instantly.

It would also allow users to follow specific companies and receive alerts as soon as new roles are posted. Security and data protection would be a core focus to ensure personal information remains safe.

The goal is to reduce friction in the job search process, save time, and help candidates apply more strategically and efficiently.

column22:
The coolest project I've built is a live socket challenge handler during FantasyCTF 2026 where my team placed 2nd. The challenge sent random problems over a raw TCP connection where you had to identify the problem type, solve it, and send the answer back within seconds, all programmatically. I built a Python solver that connected over socket, parsed incoming text in real-time, dynamically identified the problem type using regex (math equations, graph theory, Chinese Remainder Theorem, matrix determinants, knapsack problems, longest common subsequence), then routed to the appropriate algorithm and returned solutions, all mid-connection with no prior knowledge of what was coming next.
What made this cool wasn't just the technical implementation rather it was the problem-solving under pressure. I had to debug live while the socket was open, handle edge cases on the fly, and optimize algorithms fast enough to beat the time limits. It combined networking, algorithms, pattern recognition, and real-time debugging all at once. This project taught me more about adapting to unknown requirements and building resilient systems than any tutorial ever could.
GitHub: github.com/riaasehgal/ISSessionsFantasyCTF2026
Specific file: gauntletctf.py - the live socket solver
column23:
I want to build a security operations assistant that helps junior analysts investigate alerts by automatically correlating SIEM data with threat intelligence, reconstructing attack timelines from logs, and generating investigation reports in natural language—but the key is it learns from analyst feedback. When an analyst marks an alert false positive and explains why, the system learns that pattern. When they escalate an incident, it understands what made it critical. Over time, it gets better at surfacing what matters and filtering noise.
The technical approach would combine:
- Backend pipeline: Real-time log ingestion from Security Onion/Splunk, parsed and enriched with threat intel from MITRE ATT&CK and OSINT feeds
- ML model: Trained on historical incident data to identify patterns analysts care about, not just statistical anomalies
- NLP layer: Generates human-readable summaries explaining why something is suspicious, not just that it's suspicious
- Feedback loop: Analysts can correct the system's assumptions, and it updates its model accordingly
Right now, you need experienced analysts to run a SOC. This could let smaller organizations get 80% of SOC value with one security person by automating repetitive investigation work while keeping humans in decision-making.

column22:
A cool project I got to take part in was an Android app for the University of Ottawa's bionics team that served as a health metrics endpoint, showing aggregated data fetched live from an exoskeleton arm built by the mechanical team. The exoskeleton arm, which our mechanics team had the opportunity to present at CUBEC, is a 3D-printed wearable assistive device that provides basic mechanised assistance and carries sensors for biometrics, including blood pressure, heartbeat, body temperature, and oxygen level.
The data-flow begins from an ESP32 via MQTT to an app we initially built in Android Studio with Java and Kotlin (it’s currently being rebuilt for both iOS and Android). The app caches live readings to update graphs in the UI that persist during offline access of the app, while aggregating sessions of biometric readings into summaries stored in Firestore for long-term analytics.
column23:
My dream project is probably an image-to-image diffusion model that has been tuned and modified with LoRas to extensively imitate my own art style in order to generate portraits so that I can create my own artwork that was trained off of my own hand-drawn assets for personal use. 
    As someone who loves to draw and design as a hobby while also really appreciating the convenience of generative AI, I've always wanted to explore bridging the gap more and bringing diffusion models closer to the actual integrity of hand-drawn art.  Exploring building a pipeline that generates asset-built images using my own art assets in order to generate more "hand-drawn" artwork is probably how I would create training sets sufficient enough in quantity to perform traditional fine-tuning.
    In order to tune the diffusion model, I first would use MediaPipe or other existing ML solutions that extract and output facial landmark and segmentation information, then apply parameterized transformation to existing assets to fit the extracted facial landmarks, which would then serve as an asset builder for constructing a "drawing" from layered PNGs of body parts. Using the asset builder, I would then generate composited drawings based on portraits of which the most accurate outputs can be used to iteratively build a dataset for fine-tuning. 
    It would likely be a combination of hand-drawn labour (for asset building) for style details and stylization accuracy that streamlines into full ML-based later down the line. It'd also be a pretty time-consuming project with a niche purpose that serves no real interest other than personal use but it's definitely a passion project I'd undertake if given unlimited time and freedom.

column22:
It's definitely got to be beeprepared! The project was so cool in the sense of being a genuinely useful tool/application that I can't help but feel proud of! I worked entirely on the backend but the process of this specific project, of ideation into designing and architecting the data pipelines + async workflows into focusing on the core logic first, before wiring everything together into an async pipeline via FastApi background tasks that leverage 2 separate handlers, implementing a node based DAG for the data storage, wiring everything together near the end, and hooking it all up to the frontend via HTTP Polling and tanstack query, it felt like the full software development life cycle and I'm incredibly satisfied with the end result: something genuinely polished, clean, and impressive. 

But more importantly, it genuinely taught me so much about the hackathon process; I felt like I unlocked the secret on how to do hackathons then, and I am trying to persist that process into my current ones to build things that I can genuinely point to as polished finished pieces!

I'd love to make an anti vibe coding IDE that promotes active learning; it would guide the user through projects, never revealing to them the actual code of course. 

The emphasis would be on something like math, where we provide the user some direction + fundamentals, and really let them implement the core code on their own. Moreover, we'd use LaTeX diagrams, mini quizzes, an actual full on curriculum, an example + active learning based approach, a finite state machine to detect the current mentor state and the project state to provide a calibrated response at any given point in time (allowing custom guided projects), and upskilling by focusing on a tutor-like learning experience.

Of course such a project is incredibly difficult (an IDE!) to make, margins are razor thin in the student and education space, and it is incredibly ambitious to do so, yet I find the idea so compelling in that I would genuinely use it! There is definitely a lot of competitors, but the core thesis is coherent, a problem faced my many more than ever now, and the idea (vibe coding being a genuine problem) is genuinely itself something I would LOVE to use!

column22:
My coolest project so far is FluxRoute, a web app I built in a 36‑hour hackathon to fix how painful it is to compare ways of getting around the GTA. Instead of juggling separate tabs for Google Maps, Transit, Uber, etc., FluxRoute lets you enter an origin and destination, then uses APIs and a lightweight ML scoring system to compare driving, transit, and hybrid routes side‑by‑side by speed, cost, and “stress level” in real time.
​

Under the hood, I built a small pipeline that normalizes data from multiple routing sources, then calculates a composite score that balances travel time, price estimates, and number of transfers or mode switches (our proxy for stress). I handled the full stack in the hackathon window: designing the UI for quick route comparison, wiring up the backend, and deploying a working demo that judges could use live. What made FluxRoute especially cool to me was watching people plug in their usual commute and realize how different their options looked once everything was quantified and visualized in one place—exactly the kind of practical, real‑world impact I want my projects to have. https://drive.google.com/drive/home?dmr=1&ec=wgc-drive-%5Bmodule%5D-goto

column22:
One of the coolest projects I’ve built is an AI-powered meeting transcription tool designed for Google Meet sessions.

The goal of the project was to automatically convert meeting conversations into structured text while identifying who spoke at what time. The system records the meeting audio, performs speech-to-text transcription, and uses speaker diarization to detect different speakers in the conversation.

To improve accuracy, I integrated the Google Meet API to retrieve participant information and match the detected speakers with the actual participants in the meeting. The tool then generates a timestamped transcript and stores it in a text file so that users can easily review the meeting discussion later.

This project was exciting because it combined natural language processing, API integration, and real-time data processing to solve a practical problem, helping teams keep track of meeting discussions without manually taking notes.

Working on this project taught me a lot about handling audio processing pipelines, integrating external APIs, and designing systems that map technical outputs (like diarization segments) to real-world information such as meeting participants.

column22:
One of the coolest projects I’ve built is UrbanSight, a smart-city issue reporting platform created during a hackathon.

The goal of UrbanSight was to make it easier for citizens to report infrastructure problems in their city such as broken streetlights, potholes, or unsafe areas. Instead of complicated municipal reporting systems, users can simply upload a photo, add a description, and submit the issue through a simple interface.

The platform was built using a full-stack architecture with Firebase, including Firestore for real-time data storage, Firebase Authentication for secure user login, and Cloud Storage for image uploads. The application also supports real-time updates so new reports appear instantly for other users and administrators.

One of the most exciting parts of the project was building it as a progressive web application, which allows it to work across devices without requiring installation.

The project was recognized as a Runner-Up in the United Nations SDG 11 Smart Cities Challenge, which was incredibly rewarding because it showed that the idea could have real social impact.
column23:
If I had unlimited time and resources, I would build a large-scale AI platform that helps people make better life and career decisions.

Today, people often make major decisions — choosing a degree, moving to a new city, switching careers, or starting a business — with very limited information. Most advice comes from scattered sources like forums, social media posts, or personal opinions.

The idea would be to create a decision-simulation platform powered by AI and real-world data.

A user could input a life decision such as:

“Should I move to Toronto for a software job?”
“Should I start a startup or continue working?”
“Is switching careers into tech a good choice for me?”

The system would analyze data such as job market trends, salary growth, cost of living, economic indicators, and skill demand. It would then generate multiple realistic scenarios — optimistic, realistic, and worst-case — to help people understand possible outcomes.

The goal wouldn’t be to tell people what to do, but to give them better insights before making major life decisions.

This project would combine several areas that interest me deeply: artificial intelligence, data analysis, and building products that can genuinely improve people’s lives.

column22:
My ABSOLUTE favourite project is Vocabbit, a Chrome extension I originally built to help me improve my Albanian while browsing the web. I come from an Albanian family, but since I was the only one born in Canada, my language skills were never quite as strong as my relatives. Whenever I tried reading Albanian news articles or Reddit posts, I would run into unfamiliar words, look them up, then completely forget about them afterwards.

I was getting really frustrated with traditional language apps/extensions because there was no easy way to save those new words in context (and also some of them were a little too pricey for my liking). I wanted a way to build my vocabulary naturally while reading real content, without having to switch tabs or write things down manually. So, I built Vocabbit.

What started as a small tool just for me ended up being the first project I ever fully deployed. Seeing people actually use something I made has been incredibly rewarding. Right now, it has over 45 downloads and about 10 to 20 daily active users.

Building it taught me a ton about browser APIs, client-side storage, and complex DOM interactions. The biggest challenge was definitely the text highlight restoration system. I had to figure out how to keep highlights saved even after a page reloads, and make sure the system worked across tons of different website layouts. This meant diving deep into the DOM Range API, XPath, and IndexedDB.

It was also pretty tricky to get those highlights to behave on websites that dynamically load their content. Debugging all those edge cases forced me to really understand web page structure and exactly how browser extensions interact with the DOM.

With that being said, Vocabbit is easily my favourite project because I managed to turn a personal problem I wanted to solve into something that actually helps real life users.


Extension Link: 
https://chromewebstore.google.com/detail/vocabbit/jebgmmpnlcabdfjhnkcnndjljiddgcjo
column23:
If I had all the time and resources in the world, I’d build a super realistic simulator for car software and low-level programming. I’ve always been interested in how car software works, but I have to be honest and admit that I’ve really struggled to actually understand it (I genuinely suck at low-level programming). Also, low-level stuff is just way less forgiving than other dev work. If you mess up a car’s ECU or TCU, you could easily ruin the entire car.

With that being said, I would love to create a platform that perfectly mimics every sensor and circuit in a car. With unlimited resources and time, I could buy every car model out there and reverse-engineer their systems to create a sandbox that feels just like the real thing. With the sandbox, I imagine you'd open it up and see a full interactive map of a car's electrical system, and you could zoom into any part of it like the ECU, the CAN bus, or individual sensors and start writing code against them in real time. It would basically feel like a code editor and a 3D vehicle explorer rolled into one, with live feedback showing exactly how your changes affect everything else in the system.

This platform would give people an endless supply of virtual cars to experiment on, where you could break systems and reset them with a single click. The whole point would be to encourage more people to get into car modding and pushing their car to the limit, by taking away the fear that people have of destroying their own car.

What makes this project exciting is that it combines AI reasoning with real infrastructure problems. The coolest project I have built is Pinpoint, a map first student housing platform we built at QHacks 2026, where we won the City of Kingston Prize Track. We built it because finding housing in Kingston is chaotic: listings are scattered across platforms, prices are unclear, roommates are found separately, and location matters but nothing is designed around a map. Pinpoint puts listings and people looking for roommates on one shared map, lets users filter by budget and preferences, and supports natural language search like “non smoking 2 bedroom near campus under 1000” to highlight the best options instantly. It felt cool because it was not just a flashy demo, it was a working MVP aimed at a real student problem.

column23:
I would build a tool called Storyboarding from Real Life: you upload a short real world video and it instantly turns into a comic style storyboard you can edit, then export as a PDF or image. This sounds simple, but the current workflow is honestly painful. People scrub through videos, take random screenshots, paste them into PowerPoint or Photoshop, crop everything manually, and still end up with something messy.

My approach would be to make the flow feel magical but still controllable. First, the system picks key frames based on motion and scene changes, then lays them out into a clean storyboard grid. The main value is editing: dragging panels to reorder the story, cropping to focus on faces or objects, resizing panels to control pacing, and adding short captions or bubbles. The same video could tell different stories just by changing order and framing, which is what makes it genuinely fun and useful.

If I had unlimited time and resources, I would make it fast, collaborative, and good enough that students, creators, and film people actually use it instead of the screenshot grind.

Meerkat is an open source plugin for Blender (the 3d development software) that allows for real time collaboration amongst entire 3d development teams. column21:
I've won a few of them now (Rosetta, SecureFlow), and what I keep coming back to isn't the wins. It's the 2 AM debugging sessions where everything is broken and you're somehow having the time of your life. BearHacks feels like exactly that kind of environment, and I genuinely can't stay away from it.
Right now I'm splitting my time between an AI/ML internship with the Ontario Public Service, research on trustworthy medical AI systems, and leading the AI & Data Society at York. So I spend a lot of time thinking about what it means to build things that actually matter, not just things that technically work. BearHacks is a chance to take all of that energy and compress it into something real, fast, and a little chaotic.
I also want to be honest: I learn more in a 24-hour hackathon than I sometimes do in a whole semester. There's something about a hard deadline and a half-formed idea that forces you to stop overthinking and just build. And then, if you're lucky, you get to see someone else's project and realize they solved a completely different problem in a way you never would've thought of. That part, the cross-pollination of ideas, is what I'm really here for.
Plus, I've been on the organizing side of hackathons too (hey, ElleHacks!), so I know the amount of work that goes into making one great. Coming in as a participant is its own kind of privilege, and I don't take it lightly.
So yeah, I'm in for the building, the breaking, the learning, and the very real possibility that I'll be explaining a pivot to my teammates at 4 AM. Wouldn't have it any other way.
column22:
Here's a response in Aryan's voice:

---

Okay so I have to talk about Rosetta, because it's the project that made me feel like I actually knew what I was doing for the first time (spoiler: I was mostly figuring it out as I went).

The idea was simple enough: a real-time lecture translation platform. You speak, it transcribes, translates into one of 6 languages, and reads it back out loud. Low latency, live, in the browser. Easy, right? Wrong. Extremely wrong.

The part that broke my brain in the best way was the RAG pipeline. I wanted students to be able to upload their course slides and have the system surface inline citations while generating notes. So I ended up building a local ML pipeline using KeyBERT for keyword extraction, BGE embeddings for semantic search, and a TinyBERT re-ranker to actually make the results useful. No cloud calls, no API costs, just models running locally and somehow keeping up with a live lecture.

There was a moment at around 3 AM where the transcription, the translation, the TTS output, and the citation retrieval all worked together for the first time. One pipeline, end to end, in real time. I just sat there staring at it for a second before my teammate and I completely lost it.

We won the hackathon. But honestly, the win felt secondary to that moment where it just... worked.

What I'm most proud of isn't the stack or the award. It's that we built something I would've actually wanted as a student who grew up in a multilingual household. That one felt personal. Those are the projects that stick with you.

GitHub: https://github.com/AbeerDas/Rosetta-HackHive
Devpost: https://hackhive-2026.devpost.com/

One of the coolest projects I’ve built is a VS Code extension called HaptiCode, which my team and I developed in just 48 hours during a hackathon. The goal of the project was to make coding more accessible, especially for visually impaired developers or individuals who may not be able to use their hands easily.

HaptiCode allows users to write and control code using only voice commands. Instead of typing, a user can speak commands like creating files, writing code, navigating through files, or editing functions. The extension converts voice input into programming actions directly inside VS Code.

Technically, we built the project using Next.js and React, and integrated Gemini API and OpenAI APIs to process voice input and convert it into meaningful coding commands. The AI helps interpret natural language instructions and transform them into structured programming actions.

What made this project exciting was seeing how AI and voice interfaces can break accessibility barriers in software development. It showed us how technology can empower people who might otherwise struggle with traditional coding tools.

here is the Github Repo:https://github.com/katu2005/hapticode.git

My coolest project was developed at my last hackathon and it was a web app called Evidentia (which you can go check out at https://www.evidentias.tech/)!   

My friends and I developed Evidentia to help people understand and compare dense Terms of Service and Privacy Policies by turning them into an easy to read, evidence backed reports. Instead of producing a generic summary, Evidentia would look for specific red flags, find evidence through quoted text, and present the results in a checklist format. We also implemented side by side comparisons so users could see what changed between two policies. The frontend was developed using React, the backend with FastAPI, and then the app was deployed using Render and Vercel.   

I am so proud of this project because I was able to fully deploy the project even with all the obstacles we faced. The biggest obstacle I faced was in trying to make outputs more consistent. Since I had developed an agentic Ai as the backbone of the project, it was prone to errors and inconsistencies, so I had to spend upwards of 2-3 hours constantly refining each of the prompts and developing safeguards until I was happy with the results being outputted. The biggest breakthrough was when I utilized the cache and past results to speed up responses, so if the text being pasted in had been previously read, the program wouldn't force another call to the AI saving time and resources.  

Here is the link to the GitHub with a demo video being included in the README section https://github.com/JustinM12345/evidentia https://www.evidentias.tech/

column22:
One of the coolest projects I’ve built is Membership Passport, a platform my team developed for the Consultech 2026 case competition, where we placed 3rd overall. The goal was to address a real problem: many organizations struggle with member engagement and retention, especially after the first year.

We designed Membership Passport, a system that turns membership into a progression journey similar to collecting passport stamps. Members earn stamps by attending events, participating in programs, or engaging with community initiatives. As they collect stamps, they progress through membership tiers, which encourages continued participation rather than treating membership as a one-time subscription.

Technically, I helped build a full-stack web platform using Next.js, Tailwind CSS, and Supabase. The system includes role-based dashboards for business members, government members, and administrators. Members can track their progress, view upcoming events, and access resources, while administrators can manage events, membership levels, and engagement data through an admin dashboard.

What I enjoyed most about this project was combining product thinking with engineering—designing a system that doesn’t just function technically but also changes user behavior and engagement through gamification.

(Links: https://devpost.com/software/membership-passport)
column23:
Children don't lack intelligence or potential, they lack the access to resources. Growing up in a metropolitan area in Vietnam, I've seen this gap in regions such as rural and mountainuous areas. According to Vietnam’s General Statistics Office, there is approximately 60–70% of ethnic minority populations living in mountainous regions, where schools face limited budgets and lack skilled teachers, technology, and learning resources is scarce. In such provinces, students have to take a long and difficult path to school, and opportunities to learn advanced fields such as programming and financial literacy are almost non-existent. 

If I had unlimited time and resources, I would build a network of mobile teaching robots inspired by EVE from Wall-E movie that could bring quality education to remote areas. Each village would have a community center or like a library and an EVE robot so students don't have to travel across mountains everyday. These robots would project a large interactive screen and act as TAs (teaching assistants). Qualified teachers would control lessons remotely, deliver live classes and send materials. This solution can reach thousands of students and learning activities are guided by teachers hundreds of kilometers away. Also, EVE robots could run self-paced learning sessions, where students review lessons, practice math problems, or explore topics like coding or science experiments.

column22: The coolest project I've built is a bot for a game called League of Legends. I originally started this project back when I was in highschool because the process of levelling up accounts was extremely tedious. I started researching how to implement this and I found 2 options: 1. Read through the game's memory to detect things in the game 2. Use screenshots of the game to detect things. I decided to go with option 2, because even though reading memory is much faster than taking screenshots and processing them, League of Legends could detect that you're reading their memory and ban your account. So I used a library called OpenCV to handle the image processing and mostly used a function called Template Matching to detect things on screen. With these detections I wrote some simple if statements to handle(with pyautogui) different events in the game. Here's my github repo: https://github.com/thisaja/League-of-Legends-Bot

One of the most exciting things I’ve built is BrewChat, a web app that helps people prepare better for interviews by turning casual coffee chats into something useful. The idea came when I realized how many great convos we have with seniors or mentors about job prep, but then forget most of it the next day. I wanted a way to save those insights.
So I made a platform where users can record their chats, and it automatically transcribes and summarizes them. Users can store summaries, reflect on what they talked about, and even go through others’ chats to learn from their experience. Before this, I barely knew how to handle audio or work with the APIs, so figuring it all out step by step was honestly the best part. 
There were some challenges. Handling noisy audio, cleaning up messy transcripts, keeping the UI smooth... but I liked solving those things. BrewChat became more than a side project. It felt like I was solving a real problem people face while preparing for internships.

column22: One of the coolest projects I built was an AI-powered fitness journal that tracks workouts and provides context-aware guidance through a chatbot. The system uses a multi-step AI pipeline with guardrails, retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), and a QA stage to ensure responses are accurate and safe. I built the frontend with Next.js and TypeScript, the backend with Node.js and Python, and used PostgreSQL to store users, workouts, exercises, and metrics. The most interesting challenge was designing the RAG pipeline so the chatbot could reliably answer questions using structured workout data instead of generating generic fitness advice.

column22:
One of the coolest projects I’ve built is Shop-a-Sketch, which I created at UofTHacks in January.

The idea came from how frustrating online shopping can be when you don’t know the right words to describe what you’re looking for. Sometimes you have a picture in your head, but typing search terms just doesn’t work.

With Shop-a-Sketch, users can draw a quick sketch of the item they want and the system tries to find visually similar products. Instead of relying on keywords, the search is based on the shape and features of the drawing.

I worked on the backend of building the drawing interface and the system that processes the sketch and compares it to product images. One of the biggest challenges was figuring out how to make a very rough human sketch actually match real product images in a meaningful way.

What I loved about this project is that it felt playful but still solved a real problem. Watching people draw messy little sketches and still get useful results was really satisfying!

GitHub: https://github.com/clairez308/uofthacks26
Devpost: https://devpost.com/software/shop-a-sketch 
Deployed URL: https://shop-a-sketch.tech/
column23:
If I had unlimited time and resources, I would want to build a platform that helps young students explore different career paths in tech in a more interactive way.

Growing up, I always knew I liked technology and STEM, but I struggled a lot with figuring out what that actually meant. Tech is such a huge field with software, AI, design, product, research, data, security... it can be really hard to understand what those careers actually look like day to day.

I’ve spent a lot of time mentoring young girls in STEM and leading workshops, and confidence and exposure make a huge difference. So I’d love to build a tool where students could explore different roles through small hands-on challenges or simulated projects. For example, they could try designing an app interface, debugging a small piece of code, or solving a data problem, and the platform could show them what kinds of careers align with what they enjoy.

For this product, the goal wouldn’t be to tell students what they should do, but to give them a space to explore and build confidence while discovering what excites them. I think having something like that would have helped me a lot when I was younger (and honestly, even now!)

column23: As someone who makes music as a hobby, I often end up with multiple versions of the same song demo with file names like final, finalv2, super_duper_finalv67 and it becomes increasingly harder to track what changed , why it changed and which version sounds the best. The idea would be to create this GIT like tool for Digital Audio Workstations that would help artists track edits, compare versions, document these creative decisions and collab across demos, stems(individual instrument tracks) and sessions. So instead of saving duplicate project files, users would be able to view a version history, add notes to changes, branch ideas and roll back to previous versions without losing history. The main technical challenge I'm currently facing is capturing and preserving plugin states across versions since DAW plugins store complex parameter configs, dependencies all typically embedded inside proprietary project files. So yea, that's definitely something I'm super interested in working on once I get done with school.

column22:
This mechatronics gem, completed from September to December 2024, balances a steel ball on a dynamic surface through a custom-built parallel robot setup. I integrated real-time embedded systems with PID control for precise, adaptive positioning, tackling noise reduction, direct port tweaks, and full FMEA to ensure rock-solid stability across varying conditions.​​
Project Highlights

    Mechanical build fused electrical hardware and software for seamless operation, mimicking advanced flight simulators or precision robotics platforms.​​

    Stood out over others like my network-wide Pi ad blocker (70% ad cut via Raspberry Pi and Python) or 3-DOF robotic arm (modular for industrial tasks) due to its hardcore control challenges and real-world robotics relevance.​​

It sharpened my skills in Ontario Tech's robotics courses, blending theory with hands-on innovation-perfect prep for cutting-edge automation roles.
column23:
With unlimited time and resources, I'd build a fully autonomous swarm robotics system for disaster response. This project would deploy 20-50 lightweight robots that collaborate via mesh networks to map disaster zones, locate survivors, and deliver supplies—using quantized vision/language models for real-time obstacle avoidance, victim detection, and decision-making without cloud dependency.
Core Innovation

The swarm would run simulation-first development with a digital twin in NVIDIA Isaac Sim, mirroring physical tests in Gazebo for safe iteration on complex behaviors like decentralized pathfinding and resource allocation. Each robot integrates servo actuators (inspired by my Project Octopus designs), cameras, and ultrasonic sensors, with on-device LLMs handling multimodal inputs for "agentic" reasoning—e.g., prioritizing tasks based on thermal scans or voice cues.
Technical Stack

    Hardware: Custom PCBs with ESP32-S3 for edge compute, high-torque servos (<800g total per unit), and recyclable composites for sustainability.

    AI: Quantized 4B-8B models (Q4_K_M) for low-latency inference, trained on human demo data converted to robot policies.​

    Software: ROS 2 for control, Python edge computing, and a React dashboard for oversight.​

Real-World Impact

It solves key hackathon judging criteria like execution, impact, and creativity by addressing search-and-rescue gaps-faster mapping than single drones, resilient to comms failures. Perfect for BearHacks 2026's innovative vibe at Sheridan.

Essentially, we provide a knowledge base for businesses (akin to Google Drive, OneDrive, etc.), however we provide additional admin customization and features. One of the main selling points was allowing semantic search:

So normally you create a doc: "yellow, spiky, sweet, fruit" (a document all about pineapples), and you search 'pineapple', your file explorer will NOT find it! However, our Lapis service would find the document because we create vector embeddings with AI allowing each document to be searchable by words not directly in the file.

The coolest project I have built was an outfit planner database application that I worked on with two classmates at my university's introductory programming course. It stood out to me because it was the first computer science project I have created after switching from a Biomedical Sciences major. Since my background was mainly in biology, chemistry, and physics, stepping into software development came with a very steep learning curve. Unlike many students who had been exposed to programming for years, I was entering a completely new field and learning everything from the ground up. Oddly, despite the struggles, I enjoyed the process of developing this project tremendously. 
For this project, our team had to learn and apply technologies such as React, Node.js, and JavaScript while building both the front end and back end of the application. The program we were building would allow users to manage different outfit options and generate randomized outfit suggestions. Developing it required us to think carefully about data organization, server-side logic, and user experience, while also troubleshooting many technical issues along the way. What made the experience especially memorable was the timing: it was an end-of-semester group project due during final exam season, so the pressure was high. Even so, we kept working through the challenges, and seeing the finished product function successfully was incredibly rewarding. That project was meaningful to me not just because of the technical skills I gained, but because it proved to me that I could succeed in a field that was once entirely unfamiliar.
column23: If I had all the time and resources in the world, I would want to create a comprehensive transportation software platform that consolidates and optimizes all possible transit routes for commuters. As a student who relies heavily on public transportation to travel to and from school, I have experienced firsthand how stressful delays (due snow days, high volume traffic times) and missed transfers can be. While tools like Google Maps are useful, they do not always provide the best option when a trip involves multiple transfers or when conditions change in real time. In those situations, commuters often have to improvise quickly, combining different forms of transit to avoid long delays and still reach their destination on time. I would love to build a system that helps solve exactly that problem. My goal would be to create software that not only suggests routes, but also adapts dynamically when delays happen. For example, if a first bus is running late and a commuter is likely to miss the next transfer, the platform could immediately recommend the best alternative course of action to minimize travel time. I think a tool like this could make commuting far less stressful and more efficient, especially for students and workers who depend on transit every day. More than anything, I would want it to give commuters peace of mind by helping them make informed decisions in unpredictable situations.

I think the coolest projects are those that blend seamlessly into everyday life. Even though I have built projects on a bigger scale in the past, the only thing I built for myself that I use almost daily is a Chrome extension that runs locally.

For one of our courses in Computer Science, a large chunk of material is delivered via a website called PCRS. In there are a list of videos that students have to watch in order to learn. What I have noticed is that under each video, there is a transcript text file (courtesy of the instructors). What I also noticed is how painful it is going over those videos again when preparing for midterms. And so I created a Chrome extension that can detect those transcript files, feed the content back to Gemini's API to generate summaries of those videos. Just before writing this, I also added the ability to generate quizzes based on the transcript material. 

In terms of technical difficulty, this is an extremely easy project. I knew how to use Gemini's API, and I knew how to find the exact hyperlink labelled 'transcript.txt', and I knew how to parse that content to the API and write prompts to instruct the model to generate responses. I also knew how to use Cursor to build the entire front end in less than 15 minutes. What I am most proud of, however, is the idea and the keen observation that something can be improved, and the actions that followed to make the change real:)

Here is a screenshot! Take a look: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1ITJ-jQNuK89VD1mj10-NtAeYXi-vergq/view?usp=drive_link
column23:
I would go back and perfect an old project I made 3 years ago.

One of my most ambitious projects was called the Automatic Irrigation System (AIS), which I built with friends for our science club in high school. The idea occurred after our field trip to a mountainous area in Vietnam, where we noticed the tea farmers had to monitor the soil's moisture and air temperature by conducting dangerous trips, even though everything can be controlled by clever use of technology. 

We ended up with a prototype for the AIS, where we had two sensors to monitor the soil's moisture and air temperature. We then fed the data into a chip every few hours, with preset threshold values. If the moisture dropped too significantly, or the temperature became too hot (mountainous areas in Vietnam are often dry and hot), a carefully designed system of water sprinklers would start spraying water until those values fall in the safe zone.

However, a product designed by a group of high school students did not come without flaws. For once, it was unscalable: Our prototype was tested on only 3 types of crops in an area as wide as a study desk. Moreover, we lacked a good programmer to make a nice UI, and the code was unmaintainable. We also could not make use of solar energy, which we think would be abundant and accessible in mountainous areas. 

That being said, if I had all the time and resources in the world, I would go back and perfect the AIS. We saw a lot of potential in it, and it was clear that a lot of people saw the potential in the AIS, too, as we were able to win numerous awards in youth science fairs in Vietnam and in ASEAN countries. If, in the future, we were able to scale it to be more than just a small prototype, we will be able to benefit a lot of people, and I can't wait for that to happen.

column22:
While reporting for HanaTV, a Korean news organization in BC, I noticed we only published on YouTube. Our content got buried by the algorithm and the Korean community had no real way to search for our stories. So I decided to build a news website from scratch.


I'm using Next.js App Router for server-side rendering, Supabase for the database and auth, and Tailwind CSS for styling. The trickiest part has been the multilingual workflow. I set up bilingual routing so Korean and English content works seamlessly, and I'm using Supabase Row Level Security to keep everything secure.


The biggest moment was realizing dynamic routing could do the heavy lifting. Instead of making pages manually, I built one template that pulls from the database and generates SEO-friendly URLs for every story automatically. It basically turned a simple site into a news engine.


Right now I'm working on getting the bilingual system and auth fully working to make it production-ready.


https://hanatv.vercel.app/

One of the coolest projects I worked on was MediExplain, which we built during Hack Canada 2026. MediExplain is an AI-powered medical accessibility tool that helps patients understand complex medical information. Users can upload doctor notes, prescriptions, or lab results, and the app translates them into simple explanations and different languages, with both text and audio support.

The idea came from a real experience where a family member misinterpreted a prescription due to a language barrier. We wanted to create something that could reduce medical misunderstandings and make healthcare information more accessible.

During the hackathon, I collaborated with a team to build a working full-stack prototype using React, Node.js, and AI tools like Gemini. One of the biggest challenges was integrating the front end with the back end and coordinating development across a shared GitHub repository, but we were able to successfully deliver a functional prototype within the limited timeframe.

https://youtu.be/QnfCKmIoZN8
https://devpost.com/software/mediexplain

If I had all the time and resources in the world, I’d want to build an app where people can scan a flower and instantly find out what it is. When you scan it, the app would tell you the name of the flower, give some details about it, fun facts, where it usually grows, and how you can identify it in the future.

I’d also want it to have a kind of Pokédex-style system where every flower you scan gets added to your collection. You could track the flowers you’ve found in your region, your country, or even globally. I think that would make it more fun and motivate people to go outside and discover new plants (and touch grass :D ).

Another feature I’d add is a personal “garden” where all the flowers you’ve found are displayed. Instead of just showing the default image, users could also choose to draw the flower themselves as a small game, and their drawing would show up in their garden. I like the idea of mixing technology with creativity so it feels more personal.

The goal would be to make learning about plants and nature more interactive and fun, while also encouraging people to explore the outdoors.

column22: The coolest project I've built is EcoFit, a sustainable fashion platform that helps users find ethical alternatives to clothing items using image recognition. Users can upload a photo of a clothing item they like, and the platform recommends visually similar options from sustainable brands. To power this, our team built a Python backend with Flask and integrated a convolutional neural network to convert clothing images into feature embeddings. When a user uploads an image, the system generates its embedding and compares it with embeddings of products in a MongoDB database using cosine similarity to find the most visually similar sustainable items. We used Tailwind, HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, with react-three-fiber for 3D elements and Framer Motion for smooth animations and a nice frontend. The project was exciting because it combined AI, full-stack development, and sustainability into a practical tool that encourages more ethical shopping.
column23: If I had unlimited time and resources, I would build a large-scale machine learning platform for preventive healthcare. I would expand this idea into a system that analyzes long-term health records, wearable device data, and lifestyle information to detect early warning signs for different conditions. The goal would be to give clinicians and patients clearer insights so they can act earlier and make better decisions about prevention and treatment. A major challenge would be building infrastructure that can handle large healthcare datasets while protecting patient privacy and producing reliable predictions. This kind of platform would require strong machine learning models, scalable data systems, and dependable backend services. If built well, it could help shift healthcare toward earlier detection and better long-term outcomes.

column22:
One of the coolest projects I’ve built is PathSense, an indoor navigation and event discovery platform designed for large campuses. The idea came from a simple problem: new students often struggle to find classrooms, labs, or events inside big buildings where GPS doesn’t work well.

Our team built a system that allows users to navigate inside buildings using detailed indoor maps. We digitized campus layouts, georeferenced building structures, and integrated them into an interactive map interface so users can easily find rooms, hallways, and important locations. The platform also includes event discovery, allowing students to see what’s happening on campus and navigate directly to those events.

Technically, it was a really fun challenge. We worked with Mapbox for interactive mapping, React for the frontend, and cloud services for hosting and data management. A big part of the project involved converting real-world building layouts into usable map data and designing a system that could scale for multiple buildings.

What made PathSense especially cool was that it solved a real problem students face every day. Seeing people interact with something we built and realizing it could actually make navigating campus easier was incredibly rewarding. It was also one of the most collaborative and technically challenging projects I’ve worked on.
column23:
If I had unlimited time and resources, I would build an AR car intelligence system for smart glasses. The idea is that when you look at a car through AR glasses like Meta or similar devices, the system instantly recognizes the vehicle and overlays useful information about it in your field of view.

For example, if I look at a car parked on the street, the glasses could identify the make, model, and year, and show details like engine specifications, horsepower, reliability ratings, common issues, and the current market value of that car. If the license plate or VIN is visible, it could even pull up additional data such as accident history, maintenance records, or whether the car is listed for sale online.

This would be especially useful for car enthusiasts, buyers, mechanics, and dealerships. Imagine walking through a parking lot or a car meet and instantly learning everything about the vehicles around you without searching on your phone.

What excites me most about this idea is how it blends computer vision, augmented reality, and real-world data to make the physical world more informative and interactive turning every car you see into something you can instantly learn about.

How did I get this idea? I just bought a car two weeks ago, and I was pretty confused as it was my first car ever, so well, if this existed it would have eased up the process of getting information for me

column22:
At DeltaHacks, I built StreetSweep, a platform that crowdsources litter clean-up in Downtown Toronto. Combining publicly-available live traffic camera data with user-submitted images, we created a system that turns litter reports into clean-up tasks, empowering citizens to clean up the city and generating hundreds of data points that can inform local policy.

Technically, the main challenge was designing a reliable backend workflow connecting ticket creation, user claims, and cleanup verification. Tickets were scored by a computer vision model and displayed on a live dashboard and map. To ensure users actually cleaned up the area, I implemented a verification pipeline requiring post-cleanup images, then handled inconsistent CV outputs with multi-state resolution logic: fully cleaned areas closed tickets, while partially cleaned areas resolved tickets and generated follow-up tasks. This system balanced reliability, usability, and fair rewards. Through building this project, I gained hands-on experience in backend engineering, system design, and AI integration.

Devpost: https://devpost.com/software/streetsweep-ai
GitHub: https://github.com/werdna533/StreetSweep

column22:
The coolest project I’ve helped build is TransitLens. Born out of the universal frustration with the TTC, TransitLens was designed to bring transparency to Toronto’s transit chaos.
• The Problem: Most transit apps rely on "vibe-based" schedules that don't account for the "ghost buses" or the standard Saturday snags on lines like the 501 Queen.
• The Solution: TransitLens provides a high-fidelity look at the actual state of the city's movement. It’s built to give commuters the "ground truth" so they aren't stuck staring into a dark tunnel wondering if their ride even exists.
• The Result: It turned a shared urban headache into a functional, data-driven tool that empowers users to pivot their routes before they even leave the house.

https://devpost.com/software/transit-lens
column23:
The Vision
Imagine a system that doesn't just process data, but understands narrative weight.
• Deep Memory: It wouldn't just remember that you like coffee; it would remember that you like a specific roast because it reminds you of a trip you took in 2018.
• Intuitive Anticipation: Instead of waiting for a prompt, it could sense when you're hitting a creative block and suggest the exact obscure reference or piece of music that usually gets your gears turning.
• Cross-Modal Synthesis: It could take a rough sketch in your notebook, a melody you hummed in the shower, and a passing thought you had about a sci-fi novel, and synthesize them into a cohesive project plan.
Why It’s the "Dream Project"
The goal isn't just to make a "smarter" tool, but to bridge the gap between artificial intelligence and genuine partnership. It’s the difference between a GPS giving you directions and a co-pilot who knows all the scenic backroads you actually enjoy.

column23:
Given all the time and resources in the world, I would like to work on project that helps me (and maybe others) improve myself based on how I live my daily life. The description seems vague, but I have had this idea for a long time and it is grander than it sounds. There's two aspects to the process of building and using this project:

Firstly, I would need to track how I live my life, from my sleeping habits, to the focus cycles I use to complete work. Having all the resources in the world become important in this process because the software needs to integrate with a lot of different devices and wearables that I own at the same time in order to track all of those little details. On top of that, there is a major privacy and security concern with tracking all of this data, which leads me to require all the data (and the processing in the second portion) to be kept local. 

Secondly, there needs to be an analysis portion of the project that actually uses the data that is tracked and allows me to make real improvements to my life. For example, it might identify that a specific 20-minute shift in sleep onset, combined with a particular morning light exposure, leads to a 15% increase in afternoon cognitive endurance. The computation for this analysis would need to be local as well in order to handle the previously mentioned privacy concerns. A significant amount of hardware resources would be needed to make this happen. 

In an era where our data is constantly harvested by others to influence our choices, I want to reclaim that information for my own benefit. Knowing these details gives me the agency to understand my own biology and habits deeply, allowing me to be the primary architect of my own growth.

“Hello, my fellow Americans!”

Creating a talking Obama on wheels was nowhere close to what I had in mind when I applied to Hack the North this year. After getting rejected three times in a row, I was determined to build an amazing project and make my mark as a first-year UW student.

I’ve noticed that I’m always fixated at winning hackathons. Sure, I’ve taken home a few prizes, but this unhealthy mindset constantly resurfaces. With uni apps and now the co-op search, I feel like I’m always pushed to focus on getting more resume material. The grind never stops.

Obama’s tour around the UW campus enlightened me. Watching people’s faces brighten as he zoomed past, hearing them laugh at his jokes, seeing them being amazed at his feet sticking out the back… it reminded me of what made hackathons so special in the first place. The laughs, the interactions, and the small stuff that gets lost in the grind.

Inspired by Obama, I want to continue building cool things at BearHacks - whether that’s creating robust AI, tinkering with hardware, or just doing something completely random, I want to continue my goal of bringing more ambitious projects to life.
column22:
At ChessHacks, I decided to step outside of my comfort zone to explore machine learning. I was drawn to it by the thrill of learning new things and being able to apply this new knowledge to a game I enjoy.

My plan hit a roadblock immediately - building a chess engine was quite complex, especially for a first project. I struggled to understand the many moving parts, and it was hard not to feel discouraged when other hackers seemed to me making more progress with their bots.

With help from my partner, I took a different approach - researching theory first. It felt weird to be taking notes on a YouTube tutorial while everyone else was coding, but it helped me break down the complexity into digestible pieces (thanks Sebastian Lague!) I learned that understanding the concepts is often more important than coding all the time.

Another insight I took away from my research was that there was no limit in how complex a chess bot can be. What was actually important was prioritizing strategies that were effective yet simple enough to do within the time frame. After seeing the bot improve after implementing alpha-beta pruning, quiescence search, and transposition tables, I learned first-hand how research can pay off.

What makes 𝘰𝘯𝘦-𝘵𝘸𝘰-𝘳𝘦𝘥-𝘣𝘭𝘶𝘦 special to me is my effort to finally try something new, and also using a successful strategy to learn something new. But nothing can beat the feeling when my bot checkmated me for the first time, after trying to win and hoping to lose at the same time.
column23:
Picture this: a sprawling campus, nestled in the heart of a vibrant tech hub. Towering office buildings of tech giants neighbour cutting-edge research labs on quantum, AI, and more. An immersive AR experience seamlessly connects students together. 

This is my vision of a learning centre for the next generation of tech leaders.

I've modelled it after the ArtCenter College of Design in LA. Despite having a 75% acceptance rate, it's ranked very highly in terms of outputting great UI designers. Something about the environment helps give artists the skills they need to succeed in the web design space. Maybe it's the architecture, which reminds me of Apple Park.

So, a university, so to speak? Being in UWaterloo, I've experienced the cutthroat competition but also how the tuition fees can offset the value of the courses - which are often unrelated to jobs in the real-world.

Instead, I've taken inspiration for the courses from Shopify's DevDegree program, which puts more emphasis on hands-on experience and learning modern tech. The program allows for students to learn while working at Big Tech - which means zero tuition! But I would also allow students to research in labs and push the boundaries of knowledge.

And of course, they can also host and attend hackathons!

column22:
One of the coolest projects I’ve built is Moha Health, an AI-powered healthcare intake assistant that I developed during Hack Canada 2026.

Moha Health explores how AI can help streamline the first stage of a hospital visit. Instead of filling out static forms or waiting hours before speaking to a healthcare professional, patients can describe their symptoms through voice or text. The system uses a multi-agent AI architecture that simulates a hospital intake workflow: an AI “intake nurse” gathers symptoms, a routing agent determines whether a specialist is needed, and specialist agents ask targeted follow-up questions before generating a structured triage report.

The goal is not to replace doctors but to help patients reach the right care faster by organizing symptoms, identifying urgency, and producing a clear clinical-style summary that could assist medical professionals.

Technically, the project combines several modern AI and web technologies. The backend is built with FastAPI and Python, while the frontend uses React and TypeScript. It integrates Google Gemini for LLM reasoning, speech-to-text and text-to-speech for voice interaction, and video analysis to estimate vitals such as heart rate and respiration from a short face video.

Building Moha Health was exciting because it pushed me to design a coordinated multi-agent system, integrate multiple AI modalities (language, voice, and vision), and think carefully about reliability and safety in healthcare-related applications.

Links:
GitHub: https://github.com/Nafisatibrahim/moha_health

Live Demo: https://moha-health.replit.app/

column22:
One of the coolest projects I’ve built alongside my incredibly talented team is Money Hacks, a simulation-based financial literacy game we created during ElleHacks 2026. We ended up winning 2nd place in the Wealthsimple Challenge, and honestly, this project was a huge moment for me because it was my first (!!!) truly serious hackathon experience.

We built Money Hacks because we realized that a lot of teenagers turn 18 without understanding basic financial concepts like budgeting, credit, saving, or investing. And when people try to learn it online, it often feels boring or too complicated. Our idea was to make it feel like a game instead of a lesson. Players go through realistic life milestones like getting their first paycheck, managing spending, learning about credit cards, saving for goals, and exploring concepts like TFSAs. Every choice affects what happens next. We built the project using HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, and prototyped using Cursor.

The biggest challenge in my opinion was designing a game flow in such a short time (hackathon time pressure is real). We had to balance making it fun, while still keeping the content meaningful.

One of my favorite “aha” moments was when we decided to turn financial facts into unlockable achievements: instead of dumping information, we made tips and concepts something players unlock as they progress. 

Overall, Money Hacks was the project that showed me how EXCITING it is to build something useful in a hackathon with a team full of enthusiastic, smart, and ambitious people.

Devpost link: https://devpost.com/software/money-hacks
GitHub link: https://github.com/karina-vetlugina/moneyhacks-kanriya

column22: The project I am most proud of is my Heart Disease Prediction Engine. What drew me to it was the domain itself. Healthcare is one of those areas where machine learning stops being an academic exercise and becomes something that could genuinely affect people's lives. The idea that a model trained on patient data could flag risk factors a clinician might miss, or catch something earlier than a traditional screening would, is genuinely exciting to me. Building the pipeline from raw Kaggle data through cleaning, preprocessing, and model training gave me a real appreciation for how much work goes into making a prediction trustworthy rather than just technically functional. column23:
As a kid, like the rest of us, I had a lot of creative juices flowing and grandiose ambitions of bringing them to life. I was a huge fan of video games, particularly games like LittleBigPlanet, that really allowed you to bring your ideas to life. If I had all of the time and resources in the world, I would build a game where the player is not just a character in someone else's story, but the author of their own.

Most games give you the illusion of choice. You pick dialogue option A or B, and the story funnels back into the same path regardless. What I want to build is something closer to how a Dungeons and Dragons campaign actually feels, where the world is genuinely reacting to who you are and what you do, where no two playthroughs tell the same story, and where the narrative has real weight because you built it. Growing up, I had stories I wanted to tell and worlds I wanted to explore that no existing game could contain. That gap never really went away. Building something that finally closes it, not for any grand reason, just because it would be genuinely cool and deeply satisfying to play, is the project I keep coming back to.

column22:
I was competing in Hack the Valley hosted by the University of Toronto, one of Canada's largest hackathons, with a 36-hour deadline. My team was building 'EcoToken,' a platform that optimized prompts inputted into GenAI tools like ChatGPT to lower token usage (help the environment) and LLM hallucinations or glitches. Our design required a powerful, AI-driven backend, and we chose Snowflake as the core technology. The challenge was that none of us had ever used it. My responsibility was to learn it on the spot and build the entire backend, not just the database, but the API and the AI data processing. I leveraged the new Cortex AI playground in Snowflake through just documentation. I taught myself how to use it, and I successfully integrated it to perform AI analysis on our incoming data. This allowed me to create a SQL API that didn't just store data, but returned real-time results and automatically replaced the user’s bad prompt on the front-end within seconds.  In less than 36 hours, I had built a fully functional, AI-powered backend from scratch using a technology I'd never touched. We ended up winning the Snowflake category by Major League Hacking at the hackathon with over 500 participants, and it proved I can learn and make an advanced-feature solution under a very tight deadline. Also, I definitely know that I can trust and believe in myself and that I can do hard things because in the end I will always be learning.

column22:
Reroute is a Google Maps-style trip planner for the GTA that does something no major navigation app properly does, it combines driving and public transit into a single, intelligent route.

Most GTA commuters don't travel purely by car or purely by transit. The smartest route is often: drive to a GO station, take the train, then walk. Google Maps forces you to choose one mode. Reroute doesn't. It gives you three routing modes — Hybrid (best of both), Thrifty (cheapest), and Zen (least stress). It pulls real-time and static feeds from 5 GTA agencies: TTC, GO Transit, YRT, MiWay, and UP Express.

My contribution was the ML layer. I trained an XGBoost classifier on 4 years of TTC subway delay data cross-referenced with historical weather data, hitting 98% prediction accuracy by line, time of day, and season. That signal feeds directly into the Zen and Hybrid scoring, so routes are ranked by real predicted reliability, not just timetable estimates.

We built this at CTRL+HACK+DEL 2025, placed 2nd in the York Region track out of 60 teams, and we're currently pitching to YSpace, York University's startup incubator.
GitHub: https://github.com/910jmiguel/Reroute
column23:
If I had the time and money in the world, I would go all in on Reroute.

We built Reroute at a hackathon and we won second place, but let's be real, the idea is worth way more than a hackathon. The GTA has one of the most fragmented public transit systems in North America. Five different agencies, no unified experience, and every app still makes you choose between driving and transit like it's 2010. Most people end up using their cars because the alternative is way too complicated to figure out. It's a real problem.
 
The app that I want to build is the app that actually solves this. Not just the app that offers you the ability to use different types of transit, but the app that actually learns your lifestyle. It knows that you hate transfers after 10pm. It knows that the Yonge line is a disaster on Monday mornings. It knows that sometimes you want the cheapest option and sometimes you want the stress-free option. It knows that you're a busy person and you don't have the time to deal with this anymore.

column22:
One of the coolest projects I’ve built is a collision forecasting simulator in Python that predicts when two moving objects will intersect. The idea was to model motion mathematically and translate those equations into code that can detect collisions with high precision.

I designed the system using modular Python functions that calculate object positions over time and determine if their paths intersect. The program solves the motion equations numerically with a tolerance of 0.00001 to accurately forecast the time and location of a collision. Building this project helped me apply concepts from calculus, physics, and algorithm design while focusing on writing clean, testable code.

What I enjoyed most was seeing how mathematical models can be turned into practical software that predicts real-world behaviour. It also pushed me to think carefully about numerical precision, edge cases, and efficiency.

I’ve also worked on other projects, including
• A rule-based Insurance Premium Calculator that estimates risk-based pricing
• A custom mobile OS interface prototype in Figma designed around usability and user customization

I enjoy building projects that combine mathematics, data, and software, and I’m excited to keep experimenting with these ideas at hackathons.
column23:
If I had unlimited time and resources, I would want to build an AI-powered personal vision system that helps people improve everyday precision tasks through real-time visual guidance.

The idea originally came from something simple: shaving. Many people struggle to keep clean, symmetrical beard lines or hairline edges. My idea would be an app that uses computer vision to scan your face in real time, map facial landmarks, and project ideal symmetry lines directly onto the camera feed while you groom. Instead of guessing where to trim, you would see a guide that adapts to your unique facial structure.

With more resources, I would expand this concept far beyond grooming. The same technology could help people with tasks that require precision and confidence, like haircuts, makeup application, medical training simulations, or even physical therapy exercises where body alignment matters. The system could combine computer vision, machine learning, and augmented reality to provide instant visual feedback.

Technically, this would involve building a facial landmark detection model, training it on diverse datasets to handle different face shapes and lighting conditions, and creating a real-time inference pipeline that runs smoothly on mobile devices. I would also want to explore AR overlays so the guides feel natural rather than distracting.

What excites me most about this project is that it connects machine learning with something very human: confidence in everyday routines. AI often focuses on large abstract problems, but I think there is huge value in building tools that quietly improve small moments in people’s lives.

column22:
I made RouteSafe which is RouteSafe is a wildfire aware navigation system that calculates the safest route for drivers by considering predicted smoke exposure along the path.

Traditional navigation systems focus on optimizing travel time, distance, or traffic conditions. RouteSafe introduces a new factor into the routing process, which is smoke exposure risk. For every possible route, the system estimates how much smoke a driver might encounter and uses that information when choosing a path.

A key feature of RouteSafe is that it does not only look at current conditions. Smoke moves and spreads over time. Because of this, the system predicts what the smoke conditions will be when a driver actually reaches each part of the route.
