column22:
One of the coolest projects I’ve built is the Welland Canal Bridge Status Visualizer, a full-stack web application designed to solve a real problem in my community.

As a local commuter, I often got stuck at lift bridges that open for ship traffic along the Welland Canal, causing unpredictable delays. The official Seaway website provides text-based updates, but they aren’t map-based, mobile-friendly, or intuitive for tourists who don’t know road names. Over 1000+ commuters daily face these avoidable delays, and I wanted to build something practical people could rely on.

I created a real-time interactive map that shows every bridge with color-coded markers: green for open, red for closed, yellow for raising, and purple for lowering. The app refreshes every 20 seconds and uses browser geolocation so users can instantly see which bridges are closest to them and adjust their route.

The backend is built with Node.js and Express. It scrapes live bridge data from the official Seaway site using Cheerio every 20 seconds and caches it in memory to improve performance. The server exposes a REST API that the frontend consumes. The frontend uses React 18, Tailwind CSS, and the Google Maps API, following OOP and SOLID principles for maintainability. The project is deployed on Vercel.

A major challenge was handling inconsistent scraped data and preventing UI flicker when statuses changed quickly. Implementing structured parsing and smart caching was a key breakthrough that stabilized the system.

Today, over 1,000+ local residents use the app to avoid delays. I was also invited onto a local community podcast with 20,000+ viewers, where I shared how I built it and how developers can solve everyday problems with code.

GitHub: https://github.com/saqib-21/WellandCanalStatus
Website: https://www.bridgestatus.ca/

column22: LOCATR, our DeerHacks V winning project. It's a multi-agent AI location intelligence system built with Python, LangGraph, FastAPI, Next.js, Snowflake, and Gemini 2.5 Flash. We built 6 AI agents that work together: one parses natural language queries like "Cyberpunk study spot," another aggregates venue data from Google Places and Yelp, a Vibe Matcher analyses venue photos against a 52-dimension aesthetic vector space, and a Critic cross-references weather and event risk data to prevent bad recommendations. We cut pipeline latency from ~2 minutes to under 10 seconds using async parallel execution, integrated Auth0 for human-in-the-loop security, and streamed live agent reasoning logs via WebSockets. The whole stack runs in Docker. Devpost: devpost.com/minifiguresgt, https://devpost.com/software/pathfinder-6h8y1v
column23: I'd build a multi-agent AI system that actually solves the scheduling nightmare every overcommitted student faces. Not a calendar app. You dump in your class schedule, work shifts, gym goals, meal prep plans, errands, and how much sleep you need, and a team of specialized AI agents figures out your week. A Scheduler Agent blocks out non-negotiables. A Logistics Agent calculates realistic travel times between locations using geospatial APIs (so it knows you can't make it from UTM to downtown in 10 minutes). An Energy Agent models your productivity patterns and schedules deep work during peak focus hours and errands during low-energy windows. A Critic Agent catches impossible plans before you commit to them ("You have a 10-minute gap between your lecture and your shift, but the commute is 45 minutes"). The whole thing visualizes your week as animated daily routes on a live map. I juggle five concurrent roles right now (two internships, a club executive position, a webmaster role, and a campus programming role on top of a full course load), so this isn't hypothetical for me. I've already built multi-agent pipelines with LangGraph and FastAPI that won DeerHacks V, and I'd love to push that architecture into something I'd genuinely use every day.

column23:
If I had unlimited time and resources, one project I would love to work on is solving the P vs NP problem. This is one of the biggest open questions in computer science and mathematics, and solving it would fundamentally change how we understand computation.

The problem asks whether every problem whose solution can be verified quickly can also be solved quickly. If P = NP, it would mean many extremely difficult problems in areas like cryptography, optimization, and machine learning could potentially be solved efficiently. If P != NP, it would confirm that some problems are inherently hard to compute even though their solutions are easy to check.

I find this question fascinating because it sits at the intersection of algorithms, complexity theory, and mathematics, and the implications would impact everything from cybersecurity to scientific discovery. Even if solving it is incredibly difficult, working toward a deeper understanding of computational complexity would be an exciting lifelong project.

The coolest project I ever built was the Simon Says Color Game. I wanted to create a game as my first project so I decided to build simple one that uses flashing lights and sounds. It uses HTML, CSS, and JavaScript, so it's fully on browser. The most difficult obstacle was trying to figure out how to implement a system where it checks if the user matched the correct color order. To solve it, I used dictionaries and added the colours in an array, so whenever the user pressed a button, it will check if it match the current color. The one "aha" moment that got me through was trying to figure out when to implement this system. So I figured out to run it in the background as an asynchronous method.

column23:
Having all the time and resources in the world, I would want my project to be where you wear some sort of armor/clothing and it's connected to a mobile app. This app can customize how the armor should look like and then would be displayed as a 3D holographic or light based projection.

Also the system will have little item/device which can also turn into anything visually (like tools, weapons, toys) through the AR rendering. For example, the consumer can select or draw an item in the app that looks like a shield and it will appear on the little device. Also it will have motion detectors on it, to give more immersion experience to the consumer. 

But this does have some limitations, mainly the technology, and risks for safety. For the technology limitations, the tracking can be clunky in its few iterations, the hardware will be heavy depending on the size it comes with, and the visual effects looking a bit off from farther away. For the safety risks, the consumer (mainly the children), might run into things, lose awareness of their surroundings, and hurting themselves and others. 

So I suppose some ways to solve these issues are making 3 versions of the armor based on weight or size to give consumers more choices, the hardware and software limitations can only be solve iteratively, some safety measures like telling the consumer to take off the armor to give them a rest, or have the armor track how fast its going and telling the user to slow down to prevent them from getting hurt.

This is what I would want my project to be if I have all the time and resources (including people to work with I assume) in the world.

column22: MemoMuse, hands down. I built it in 4 hours at ListenHacks and it won Best Use of Featherless AI. It's a full-stack platform that takes a voice memo and turns it into a fully produced music track you can sell as vinyl through Shopify. The backend runs a 6-stage async pipeline with FastAPI: OpenAI Whisper transcribes your voice memo, Gemini 2.5 Flash analyzes the lyrics and mood, Featherless AI (Qwen2.5-7B) refines the lyrics, Google Lyria generates the instrumental at 48 kHz, and ElevenLabs handles vocal synthesis across 20+ voice models. I built parallel audio processing with pydub and FFmpeg for mixing, EQ, and pitch shifting, then wired up the Shopify Admin API so you can publish your track as a vinyl product with one click. The fact that it all came together in 4 hours and actually worked end-to-end is still wild to me.
column23:
I'd turn FluxRoute into a real product. Right now it's a hackathon prototype that compares multimodal transit routes across 5 GTA agencies with an XGBoost model trained on 10,000+ TTC delay records to predict delays by line, time, and season. But the GTA's transit system is genuinely broken for people who rely on connections between the TTC, GO, MiWay, YRT, and Brampton Transit. Nobody has built a routing tool that actually accounts for cross-agency delays and real-time congestion in a meaningful way.
With unlimited time and resources, I'd expand the delay prediction model to cover all 5 agencies (not just TTC), integrate real-time GTFS feeds for live vehicle positions, and build a mobile app with push notifications for predicted delays on your saved routes. I'd also want to partner with municipal open data teams to get richer historical data and eventually layer in accessibility routing for people with mobility needs. The vision is a transit app that doesn't just tell you the fastest route on paper, but the route most likely to actually get you there on time. Toronto needs this badly, and I want to be the one to build it.

Hackathons are one of the few environments where ideas can move from concept to working product in a matter of hours. I enjoy the intensity of that process, brainstorming a problem, designing a solution, and then building something tangible with a team under time pressure.

Recently at HackCanada, my team and I built MediExplain, a platform that translates complex medical language and medical images into plain-language explanations so patients can better understand their health. Building it in 48 hours showed me how powerful rapid prototyping can be when people from different backgrounds collaborate.

I want to participate in BearHacks because it brings together ambitious builders who care about solving real-world problems. My background sits at the intersection of computer science and healthcare, and hackathons are the perfect place for me to explore ideas that make complex systems more accessible, whether that’s through AI, better interfaces, or new tools that help people understand information that usually feels out of reach.

Most importantly, I come to hackathons to learn from other builders, experiment with new technologies, and push myself to build things I wouldn’t normally attempt in a traditional classroom environment. I also go to network and meet cool people!
column22:
One of the coolest projects I’ve built recently is MediExplain, which we developed during a 36-hour hackathon.

The idea came from a simple observation: medical reports, prescriptions, and test results are often filled with complex terminology that patients struggle to understand. MediExplain aims to bridge that gap by translating medical language and medical imagery into simple, plain-language explanations that anyone can understand.

The platform allows users to upload a medical document or image, and our system processes it using AI to generate a simplified explanation of the content. We also added optional audio explanations so users can listen instead of reading.

Technically, the project uses:

FastAPI (Python) for the backend API

React for the frontend interface

AI models to simplify medical terminology and descriptions

Cloudinary for image processing and handling uploads

ElevenLabs for text-to-speech audio explanations

The most rewarding part was seeing how quickly an idea could turn into a working prototype that could realistically help people understand their health better.

GitHub: https://github.com/zaourid1/MediExplain
column23:
If I had unlimited time and resources, I would build a platform that democratizes complex knowledge, especially in fields like healthcare, law, and finance.

Many systems that affect people’s lives are built around language and processes that are difficult for the average person to understand. This creates a huge information gap between experts and the public.

The project I would build is essentially an AI-powered “knowledge translator”, a system that can take complex expert information (medical reports, legal documents, research papers, financial contracts, etc.) and convert it into clear, understandable explanations tailored to the user.

For example:

A patient could upload a medical report and receive a personalized explanation of their diagnosis and next steps.

A tenant could upload a lease agreement and understand what each clause means.

A student could upload a research paper and get an interactive explanation of the key ideas.

The goal would be to create a system that makes expert knowledge accessible to everyone, regardless of their technical background.

Ultimately, I believe technology should reduce information inequality, and this type of platform could empower people to make better decisions about their health, finances, and lives. I've had difficulties in school in the past trying to learn "complex ideas" and it was not until I was able to break it down that I felt like I actually was able to learn properly. I also believe it would help solve the inequality of people who may not have access to many resources.

column22:
The coolest project I’ve built so far has to be TriageID. It was my first project integrating software and hardware to build a real solution to a problem that affects people every day. 

TriageID is a hackathon-winning secure emergency information system designed for chronic patients. The idea is simple: when someone is in a medical emergency, the first few seconds matter, but doctors and EMTs often have little to no information about the patient. With TriageID, each patient wears a bracelet that stores their medical data. In an emergency, EMTs can tap the bracelet on our Arduino-powered scanner to instantly retrieve critical information through our app, such as medications, allergies, and key risk indicators. 

Building TriageID pushed me to work across both hardware and software, from integrating NFC scanning with an Arduino to building the system that retrieves and presents the patient’s medical data. It was also the first time I worked on a project where the goal wasn’t just to build something cool, but to design something that could realistically help people in critical moments.

Devpost: https://devpost.com/software/triageid
Github: https://github.com/Megaman222111/TriageID

The coolest project I’ve built was for the “Hack the Track” hackathon, and it’s called GuidoPro, an AI-powered race strategy analytics platform designed to help racing teams make smarter pit stop decisions using data. The system analyzes real lap time and telemetry data from seven tracks in the Toyota GR Cup Series to recommend optimal pit windows that minimize total race time. It models tire degradation, traffic conditions, and probabilistic caution scenarios to evaluate multiple strategy options and determine the most effective one. I also implemented telemetry analytics that can detect anomalies such as engine performance drops or braking issues in real time. Building GuidoPro challenged me to design optimization algorithms, process high-frequency telemetry data, and translate complex race strategy logic into an interactive analytics tool. It was especially exciting because it combined my interests in software engineering, data analysis, and motorsports strategy.

GitHub: https://github.com/cthanges/GuidoPro
Devpost: https://devpost.com/software/guidopro
column23: If I had all the time and resources in the world, I would love to build my own fully autonomous battlebot from the ground up. As someone passionate about embedded systems and robotics, the idea of designing and engineering an entire robot myself is incredibly exciting. I would start by selecting and integrating all of the core electronic components, such as microcontrollers, sensors, motor drivers, and communication modules. From there, I would design custom PCBs using tools I’m familiar with, such as Altium Designer, and assemble the hardware myself while improving my soldering and hardware prototyping skills. On the software side, I would program the microcontrollers to control the robot’s movement, sensing, and decision-making. I would also experiment with autonomous navigation and combat strategies, using sensors and computer vision techniques, such as OpenCV, to allow the robot to detect opponents and react in real time. Overall, this project allows me to apply the skills I already have while continuously learning new ones.

column23:
If I had unlimited time and resources, I would build an AI powered operational copilot for mission driven organizations. Many nonprofits, public sector teams, and small institutions still rely on fragmented spreadsheets, manual reporting, and disconnected systems to run critical programs. I would design a platform that integrates data pipelines, knowledge retrieval, and agent based AI to help these teams automatically track operations, generate reports, surface insights, and make better decisions in real time.

The system would combine structured data infrastructure with AI reasoning. Data from case management systems, forms, and operational tools would flow into a unified backend, where AI agents could query the data, generate reports, flag anomalies, and suggest next actions. Instead of spending hours preparing grant reports or manually analyzing program outcomes, teams could ask natural language questions like “Which programs had the highest impact this quarter?” or “What factors are contributing to increased service demand?”

What excites me most about this project is the intersection of technical depth and real world impact. Building reliable AI systems that integrate with production data pipelines, APIs, and human workflows is a challenging engineering problem. At the same time, the outcome could meaningfully improve how organizations allocate resources and serve their communities. My goal would be to create infrastructure that empowers small teams to operate with the same data and intelligence capabilities that large organizations have today.

The idea actually came from sitting in lectures myself and noticing something that happens all the time: a teacher explains something, a bunch of students get confused, but nobody says anything. The lecture keeps going, the topic moves on, and then everyone realizes they didn’t really understand it when they start studying for the exam.

column22:
The coolest project I've built is a Smart Irrigation Scheduler, a web application designed to tackle a critical, real-world problem: water scarcity for farmers in agricultural regions like Rajasthan.

Being from a tier 3 desert city, I wanted to build something that could directly help local farming communities conserve resources. The scheduler is a platform that optimizes water usage, allowing users to manage irrigation cycles efficiently so crops get exactly what they need while minimizing waste.

I built the application using Next.js 16, React, TypeScript, and Tailwind CSS, and deployed the live version via Vercel. Working on this was incredibly rewarding because it pushed me to architect a clean, responsive UI for non-technical users while solidifying my front-end and full-stack development skills. It’s my favorite project because it bridges the gap between modern web technologies and a vital sustainability challenge.
column23:
If I had unlimited resources, I would build a globally scalable, AI-driven smart irrigation platform to eradicate water waste and optimize crop yields for farmers in developing nations.

Currently, millions of farmers rely on intuition and unpredictable weather, leading to severe groundwater depletion and crop failure. Existing precision agriculture technology is simply too expensive and inaccessible for them.

My project would bridge this gap by deploying networks of low-cost IoT soil sensors that feed real-time moisture and temperature data into a predictive AI model. Combined with satellite imagery and weather forecasts, the system would generate hyper-localized, automated irrigation schedules.

The core technical challenge would be building a massively scalable backend to process this data, paired with an accessible frontend—built with React and Next.js—that delivers insights via simple SMS or voice prompts for farmers without smartphones. Ultimately, this project would democratize agricultural technology, helping farmers conserve vital water resources and build a more sustainable global food supply.

column22: My coolest build is my Smart Calendar Assistant, an NLP-powered productivity tool that reduced calendar event creation time by 88%, from 3 minutes to 15 seconds. I built it end-to-end using Java Spring Boot on the backend, React on the frontend, and the Gemini LLM API to process 500+ natural language queries. I implemented OAuth 2.0 for secure token management, optimized API response times by 40% through intelligent request batching, and achieved 95% test coverage via comprehensive JUnit suites covering edge cases and concurrent request handling. This was not a tutorial project. Every architectural decision was mine, and every bug was mine to fix.
column23:
I grew up watching cities struggle with problems that felt inevitable but never really were. Housing shortages, crumbling infrastructure, communities displaced before anyone sounded an alarm. Nobody predicted them early enough to act. That bothers me.

If I had unlimited time and resources, I would build an AI system that sees urban crises before they happen. Not a dashboard. Not a report. A living, breathing model that ingests zoning permits, transit patterns, demographic shifts, and construction timelines and tells city planners exactly where the next bottleneck is forming, months before it does.

I am not pulling this out of thin air. I already built a disease prediction engine that achieved 78% accuracy across 4,900 patient records by mapping symptoms through a custom probability graph using Dijkstra's algorithm. That project taught me something important: chaos has patterns, and patterns have solutions. I just want to apply that same thinking to something bigger than one hospital dataset.

Cities are the hardest engineering problem humanity has ever tried to solve. I want to be one of the people who actually tries.
column28: One idea that I think would make BearHacks truly unforgettable is a "real client" track where actual local startups or nonprofits submit real unsolved problems before the hackathon, and teams compete to build working solutions for them. Not fictional prompts, real pain points with real stakeholders in the room. Winning teams get their solution piloted by the organization after the event, and if the chemistry is right, the organization gets first right of refusal to bring that team on board, whether as co-op students, contractors, or full hires. That single change transforms a hackathon from a weekend build sprint into a launchpad for actual careers and actual impact, and gives every participant a story worth telling in every interview for the next two years. The best hackathons do not just produce projects. They produce futures.

I love reading papers. Reproducing great papers is one of the fastest ways to grow as an engineer, but it’s hard to start from a blank repo and messy PDFs. I am always a bit lost when I attempt to reproduce one. I also love LeetCode. I love the way it lays out a task for me to do with functions already defined. All I need to think about is just implementing the solution. And so I decided to build a platform where I could just upload papers and implement them LeetCode style. I built everything on Next.js and deployed on Vercel. The backend lives in API routes with Neon Postgres for state, Vercel Blob for PDF storage, QStash for background processing, and Vercel Sandbox for running submissions. Gemini 3 powers the code scaffold generation, which is rendered on a Monaco IDE in the browser, with the uploaded paper to the left of the screen. It is honestly such a huge unlock for me. I'm having lots of fun with it. 

Here is the Devpost link: https://devpost.com/software/mfon_repro

column22:
One of the coolest projects I’ve built is StudyBuddy, a platform designed to help university students find and share study spaces more easily.

The idea came from a real problem at my university. Libraries and study spaces fill up quickly, and students often waste time walking around campus trying to find somewhere to work. I wanted to build something that made it easier for students to discover available study spots and connect with others who are studying similar subjects.

StudyBuddy allows students to browse and share study spaces, discover where other students are studying, and find environments that fit their needs. I designed and built the platform using React, Tailwind CSS, and Firebase, focusing on a clean and simple interface so students can quickly find what they need without friction.

One of the most interesting parts of the project was building it as a real product rather than just a class assignment. I deployed it publicly, added authentication and a small donation feature, and started sharing it within student communities to see how people interacted with it. Seeing real users explore something I built from scratch was incredibly motivating and taught me a lot about product design, user experience, and deployment.

You can check it out here:
Live app: https://studybuddyapp.ca
Portfolio: https://arifshaikh.work
LinkedIn Post: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/arif-shaikh-40695730a_studybuddy-is-officially-live-just-in-ugcPost-7394086482610843648-vGSc
column23:
If I had unlimited time and resources, I would want to build a software platform that helps retail employees and managers actually understand and improve sales performance in a meaningful way.

I currently work in retail sales, and one thing I’ve noticed is that a lot of performance in sales environments depends on numbers that most employees barely see. Metrics like attach rate, conversion rate, product knowledge, and customer engagement are extremely important, but the tools used to track them are often outdated, fragmented, or only visible to managers through internal systems and spreadsheets. Employees who actually want to improve don’t have a clear way to measure themselves, and managers don’t always have simple tools to coach their teams based on real data.

The project I would want to build is a SaaS platform that connects to point-of-sale systems and creates clear, real-time analytics dashboards for both employees and managers. Employees could see their own sales metrics, trends, and improvement areas in a simple mobile interface, while managers could see team performance, identify coaching opportunities, and better understand what products and behaviors actually drive sales.

I would also want the platform to focus on development and growth rather than just monitoring. For example, it could provide personalized recommendations, highlight opportunities to improve attach rates, and show how top performers approach sales conversations. Over time the platform could incorporate training modules, product knowledge tools, and predictive analytics that help stores understand what actually drives revenue.

One of the coolest projects I’m currently building is called Ultimate Trip Planner, which is a full-stack web application that recommends travel destinations in Canada based on a user’s interests, budget, and number of travel days.

For the backend, I used Python with FastAPI to build the API and handle the application logic. I also created a small data pipeline to clean and process travel datasets and stored the structured data in a PostgreSQL database running in Docker. On top of that, I implemented a recommendation system using a TF-IDF based machine learning model that analyzes attraction descriptions and matches them with user interests to rank relevant destinations.

For the frontend, I built a simple interface using HTML, CSS, and Bootstrap, where users can input their preferences and generate a personalized day-by-day travel itinerary.

What I enjoy most about this project is that it combines data engineering, backend development, and AI into one system, and it helped me understand how different components like databases, APIs, and machine learning models work together in a real application.

column22:
One of the coolest projects I’ve built is a small game written in C with a Python interface for controlling gameplay. I called this game Dungeon Runner.

The core engine is implemented in C and handles the underlying game logic: managing rooms, tracking the player’s position, validating movement, and handling interactions like collecting treasures. Because it runs in C, it’s efficient and closer to the system level, which made it a great exercise in memory management, structs, and designing a stable C API.

On top of that, I built Python bindings using ctypes so that the engine can be controlled from Python as if it were a normal Python module. The Python layer wraps the C functions into classes like GameEngine and Player, converts error codes from the C library into Python exceptions, and exposes enums for directions and game states. This lets a Python developer interact with the engine using simple method calls while the heavy logic runs in compiled C.

The project helped me understand how systems programming and higher-level languages can work together, and it gave me experience debugging issues across the boundary between C and Python, as well as things like segmentation faults, pointer handling, and translating error states into meaningful exceptions.

One of the projects I enjoyed building was PassGuard, a password strength checker written in Python.

The goal of the project was to better understand how password policies work and how basic security checks can be implemented programmatically. The script evaluates a password based on multiple factors such as length, the presence of uppercase and lowercase letters, numbers, and special characters. Based on these checks, it categorizes the password as Weak, Okay, or Strong and provides feedback to help the user improve it.

While the project itself is simple, it helped me think more about how password security works in practice and how even basic validation can help prevent weak credentials. It also helped reinforce programming fundamentals such as conditional logic, input validation, and designing user feedback.

column22: The coolest project I've built is MoodNest, a Generative AI smart home concept that I developed alongside three teammates during the 24-hour CxC Hackathon in February 2026. The idea was to automate environmental controls by transforming voice recordings into structured sentiment data. I built out the Python backend, orchestrating a sequential data pipeline. It takes a recorded audio file of the user's voice, converts it to text, and passes it to the Gemini API for sentiment classification. We then used a RESTful API built with FastAPI to serve that mood data to our frontend. The result was a 3D model house that dynamically adapted its music and lighting based on the user's detected emotion, while also triggering a spoken response via the ElevenLabs API. Piecing together an end-to-end audio processing workflow in just 24 hours was intense, but seeing a physical environment shift based on a voice recording was incredibly rewarding.

column22:
The coolest project I’ve built is Foosgoos, a hybrid full-stack platform designed to be an AI-powered referee and stat tracker for foosball. This project was a collaborative intern effort, born from our company culture where the foosball table is the heart of the office. It was created to solve the common frustrations of competitive play, disputes over shot legality, uncertainty about which bar last touched the ball, or confusion over how a ball flew off the table. I wanted to build a hands-free solution where a machine learning model handles the officiating, allowing players to focus entirely on the game.

To make this possible, I engineered a local inference engine using YOLOv8 and OpenCV that tracks ball velocity and player positions at 100 FPS via a wired USB camera. This setup distinguishes complex game events like saves, goals, and possession with near zero latency using edge computing. I also developed a state machine logic engine specifically to enforce technical rules such as the two bar touch requirement to settle mid game arguments instantly.

Beyond officiating, foosgoos is a hub that stores all our game results so users can see who is on top of the leaderboard, what types of goals they are scoring, and which positions they perform best in. It even helps players identify their best teammates by analyzing win rates and chemistry. It has the ability to capture and store snapshots of incredible shots so the users can save and email to themselves, while effectively creating a highlight reel of every match. By combining high velocity object detection with a great frontend the interns and I turned our company's favorite pastime into a fun interactive arena.

GitHub: https://github.com/Csermakd/foosgoos
column23:
If I had all the time and resources in the world, I would develop an autonomous, spatial temporal vision AI bot system designed for high stakes search and rescue in burning buildings. This system would function as both an active assistant and a real time alert platform for first responders. By integrating Gemini 3’s multimodal reasoning with elevenlabs voice synthesis, the bot would provide clear audio guidance and status updates even in chaotic environments.

The core of the project involves x-ray vision achieved through a combination of thermal imaging, millimeter wave radar, and Lidar to penetrate thick smoke and see through walls. By overlaying a building’s digital blueprints onto the real-time sensor data, the AI can pinpoint the location of trapped individuals and map out the safest routes to reach them. This physical AI would be housed in fire hardened hardware capable of withstanding extreme temperatures, effectively acting as a guardian for those who risk their lives to save others.

The system would utilize spatial temporal modeling to predict structural collapses and fire spread before they occur. It would analyze heat signatures and stress patterns on load bearing elements in real time, alerting rescuers to evacuate seconds before a floor gives way. It would have predictive analytics and real time vision creating a life saving tool that turns a disaster and panic zone into a navigable environment.

I used an Arduino Uno as the controller along with a motor driver board, Bluetooth module, water pump, and DC Motors to design a robot that could move and spray water for cleaning. Throughout the project, I went through the full engineering process, such as researching components, wiring and assembling the hardware, testing control code as well as different designs to improve the robot's functionality. While the robot wasn't perfect at cleaning in the end, the experience itself taught me a lot about troubleshooting hardware, integrating multiple systems at once, and staying persistent at times when things didn't work the first time. More importantly, this project sparked my passion for engineering. It showed me how exciting it is to take an idea and turn it into a real physical system. Overall, this experience motivated me to choose Computer Engineering as a major, and continue pursuing more complex projects in the future.

column22:
The coolest project I've built is my CHIP-8 emulator, written in C++ and compiled to WebAssembly so it runs directly in the browser. Growing up, I spent a lot of time on emulators like Drastic for the DS and Mupen64, so being able to build one myself felt like closing a loop I didn't know was open. I implemented the full CPU core from scratch, including the fetch-decode-execute cycle, memory layout, sprite rendering with XOR collision detection, timers, and a 16-key input system. I then used Emscripten to compile the same C++ codebase to WebAssembly, and wrapped it in a retro terminal-style browser UI with ROM selection, custom ROM uploads, and a color picker that communicates with the Wasm module through a JS bridge. I also wrote a full test suite using GoogleTest covering nearly every opcode. The project taught me a lot about low-level systems, cross-platform compilation, and the boundary between native and web environments.
Live demo: https://chip8-emulator-seven.vercel.app/
GitHub: https://github.com/melchior729/chip8-emulator

If I had unlimited time and resources, I would build a system that identifies inefficiencies in how the real world is designed and engineered, and then proposes and automates better approaches using software.

The core idea is this: a lot of broken things in the world, whether that's traffic systems, public transit routing, hospital workflows, or supply chains, are broken in ways that data can actually reveal. I want to study real-world datasets, find where things consistently bog down or fail, develop a theoretical explanation for why, and then engineer a concrete fix. The traffic system is one example: given road usage data, where are the bottlenecks, why do they form, and what does an optimized routing or infrastructure change actually look like? Then, can that solution be automated or deployed through software?

What excites me most is the full pipeline, from the theoretical computer science of modeling the problem, to the software engineering of building something that acts on it.

I don't yet know which domain matters most to me, and I think that's part of why I want to be in environments like BearHacks. I want to be around experienced people who have already found the broken thing they care about fixing, because I think exposure to that is how I'll find mine."

A collaborative JavaFX-based paint application developed through iterative sprints, implementing professional software engineering practices including design patterns, code reviews, and comprehensive documentation.
Individual Contributions
Sprint 1: Foundation & Learning
US1.004: Draw Rectangle Feedback (4 hours)
Sprint 2: Core Features
US2.001: Draw Square (2 hours)
Sprint 3: Tool Customization
US3.007: Line Thickness Selector (3 hours)
Sprint 4: Advanced Features & Refinement
US4.014: Copy/Paste Functionality (5 hours)
US4.015: Stickers (3 hours)
US4.016: Polyline Drawing (4 hours)
BUG3.008: Icon Display Fix (30 minutes)
BUG4.010: Remove instanceof (1 hour)
Total Contribution: 8 User Stories, 2 Bug Fixes, ~35 hours

Architecture & Design Patterns
Design Patterns Implemented
1. Model-View-Controller (MVC)
2. Strategy Pattern (Sprint 2 Refactoring)
3. Visitor Pattern (Sprint 3 Refactoring)
4. Command Pattern
5. Observer Pattern

Additional Features Delivered (Team Effort)

Clear Canvas functionality
Background color customization
Textbox with font size control
Eraser tool with adjustable thickness
Glow effect for visual enhancement
Undo/Redo operations
Gradient fill capability
Zoom In/Out functionality
Cut operation

Development Process
Scrum Methodology

Sprint Planning: Estimated user stories, prioritized backlog, assigned ownership
Daily Scrums: Documented progress, blockers, and plans
Sprint Reviews: Demonstrated working features
Retrospectives: Identified improvements for subsequent sprints
Product Backlog Management: Maintained prioritized list of 40+ items
Pull Request Reviews: Peer code review before master merge
column23:
An AI-powered app that turns creative stories into playable, branching-path games. Users submit a raw story idea, snippet, or outline, and the app uses Generative AI (like LLMs) to improve the narrative, identify potential branching points, and generate a playable adventure. It bridges the gap between passive creative writing and interactive game development, making game creation accessible to writers, students, and beginners.
The core functionalities would be: 
Story Input & Refinement: Users input text (short stories, scenes) and the AI refines the prose for pacing and engagement.
Narrative-to-Game Parser: The system analyzes the text to identify key decision points, plot twists, and character choices.
Branching Structure Builder: Automatically generates a "choose your own adventure" node structure based on the story.
Asset/Image Generator: Generates corresponding visual assets (backgrounds/character portraits) for scenes using tools like Stable Diffusion.
Multi-Platform Output: Exports the final project as a playable web-based game (HTML) or a shareable link.

column22:
My most favourite project is TSTorch, a TypeScript-native deep learning runtime I’m building with a collaborator.

The goal is to run and inspect neural networks outside of the usual Python server environment, specifically in the browser and edge environments via WebGPU. I started the project because I found that many real issues in machine learning systems are difficult to diagnose when the framework hides the execution model. I wanted to understand what actually happens between a model definition and the numerical operations executed on hardware.

Technically, the project reconstructs core pieces of a deep learning framework from first principles:
-reverse-mode automatic differentiation
- dynamic computation graph construction
- gradient propagation across arbitrary operations
- tensor abstractions layered on a scalar engine
- execution compatible with GPU backends

This required implementing autograd tapes, graph traversal, and tensor execution in a runtime (TypeScript + browser) not designed for scientific computing, which exposed many practical concerns normally handled in C++ backends: numerical stability, memory usage, and execution ordering.

The value of this project for me was not just re-implementing PyTorch-like behaviour, but gaining the ability to reason about failure modes. Building the differentiation engine myself made it much easier to understand why models diverge, why gradients vanish or explode, and why performance bottlenecks appear when moving from small experiments to real deployments.

Because of this, I now approach ML systems as end-to-end systems rather than just models. That mindset is what I want to bring to applied work: diagnosing issues in real environments and making models reliable rather than only accurate.

column22:
https://www.cardiovue.ca/

One of my most coolest projects was a startup I founded. Our goal was to create an AI model which could annotate ECG graphs for doctors. The initial plan was to create a model which could run on device. A person who has a heart condition could wear our device which would monitor a patient's heart in real time and then alert them on an app when anything happens. We managed to make a prototype device, a strap which could be worn aroud the chest. That took about a year of development, starting as an Arduino Uno in a box, to a more compact prototype PCB the size of a credit card, to a more slim 2 pcb design with a hinge, to finally a custom printed pcb the size of half a palm which could fit perfectly in our form factor. During this time the electrodes also underwent a lot of changes starting as premade medical sticky electrodes with loose wires to custom made little plates that were comfortably placed around the strap. The AI was the other large problem. The model started off as a relatively big 1D CNN. From there, taking inspiration from SSM language models, I developed a model using an SSM attention mechanism which allowed the model to inference like an RNN with constant memory consumption rather than quadratic like with Transformers. This required a lot of research and a custom implementation but at the end it ran on device blazingly fast. During the span of the development we also developed our own proprietary training techniques to work around limitations in the data available in the field.

column22:
One of the coolest projects I’ve built is Healix, a self-healing Linux system designed to automatically detect and recover from system failures. The idea came from managing servers for cybersecurity labs and CTF environments where downtime during events can disrupt hundreds of participants.

Healix monitors logs, system metrics, and service states in real time. When it detects anomalies—such as crashed services, failed updates, or abnormal log patterns—it can automatically trigger recovery actions like restarting services, repairing configurations, or rolling back changes. The system uses a modular architecture so new detection rules and remediation actions can be added easily. I also designed a CLI interface that allows administrators to inspect system health and review automated decisions.

What makes this project interesting to me is that it combines systems engineering, automation, and security operations. Instead of waiting for a human operator to fix problems, the system can respond immediately, which is especially useful for infrastructure running security competitions or lab environments.

The project taught me a lot about log analysis, system reliability, and building tools that operate safely in production environments. It also pushed me to think about how automation and AI can help system administrators focus on higher-level tasks rather than repetitive troubleshooting.
column23:
If I had unlimited time and resources, I would build an open, self-healing cyber defense platform designed to automatically detect, analyze, and respond to cyber attacks in real time. The goal would be to combine AI-assisted analysis with automated infrastructure to help organizations respond to threats faster than human teams alone could.

The system would continuously monitor logs, network traffic, and system behavior across large environments. When suspicious activity is detected, it could automatically investigate the incident, isolate compromised systems, deploy patches, and generate detailed reports for security teams. Instead of simply alerting administrators, the platform would actively participate in defending the infrastructure.

I’m particularly interested in making this platform accessible for education and smaller organizations. Many advanced security tools are expensive or difficult to deploy, which creates a gap between large enterprises and smaller teams. An open platform could allow universities, security clubs, and researchers to simulate real-world attacks and defenses in safe environments.

Ultimately, I’d want the project to function as both a defensive security system and a training platform, where students and professionals can practice responding to realistic cyber incidents while the system demonstrates how automated defense strategies work. It would combine my interests in cybersecurity, automation, and building resilient systems.

One of the coolest projects I've built is an Excel-based Daily Sales Report system for my workplace. This was a project that acts as both a way for me to contribute to my workplace through my technical skills, while also providing a solution to a real business problem we were facing.

The report is designed to track key KPIs such as sales performance, and compare current results against historic data. This provides clear insight into trends over time and allows us to gain a deeper understanding into how certain business decisions have affected the health of the business. I worked on organizing messy sales data into a structure that I could then use as the backend for a dashboard. This dashboard then takes the structured data, and calculates it into actionable insights represented through visual reporting tools to make it easier for my boss to understand. Examples of results shown on the dashboard include year-over-year comparisons, performance patterns, as well as a tagging feature to categorize pieces of data, thereby removing noise from the analysis. This DSR was developed to automatically update the dashboard as the day-to-day sections of the DSR are filled in, allowing for easy access to important decision metrics.

This project allowed me to combine both my technical skills with my knowledge gained through working at this position for many years. It required problem-solving, attention to detail, an understanding of how the data should be represented within the context of this business, and communication of the KPI's to support decision making. It also made me realize I especially enjoy building tools that help people understand complex information more clearly.
column23:
If I had unlimited time and resources, I would build a fully featured version of the project I proposed in my thesis, a machine learning platform designed to measure and simulate financial well-being across populations to support better economic policy decisions.

The system would use random forest models to measure financial well-being using income and consumption data across Canadian demographics. My motivation is both personal and practical: growing up in a lower-income environment shaped how I understand economic hardship, and it drives my interest in building tools that help policymakers respond more quickly and efficiently to real financial stress in society.

With unlimited resources, I would expand this into a full decision support platform that goes beyond measurement into simulation. The system would integrate historical datasets including income levels, consumption patterns, regional cost of living, employment structures, and household demographics to model how proposed economic policies may affect different groups. Rather than evaluating outcomes at a general level, the platform would highlight how impacts vary across regions, income brackets, industries, and households.

The platform would prioritize interpretability and transparency so economists can clearly understand how results are produced and which variables drive outcomes. Ultimately, I want to build a tool that helps decision-makers better understand trade-offs, respond faster to economic shifts, and support policies that improve people’s ability to meet basic needs like food and housing.

column22:
One of the most interesting projects I’ve been working on recently is a travel concierge app designed to simplify how people explore new cities.

When people travel, they often have to switch between multiple apps to plan their day; searching for restaurants, checking maps, looking for events, and figuring out how to get between locations. The idea behind this project was to combine those steps into a single intelligent system that can generate a personalized itinerary for the user.

The application allows users to input their location (for example their hotel or current position), their preferred mode of transportation, and how many places they want to visit in a day. Using that information, the system recommends nearby attractions, restaurants, and events, and then generates an optimized route that allows the user to visit everything efficiently.

The project integrates mapping APIs, location-based data, and AI-powered recommendations to create a more dynamic travel planning experience. Instead of giving users a static list of suggestions, the goal is to act more like a digital tour guide that helps them plan their day based on their interests and constraints.
Working on this project has been a great opportunity to explore API integration, UI design, and how AI can be used to create more personalized user experiences.
column23:
If I had unlimited time and resources, I would build a platform that helps people navigate education and career paths using real world data. I actually have started this project at UoftHacks13, where the aim was to bridge the gap between professional and personal identity.

Choosing a career or field of study is often based on incomplete information. Students typically rely on anecdotal advice or general guidance, but they rarely have access to data that shows how people with similar backgrounds actually progress through their careers.

The platform I imagine would analyze large scale datasets such as job postings, skill requirements, career trajectories, and educational outcomes. Users could input their current skills, interests, and goals, and the system would generate possible career paths along with insights about the skills they would need to develop to reach those goals. 
Beyond just suggesting paths, the platform could recommend projects, learning resources, internships, or mentorship opportunities that help users move toward those roles. Over time, it could adapt as the user develops new skills or changes interests.

The long-term goal would be to make career exploration more transparent and data-driven, giving people a clearer understanding of what opportunities exist and how they can realistically reach them.

column23:
One thing I truly believe is that AI is one of the best learning tools that humans have ever invented, if used correctly. Naturally, this draws me towards the potential of human growth through learning with AI. 

A lot of learning tools exist now that can provide answers instantly, but most don't really understand when the user is stuck and why. If I had unlimited time and resources, I'd want to build a system that could accurately adapt to how individuals think. For example, if a student is learning about programming, math, or sciences, the platform would avoid just providing an answer right away. It would figure out whether the issue is missing prerequisite knowledge, misconceptions, or just not seeing the intuition behind the problem. Then, after identifying the root issue, it could provide a visual representation of the problem, a simpler sub-problem, a simulation, or tiny hints that avoid giving away the solution. 

My current limitations are the need for a thorough understanding of students' study patterns and how to develop an AI capable of the same. To train a custom AI specifically for this purpose would probably require a huge dataset from real people and building a custom model.

column21: Most would say "creating something meaningful" or "working with like-minded peers," and while I do want to build something meaningful in an environment filled with innovative and creative peers, I'm more excited to break things first. Before you terminate my application, I'd like to say that every useful and robust project I've made started with me breaking the standards of what's "possible" with my skillset, tearing apart my original product, and putting it back more optimized, user-friendly, and impactful to it's users. They say you're only as good as you believe you are, but I always believe that I can be better. I’ve spent the last year working on real projects, not tutorial-level stuff. At my auto company internship, I wrote authentication routes that completely fell apart when we added role-based access. It forced me to rethink how I structured the whole API and rewrite it in a different format. At my full-stack internship, I shipped features to over a thousand monthly users, but only after fixing a bunch of bugs caused by badly structured queries and slow endpoints as well as optimizing the website for devices of all kinds, from Iphones, Laptops, to even Google NestHub Displays. Even my trading dashboard started off as a mess. My SMA indicators drifted because I wasn’t handling inconsistent data pulls from Yahoo Finance. I had to rebuild the pipeline before anything made sense. Thus I'm interested in taking fragile projects apart, exposing every flaw, and turning them into reliable, unexpected, working solutions.
column22: Index funds, ETF's, the Magificent 7, they all have one thing in common: being insanely hard to monitor and analyze manually. It's a pain having to update spreadsheets of data and copy-paste formulas, and perform calculations daily to quantitatively calculate the risks of entering a trade at a specific time. That's why I decided to build my own Trading Dashboard and Bot using matplotlib, backend API's, and Streamlit to solve this inconvenience. I didn’t want a static graph or chart, so I built an interactive Streamlit dashboard that lets me switch between timeframes, SMA indicators, and different sectors. I made sure to connect API's such as NewsAPI and YahooFinance so that my indicators and data are based on real-time data and gives me accurate representation of the stock market at all times. The second piece is the Dashboard Bot. I've never had prior experience creating bots before, so this was quite a confusing process for me. I started with simple SMA20 and SMA50 signals because they were easy to test. The bigger problems came from the data. Sometimes the API would return slightly old values, or the requests would come in at strange intervals, and the bot would generate signals based on incomplete information. It took a while to figure out why things were firing at the wrong times. What made this project cool wasn’t the tech stack. It was how many times I broke it, rebuilt it, and slowly learned what actually matters: "Progress came from fixing the mess."

column22:
The coolest project I've built is Mr. Beep-A-Lot, an autonomous robot with personality.

THE PROBLEM:
Five days before my robotics presentation, I wanted to build something that felt alive - a robot people would connect with, not just admire.

THE SOLUTION:
Mr. Beep-A-Lot has three modes (obstacle avoidance, radar scanning, human following) with a personality-driven LCD. Boot-up: "MA LOOAARD!" Following: "POOKIE HOOMAN!!" Lost: "Hooman? Where?" Undefined buttons: "I'M A MINOR!" (yes, i know, questionable)

THE TECH:
Dual ultrasonic sensors, servo radar with Processing visualization, I2C LCD, IR remote, sensor fusion for autonomous navigation.

THE OBSTACLES:
Everything broke in ways documentation never mentioned. Fried an Arduino wiring GND to VCC (learned about short circuits the hard way). Servos jittering - traced it to power supply noise, soldered capacitors onto power rails at 2 AM. Figured out parallel vs series circuits when motors weren't getting enough voltage. Learned what transistors actually DO switching higher current loads.

The unglamorous part nobody talks about? The wiring. Cutting wires to exact lengths so they fit point A to point B without exposed copper. Cable managing everything on a tiny breadboard, making sure nothing shorts. I was on a tight budget with limited wire colors, so I developed my own color-coding system and meticulously documented which wire went where. One exposed wire at 2 AM could fry everything I'd built.

THE 'AHA' MOMENT:
Good robotics isn't about perfect code - it's about building systems that work under pressure with the resources you have. I documented nothing until it worked, then created full schematics so others could use what I built.

Built with Arduino, C++, Processing, one sacrificed Arduino, perfectly cut wires, 3 hours of sleep per night, and way too much hot glue.

GitHub: https://github.com/Shayan-Mazahir/Mr.Beep-A-Lot
column23:
If I had unlimited time and resources, I would build ATOM (from the movie Real Steel) a full humanoid robot that mirrors human movement in real time, just like in Real Steel.
The idea starts simple: a camera captures your movements, processes them through a pose estimation pipeline, and translates them into servo commands that make a physical robot mirror you instantly. But that's just phase one.
The real goal is to get rid of the camera entirely and go EMG, reading the electrical signals from your muscles directly. Before your finger even fully moves, your muscle has already fired. A robot reading that signal wouldn't just mirror you, it would almost predict you.
What excites me isn't just the robot itself, it's what it represents. The same technology that makes ATOM shadow a boxer is what gives someone who lost their arm the ability to pick up a cup again. Prosthetics, assistive devices, human augmentation; it all lives in the same problem space.
I want to build it phase by phase. Vision based first, then IMU sensors, then EMG. Each phase teaches me what the next one needs. I don't want to just build something cool; I want to understand every layer of it deeply enough to eventually make it real.
And eventually (haven't thought how exactly this works) I want to integrate the Vision system IN the robot so it is exactly like ATOM, a big dream but something I really want to work on if i had the chance or even if i didn't i would

column22:
One of the most interesting projects I worked on was during a hackathon where my team built a remotely controlled car using a Raspberry Pi with live video streaming. The goal was to create a small vehicle that could be controlled through a web interface while transmitting real-time video from an onboard camera.

We used a Raspberry Pi connected to a camera module to capture video and stream it over a local network. The vehicle’s motors were controlled through a motor driver connected to the Pi’s GPIO pins, which allowed us to control movement through commands sent from a web dashboard. This required integrating several pieces of technology including Python for the backend control logic, networking for the video stream, and hardware control through the GPIO interface.

One of the biggest challenges was reducing latency in the video stream while still maintaining reliable control of the vehicle. We spent a lot of time experimenting with different streaming methods and optimizing the communication between the interface and the hardware. Even small delays made the vehicle difficult to control, so tuning that system was a big part of the project.

What made the project exciting was how quickly we were able to turn an idea into something physical and interactive. By the end of the event, we had a working prototype that could be controlled remotely while providing a live camera feed, which opened up possibilities for applications like remote inspection, exploration, or robotics experimentation.
column23:
If I had unlimited time and resources, I would work on developing autonomous outdoor service robots that handle tasks people regularly do manually, like snow removal and lawn maintenance. We already have indoor robots like Roombas that vacuum floors, but outdoor environments are much more complex and less predictable. Building a system that can reliably operate outside would be a challenging and interesting engineering problem.

The idea would be to design an autonomous robot that can automatically clear snow from driveways or mow lawns without human supervision. Unlike indoor robots, it would need to handle uneven terrain, obstacles, changing weather conditions, and large open spaces. The robot would likely use a combination of computer vision, LiDAR, and GPS to map its environment and plan safe paths while avoiding obstacles like cars, trees, or people.

A major focus of the project would be making the system reliable in harsh conditions. For example, a snow-clearing robot would need to operate in low visibility, freezing temperatures, and slippery surfaces. Designing hardware and software that can still navigate accurately and safely in those conditions would require solving problems in sensing, navigation, and real-time decision making.

Projects like this interest me because they combine robotics, AI, and real-world problem solving. Autonomous outdoor machines could save people time and reduce the physical effort required for everyday tasks. If systems like this became practical and affordable, they could eventually become as common for outdoor work as robotic vacuums are inside homes today.

column22:
The Problem:
YouTube is arguably the world's most powerful free educational tool, yet it is designed to keep you watching entertainment. We faced a constant struggle. We would log on to watch a coding tutorial but inevitably fall into a rabbit hole of irrelevant shorts and entertainment videos. We wanted a way to leverage YouTube's educational library while stripping away the noise that kills productivity.

The Solution:
Clarigo, Co-developed with Reese Chong, Clarigo is a Chrome extension that transforms YouTube into a dedicated learning platform. It works by analyzing your feed in real time. The extension scrapes video titles and channel names as they load, passes them through our classifier, and dynamically removes any non-educational content from the DOM. This leaves the user with a curated, distraction-free interface where only tutorials, lectures, and educational content remain.

Under the Hood We built the core logic using Scikit-learn to train a Logistic Regression model on a dataset of approximately 3,000 videos scraped from our own recommended pages. The challenge was bridging the gap between our Python-based machine learning model and the JavaScript environment of a Chrome extension.

The 'Aha' Moment Our breakthrough came when we realized we didn't need a heavy backend to run the model. We wrote a custom exporter that converts our trained Logistic Regression weights and intercepts directly into a JSON format. This allowed us to reconstruct the mathematical model entirely in client-side JavaScript. This "Aha" moment meant that after the initial page load, the extension runs smoothly and classifies videos instantly without server latency.

Current Status:
We are currently achieving 87% accuracy on live feeds. While we are looking to further refine the model, Clarigo effectively filters out the noise, allowing students to finally use YouTube strictly for its benefits.

Links:
https://github.com/r-chong/Clarigo

column22:
One of the coolest projects I’ve worked on was a smart soil monitoring system for urban gardening. The goal was to make plant care more data-driven and accessible, especially for people who don’t really have experience with plants.

I built a custom sensor setup that tracks soil moisture, temperature, and light, and sends the data to a simple dashboard. Instead of relying on fixed schedules, the system gives watering recommendations based on real conditions, which makes it feel a lot more practical. A big challenge was dealing with hardware constraints like power usage and noisy sensor data, while still keeping the frontend clean and easy to use.

What I liked most about this project is that it wasn’t just technical for the sake of it. It actually felt useful. It pushed me to think more about real-world usability and design, not just building something complex.

I’d want to focus on bridging the gap between hardware and usability, building tools that people who aren’t technical can still benefit from. column22:
Just this past weekend, at GenAI Genesis 2026, my team and I built an AI-powered resource navigator, that aids people in crisis in finding their way to stability. It comprises of 2 main features: 

1) Resource finding. This feature takes the user's location, and using a regularly updated repository of services (done via periodic web-scraping), it displays resources near the user in various categories (food, shelter, etc.) and also has a chatbot that will direct the user to specific services based on their current situation.

2) Stability roadmap. This feature allows the user to explain their current situation, and builds a step-by-step plan for the user to reach stability. It gives specific instructions for what to do during the current week, the next month, and in the longer term. It leverages Langchain with Gemini and Agentic AI to plan this out, first taking in the user's input and converting it into a profile, which is fed to a ReAct agent that has access to Brave's search interface, using it to research and create the plan

It was my first time at a hackathon, and it really was an exploration of possibilities and truly testing the limits of my beliefs.

column22: I built nginx-defender, a lightweight log monitoring tool written in Go that detects and automatically blocks IP addresses showing signs of abuse such as brute force attacks, excessive request rates, and suspicious traffic patterns. instead of relying on any external service (like cloudflare), it integrates directly with the server firewall through iptables or ufw and runs as a systemd service, making it fully self hosted and production-deployable. It ships with a WebSocket powered web dashboard for live threat visibility, an ML-based anomaly detection layer, and Prometheus metrics for observability. It has picked up over 70 stars on GitHub entirely organically, which told me that the problem it solves is real and that other developers actually find it useful. The project also taught me the importance of responsible disclosure after I identified and patched a default credentials vulnerability in the configuration files in version 1.5.0. GitHub: https://github.com/anipaleja/nginx-defender

column22: One of the most meaningful projects I’ve built is VYBE, an iOS accessibility application that translates live audio into real-time haptic feedback. The idea came from thinking about how much of our digital and physical environments rely on sound, whether it’s music, alerts, or ambient cues, and how inaccessible that can be for people with hearing impairments. Instead of trying to “visualize” sound, we wanted to make it something you could physically feel. Technically, the biggest challenge was building a low-latency pipeline that could process live microphone input and convert it into meaningful haptic patterns in real time. I used AVFoundation to capture and analyze audio signals and Core Haptics to generate dynamic vibration patterns. A key design decision was mapping amplitude and frequency characteristics to distinct haptic intensities and rhythms, so users could differentiate between types of sounds rather than just feel generic vibration. One of the hardest parts was tuning responsiveness. Even small delays made the experience feel disconnected, so I spent a significant amount of time optimizing the signal processing and reducing latency to make the feedback feel immediate and intuitive. We ended up with a working prototype that allowed users to “feel” music and environmental sounds in real time, and the project placed 2nd at Hackville 2026. What made it stand out to me wasn’t just the technical execution, but the fact that it reframed accessibility as something experiential rather than compensatory.
column23: If I had unlimited time and resources, I would build a multisensory accessibility platform that translates real-world environments into adaptive feedback systems tailored to different sensory needs. The idea is to go beyond single-purpose tools and create a system that can interpret sound, speech, and environmental context in real time, then convert that information into haptics, visuals, or spatial cues depending on the user. For example, a wearable or mobile system could detect important sounds like alarms, traffic, or someone calling your name, and translate them into distinct physical or visual signals. Technically, this would involve combining real-time audio processing, on-device machine learning for sound classification, and personalized feedback mapping. It would also require thoughtful hardware design, potentially integrating with wearables to provide more nuanced haptic output than a phone alone. What makes this project compelling to me is that accessibility is often treated as an afterthought, added onto existing systems. I’d want to build something designed from the ground up with accessibility as the primary goal, shaped by continuous input from the communities it’s meant to serve. Ultimately, I see it as a step toward making everyday environments more inclusive, not by removing complexity, but by translating it into forms that more people can engage with.

column22:
Last fall I was applying to co-ops and doing what everyone does: rewriting the same resume over and over for each posting, swapping keywords, adjusting the summary. Tedious work, and I kept wondering why I couldn't just automate it.
So I built Just Apply. It pulls your GitHub and LinkedIn data through their APIs, takes a job description, and uses Gemini to generate a tailored resume that actually reflects what you've done, not a generic template, but one that maps your real projects and experience to what the posting is asking for. Then it exports to PDF and can send the application by email automatically.
Most people send the same resume everywhere, and one that actually speaks to the specific role reads differently to a recruiter which is what Just Apply helped me with.
column23:

BearLink would start with the obv stuff: infinite memory. Never forget why you walked into a room. Retain exactly what you studied instead of blanking mid-exam. We already have this memory to our phones anyway, this is just cutting out the middle guy.

BearLink would capture that as something you can like actually walk through. Wake up, load your dream like a game level, explore it. The neural signal interpretation alone would be an insane problem to work on, and we don't even fully understand what we'd be capturing yet.
That's the project. Infinite memory and reconstructable dreams are the reasons I'd actually want to build it.

column22:
I built an AML fraud detection system using graph data.

Accounts are nodes, transactions are edges. I trained a model to assign a risk score to each account using patterns like transaction flow and behavior. The model over-flags on purpose.

Then I added three analysis agents that review flagged accounts from different perspectives and generate explanations.

On the frontend, you can click a node, see all related transactions, and get a clear explanation of why it was flagged.

The focus was not just detecting fraud, but making the result understandable.

I integrated Veo 3 for AI video generation and Nano Banana for visual ad creatives optimized for Facebook Ads and Shopify. The results surprised me. column23: If I had unlimited time and resources, I would build a universal prediction engine that can forecast virtually anything with high accuracy. The core idea is a system that continuously ingests data from every available source, financial markets, weather patterns, social media signals, scientific datasets, geopolitical events, and runs billions of simulations across every possible scenario in parallel to surface the most likely outcomes. Drawing on my experience building a stock market predictor with Random Forest models and working with RAG pipelines and Gemini AI, I know how powerful even small-scale predictive systems can be. But the real vision is something far bigger: a general-purpose forecasting layer that researchers, doctors, urban planners, and policymakers can query in plain language and get back probabilistic predictions grounded in real-world data. The goal is not to replace human judgment but to give it an unprecedented foundation to work from.

One of the coolest projects I’ve worked on is my capstone project called the **TechFusion SmartCart**, a dual-mode autonomous robotic transport system.

The project is a small-scale prototype of an automation system that can be used in environments like retail stores and warehouses. The idea was to design a robot that can both be manually controlled and operate autonomously, which makes it flexible for testing, debugging, and real-world use.

The SmartCart uses a differential drive system with two motors and infrared sensors to follow a predefined path. In autonomous mode, it detects a line on the ground and adjusts its movement in real time to stay on track. In manual mode, it can be controlled through a custom graphical user interface (GUI) over WiFi, allowing a user to move it forward, backward, left, right, or stop instantly.

One of the most interesting parts of the project was integrating hardware and software together. I worked on programming the control logic in Python, designing the GUI, and making sure the motors respond accurately to both sensor input and user commands. We also implemented safety features like an emergency stop system to ensure safe operation.

From a performance perspective, the robot can move at speeds up to about 0.5 m/s and carry small loads of around 2 kg, which makes it a realistic prototype for lightweight transport tasks. This makes it similar in concept to automated carts used in large stores like Walmart, but built using low-cost components.

What makes this project stand out is that it combines multiple areas of engineering—embedded systems, control systems, and mechanical design—into one complete working system. It also has real-world relevance, since the same concept can be scaled up for industrial automation.

Overall, this project gave me hands-on experience in building a full system from scratch and showed me how different engineering components come together to solve real problems.
column23:
If I had all the time and resources in the world, I would want to develop a **fully autonomous smart logistics system for retail and warehouses**, expanding on the idea from my capstone project.

Instead of a single robot like the SmartCart, the system would consist of multiple autonomous robots working together in a coordinated network. These robots would be capable of navigating large store environments, dynamically avoiding obstacles, and transporting goods between storage areas and shelves without human intervention.

I would integrate advanced technologies such as computer vision, machine learning, and real-time mapping (SLAM) so the robots wouldn’t rely on fixed paths like line-following. They would be able to understand their surroundings, detect people, and make intelligent decisions in real time.

Another key feature would be a centralized control system that monitors all robots, optimizes routes, and manages inventory movement efficiently. This could significantly reduce manual labor in places like Walmart or large distribution centers while improving speed and accuracy.

I would also focus on scalability and safety—designing the system so it can handle heavier loads, operate continuously, and safely interact with humans in busy environments.

This project interests me because it combines robotics, AI, and real-world problem solving. It would be a natural evolution of my current work, taking a simple prototype and turning it into a complete industrial automation solution.
