column22:
One of the coolest projects I’ve built is ScamShield, a real-time AI-powered assistant that helps users detect and understand online scams before they fall for them.

The idea came from seeing how scams often target vulnerable groups like seniors, immigrants, and students. Most fraud systems only act after a transaction has already happened, which is too late. We wanted to build something that helps users in the moment while they’re reading a message, clicking a link, or even on a call.

We built ScamShield using Next.js for a full-stack web app, with IBM watsonx.ai powering the AI layer (and flexibility to switch to Gemini or OpenAI). We used Supabase for user data and feedback, face-api.js for secure in-browser Face ID, and the Browser Speech API for live call transcription. A key feature we added was behavioural context, where the AI considers a user’s typical online activity to make more personalized and accurate decisions.

ScamShield analyzes messages and URLs in real time and returns a risk level, confidence score, and clear reasons for why something might be a scam. It detects phishing links, impersonation, and urgency tactics, and explains everything in simple language with recommended next steps. One of the most distinctive features is live call scam detection, in which the system transcribes an active call and flags suspicious language or pressure tactics in real time. Users can also give feedback to improve future predictions.

We built a working prototype focused on real-time prevention and accessibility, acting like a personal fraud guide. It has strong potential to scale into browser tools or financial platforms.

Devpost + demo: https://devpost.com/software/scamshield-ai-personal-fraud-guardian
column23:
Growing up in a first-generation immigrant family, I witnessed how capable women struggled to continue their professional careers after moving to Canada. Many arrive with degrees and experience, yet face barriers because their experience does not translate cleanly into a new system. Differences between non-Western and Western career systems often misalign credentials, job titles, and hiring norms, leaving many qualified women without professional pathways.

With unlimited resources, I would build an AI-powered career development platform to address this gap by translating existing experience into actionable career pathways within the Canadian job market.

Users would upload resumes, degrees, and job histories in their preferred language. Large language models would parse and contextualize this information, interpreting credentials and mapping past roles to Canadian skill equivalents while accounting for cultural and structural differences. A recommender system would analyze anonymized data from women with similar backgrounds who successfully re-entered the workforce to suggest realistic career paths. Instead of listing jobs, the system would explain how others navigated accreditation, bridging programs, or entry-level transitions.

A skill gap analysis model would identify missing requirements between current skills and target roles and recommend learning steps or certifications. The platform would also include a matching system to connect users with mentors and community groups, helping rebuild professional networks lost through migration.

Bias monitoring would be integrated to prevent users from being funnelled into lower-paying roles, with explainable recommendations and data privacy controls. Over time, the system could expand to mobile and messaging-based access, improving accessibility and confidence in career decision-making.

column22:
In Canada, approximately one-sixth of children struggle with reading, with 18% falling below standard literacy benchmarks. For a child, this means sentences feel overwhelming, leading to disengagement and frustration. 

At RythmHacks, my team and I built ReadBuddy, an eye-tracking reading tutor that monitors where a child is looking on the screen as they read an e-book. ReadBuddy uses live gaze tracking to detect when a student repeatedly looks at and pauses on the same word, equating to difficulty. Once this occurrence is detected, ReadBuddy responds by pronouncing the word out loud, displaying its spelling, and displaying a short definition, providing the student with supportive feedback without breaking their flow.

This project is built with a Next.js frontend and a FastAPI backend in Python, using a custom OpenCV pipeline. Our pipeline processes live webcam frames, crops them to a 4:3 ratio, converts them to grayscale, and then identifies the pupil by finding the darkest region of the eye. Next, an ellipse is placed around the pupil, and its position is mapped to screen coordinates to estimate gaze direction. ReadBuddy then aligns this gaze direction with text on the screen, precisely identifying which words a reader is looking at, and which words they pause on or look at the most (more pauses = more difficulty). Our elegant process provides children with pronunciation guides and simple definitions when a student is experiencing difficulty with a word.

One big challenge ReadBuddy faced was latency from our live video processing. Originally, the team considered using heavy ML models, but we achieved a major breakthrough by using a lightweight pupil-detection algorithm with a heuristic, as described above. Our algorithm allowed us to map which word a user is currently looking at on a screen without requiring a powerful GPU.

ReadBuddy placed 3rd Overall at RythmHacks!

column23:
General-purpose AI models receive billions of queries daily, consuming loads of energy. Simple, repetitive tasks currently use almost the same amount of energy as problems that require complex reasoning, a major inefficiency that makes mass AI deployment both environmentally and economically expensive. Currently, to “solve” this problem, model compression and distillation are used to reduce the total energy used. However, a key issue is that these techniques must be manually designed, rather than automatically adapting to real usage.

If I had unlimited resources and time, I would build an AI ecosystem where frontier models serve as teachers, automatically distilling smaller, but specialized models when many similar queries from large groups of users are found. For example, a math tutoring model trained from a larger LLM could run at a fraction of the parameters and energy cost, while still providing the same output to users. 

My system architecture would consist of four main components. First, a monitoring and clustering layer would embed and group incoming queries, detecting when many users want similar tasks to be completed. Once a cluster is marked as high-frequency, a frontier model would act as a teacher, generating supervisory outputs for that task. Then, an automated distillation pipeline would train a specialized student model aligned to the found high-frequency query distribution. Finally, a routing layer would redirect future related queries to the specialized model. 

Since inference cost scales approximately linearly with model size, specializing related tasks into tiny models would decrease total compute demand by billions of GPU-hours annually.

column22:
The coolest project I’ve built is Polymolt, a prediction market powered by AI agents. I created it during the GenAI Genesis hackathon, and even though we didn’t win, it was one of the most exciting projects I’ve worked on because it explored something completely new. I’ve always been driven to build ideas that don’t already exist, and Polymolt was exactly that, it combines prediction markets with multi-agent AI systems. The core idea is based on how prediction markets tend to converge toward accurate outcomes over time. Instead of relying on real people, Polymolt simulates a market using multiple AI agents acting as independent participants. Each agent forms its own opinion using retrieval-augmented generation (RAG), pulling in real-world data like news and statistics to support its predictions. As these agents “interact” in the market, their collective behavior converges toward an informed answer. This allows users not only to see a predicted outcome, but also to understand the reasoning behind it through cited sources. Overall, it shifted how I think about AI, from single responses to systems of interacting agents, and showed me how combining existing ideas in new ways can lead to something genuinely innovative.
https://github.com/derek750/Polymolt
https://polymolt.vercel.app
column23: If I had unlimited time and resources, I would tackle the growing challenge of AI-generated misinformation and fraud, especially through highly realistic synthetic media such as deepfake videos and audio. As generative models continue to improve, it’s becoming increasingly difficult to distinguish real content from AI-created content, which poses serious risks to public trust, personal safety, and digital security. My project would focus on building robust, large-scale detection systems capable of identifying AI-generated content across multiple modalities, video, audio, and images. These systems would use state-of-the-art models trained to detect subtle artifacts left behind by generative AI, continuously updated through adversarial training so that detection keeps pace with evolving generation techniques. Beyond detection, I would invest in content provenance and authentication tools, such as cryptographic watermarking and digital signatures embedded at the time of media creation, allowing platforms and users to verify whether content comes from a trusted source. I would also collaborate with social media platforms, governments, and journalists to deploy these tools at scale and promote media literacy. The ultimate goal would be to preserve trust in digital media while enabling AI to be used creatively and responsibly, ensuring that its benefits are accessible without amplifying misinformation or fraud.

One of the coolest projects I’ve worked on was a small data analysis tool that looks at patterns in people’s daily habits and mood. The idea was to explore whether simple lifestyle factors like sleep, screen time, and study hours had any visible relationship with how someone feels day-to-day.

I built it using Python with pandas and matplotlib. I started with a simple dataset that I created myself by tracking my own habits for a few weeks, and then expanded it slightly with sample data to test patterns. The program cleans the data, calculates basic correlations, and generates visualizations like trend lines and heatmaps to show relationships between variables.

What made it interesting for me was seeing how small changes, like inconsistent sleep, actually showed up in the data. It wasn’t anything super advanced, but it helped me understand how data can be used to reflect behavior in a really tangible way.

It was also my first time taking something from a rough idea to a working script that produces outputs you can actually interpret, which made it a really rewarding experience.
column23:
If I had unlimited time and resources, I would want to build a system that helps understand and predict human behavior in a way that is actually useful in real life. I would focus on health decisions, like why people don’t follow treatments, ignore symptoms, or struggle to stay consistent even when they know what they should be doing.

The idea would be to combine different types of data such as biological signals, daily habits, environment, and psychological patterns into one model. Instead of just tracking what people do, it would try to understand why they do it. A lot of current systems treat behavior as something secondary, but I think it plays a huge role in real outcomes, especially in healthcare.

I would want the system to find patterns at both the individual and larger population level. For example, instead of just predicting that someone might miss medication, it would try to explain the underlying reasons, like stress, routine changes, or social factors, and suggest solutions that actually fit their life.

What interests me most is how messy and unpredictable human behavior is. People don’t act like clean datasets, and I think many models fail because they ignore that. So the goal would not be to be perfectly accurate, but to better capture that complexity in a way that is still useful.

This kind of project would need collaboration across different fields like data science, biology, psychology, and design, which is something I really want to explore more.

column22:
The coolest project I’ve built is PawPrint, a web app that turns a few shelter photos into a complete adoption campaign in minutes.

We built it around a real problem—animal shelters rely heavily on online visibility, but volunteers usually only have time to take quick phone photos in poor conditions and don’t have time to edit images or create posts for every pet. That directly impacts adoption rates, so we focused on automating that entire workflow.

With PawPrint, a volunteer uploads photos and enters basic pet details, and the app handles everything else. It automatically selects the best “hero” image using Cloudinary’s quality analysis, enhances images, generates platform-specific formats for social media, and creates AI-generated captions using Gemini. It also produces a shareable pet profile, downloadable social media packs, and a QR-coded kennel card that links directly to the pet’s page.

Technically, it was a really interesting build. We used React and TypeScript for the frontend and Vercel serverless functions for the backend. One of the most unique decisions was using Cloudinary as both our media storage and database by storing structured metadata on each asset and querying it through their Search API. This let us avoid a traditional database while still supporting full functionality. We also built a full media transformation pipeline and handled ZIP generation client-side.

The biggest challenge was dealing with inconsistent image quality and making sure enhancements actually improved photos without making them look unnatural, while keeping the workflow fast and simple.

What I like most about PawPrint is that it solves a real problem and could realistically be used—it turns a time-consuming process into something that takes just a few minutes.

GitHub: https://github.com/Jarank-git/HackCanada2026
Demo Video: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=D3_WjKy2eTc
Deployment URL: https://pawprint-app-mocha.vercel.app/
column23:
If I had unlimited time and resources, I’d want to build a real-time “physical world operating system”—a platform that connects sensors, environments, and software to continuously understand and optimize real-world spaces.

A lot of my projects point in this direction already. With my environmental room regulator, I worked on using sensors to monitor conditions and automatically adjust environments. With Arctic Analytics, I built systems that collect and stream real-time data from hardware into usable dashboards. What I’m interested in is taking that idea much further—scaling from a single room or robot to entire buildings, cities, or systems.

The core idea would be a unified platform where physical spaces are constantly producing data (temperature, movement, energy usage, noise, air quality, etc.), and that data is processed in real time to make intelligent decisions. For example, hospitals could automatically adjust environments to improve patient recovery, shelters could optimize conditions for animals, and cities could dynamically respond to congestion or energy demand.

But the key isn’t just collecting data—it’s making it actually usable. I’d want to build clean interfaces and APIs so developers can easily build on top of it, similar to how we build on cloud platforms today. Think of it like AWS, but for the physical world.

Technically, this would combine embedded systems, distributed systems, real-time data pipelines, and intuitive frontends. It would also require solving challenges around reliability, scalability, and making hardware-software integration seamless.

What makes this exciting to me is that it’s not just another app—it’s infrastructure. It’s something that could quietly improve how real-world systems operate at scale, which is the kind of impact I want to work toward.

I want to participate in BearHacks because it’s one of the few environments where I can focus entirely on building something end-to-end with a team under real time constraints. Most of my experience so far has been in structured projects, and I’m interested in pushing that into a faster, more iterative setting where decisions actually matter and things break.
I’ve built end-to-end systems before—like integrating LLM pipelines using FastAPI and LangChain with a Next.js frontend, and working with real-time data through Firebase in a team setting—and I want to apply that experience in a setting where I’m adapting quickly and building alongside others.
I’m also looking for a strong team where I can contribute technically while learning how others approach problems and structure systems. For me, BearHacks is less about just participating and more about building something solid with people who take it seriously.
column22:
The coolest project I’ve built is Smart Schedule Maker, an AI-powered system that takes real calendar data and generates feasible daily plans.

What made it interesting wasn’t just using an LLM—it was figuring out how to make its output actually reliable. I parsed .ics calendar files as input and used Gemini through LangChain, but instead of letting the model freely generate schedules, I designed a pipeline where deterministic logic filters valid time blocks first, and then the LLM fills in structured plans within those constraints. That cut down unnecessary calls and avoided a lot of unrealistic outputs.

On the backend, I built FastAPI endpoints to validate everything the model produced before applying it, including checking for time conflicts and basic feasibility like commute gaps. I also enforced strict schemas with Pydantic so the system only accepts structured JSON outputs. On the frontend, I built a Next.js interface where users can preview and approve changes before anything is committed.

The most interesting part was treating the LLM as just one component in a larger system rather than the core decision-maker. Once I approached it that way, the system became much more stable and predictable, and it felt closer to building a real product instead of just a demo.
repo: https://github.com/HosseinOfive/Smart-Schedule-maker
column23:
If I had unlimited time and resources, I’d want to build a system that acts like a real-time decision assistant—something that integrates directly with a person’s context, like what they’re seeing, hearing, and feeling, and helps them respond in situations where they might hesitate or go blank.

The goal wouldn’t be to replace decision-making, but to support it by suggesting what to say or do in the moment based on context. That would mean combining continuous input from wearables or environmental sensors with models that understand intent and social context, while handling real-time data in an event-driven way similar to what I’ve worked with in syncing user activity in mobile apps.

What interests me most is making something like that actually reliable. Instead of just predicting possibilities, the system would need strong constraints and feedback loops so it doesn’t suggest unrealistic or inappropriate actions. In a way, it’s similar to the kind of AI you see in Person of Interest, but grounded in systems that have to deal with uncertainty and real-world consequences.

It’s a big idea, but it builds on what I’ve already worked on—treating AI as part of a larger system rather than something that makes decisions on its own.

Not only would it be interesting to analyze the common "patterns" found in generated content, but would also be incredibly useful for individuals who are less familiar with modern technology, and are the prime targets of scam and fraud using AI.

The main issue with this type of program would be false positive reports, which could cause significant harm. Meaning the accuracy of the program should be paramount. It would also need to keep up with the rapid development of new models, sort of like an "arms race". All this being said, I think this would be a profoundly interesting project, but one that I do not yet have the experience to implement effectively.

column22: My personal favourite is Atlas Queue it's a distributed, crash-safe job processing system built from scratch in Go, backed by PostgreSQL and Redis. No external message broker. I designed the full job lifecycle state machine, a lease-based fencing protocol to guarantee zero duplicate execution under worker crashes and network partitions, and a gRPC control plane with real-time streaming. The hardest part was getting the correctness guarantees right and making sure a zombie worker waking up after a reaper reclaimed its job couldn't corrupt state (I find it so cool because I can keep building on this project and the deeper I go I get to learn new things). GitHub: github.com/ryanshabaneh/atlas-queue

The coolest project I’ve built is a community focused events app called Community, developed as part of a team project. The goal wasn’t just to build another events app, it was to address a real issue we noticed: a lot of people, both younger and older, struggle with loneliness and finding meaningful connections. We wanted to create something that makes it easier for people to step out, meet others with similar interests, and feel part of something. The app recommends events based on a user’s location and also allows users to create their own. One decision we were intentional about was inclusivity, we added language tags to events so users can find spaces where they feel comfortable, even if they don’t speak English or French. That was important to us because community shouldn’t be limited by language. From a technical point of view, we integrated the Ticketmaster API to bring in real world events alongside user created ones, which helped make the platform more useful and engaging from the start.

What made this project stand out to me is that it wasn’t just about functionality, every feature was tied back to making it easier for people to connect in real life.

column22:
The coolest project I've ever built is quite recent actually. I'm currently working on a command line RPN calculator based on the HP 28S. I call it "rpnc". Right I only have some basic functions for simple arithmetic and stack manipulation, and also persistent memory. But the main REPL is done so now it's just a matter of implementing more functions. It'll have compatible commands for the most part, and also be fully programmable using a subset of RPL that I'm implementing. In addition I'm also aiming for it to have an interpreter mode so it can evaluate RPL/RPN expressions from files or stdin.

The whole program is written in pure Go, which is my main language. But an issue that I always face with Go is it's lack of built-in data structures like Java for instance. RPN calculators are stack oriented, so I had to make my own stack data structure, which I'm pretty proud of since it uses generics.

It's kind of embarrassing to say that my "coolest project" is a calculator of all things. But it's my current obsession, and I aim to make this as faithful as possible to my 28S. Which is gonna be super cool to me at the very least.

Once I have a little more time (once there aren't 40 billion school assignments to do), I'll start work on the RPL interpreter portion of the project, which by itself is basically another whole project on its own. I've been reading up on how to design and implement interpreters in this book I found called "Crafting Interpreters", and the things it goes over align perfectly with the scope of this project which is really nice.

There's a lil demo in the repo:

https://github.com/caiocotts/rpnc

Yosemite,  a compliance and fraud detection dashboard I built at GenAI Genesis 2026 with a team of 4. Small businesses are way too vulnerable to fraud and sanctions violations but can't afford proper compliance tools, so we built one.

To date, the coolest project I’ve had the opportunity to work on has come from one of my engineering courses, where we were tasked with solving a real-world client problem. My team came together to create Kinetix. We created this so that the client could better understand the physical properties of hail, specifically its potential impact through measurements like mass, velocity, and kinetic energy in rural areas impacted the most by large hail storms.

Within a 5-member interdisciplinary team, I led the electrical architecture and embedded software development. We designed and built a functional prototype capable of capturing over 1,000 high-frequency sensor readings per trial. These readings were then processed to estimate key variables such as hail mass, velocity, and ultimately kinetic energy. The project required integrating sensors, ensuring reliable data acquisition, and developing logic to translate raw signals into meaningful physical insights.

What made this project particularly rewarding was the combination of technical depth and real-world application. It wasn’t just about building something that worked, it was about delivering a solution grounded in an actual industry need, under constraints similar to those faced in professional engineering environments. Collaborating closely with my team to iterate on the design, troubleshoot issues, and refine our approach made the experience both challenging and incredibly fulfilling.

While the project was developed within a course setting and doesn’t currently have a public repository or demo link, I would be happy to walk through the design, architecture, and outcomes in more detail if given the opportunity.
column23:
If I had unlimited time and resources, I would work on building a fully autonomous, resilient food production and distribution system designed for regions affected by war and instability. Food insecurity is a persistent issue in many parts of the world, and creating self-sustaining systems that can operate in conflict zones with minimal human intervention would directly address this challenge.

At its core, the system would have three main parts. First, small, self-contained farming units that could be set up almost anywhere, whether in damaged cities or remote areas. These units would grow fresh, nutritious food using very little water or space, making them practical even in resource-scarce environments.

Second, these farms would be able to monitor themselves, tracking plant health, growth, and changing conditions. Rather than relying on constant human oversight, the system would adapt on its own to maintain consistent food production, even when conditions are unpredictable.

The third part, and what excites me most, is distribution. In war zones, producing food is only half the challenge, getting it safely to people is just as critical. I envision a system that can transport food across dangerous or inaccessible areas without putting lives at risk, ensuring it reaches the most vulnerable communities quickly.

What makes this project meaningful is its focus on long-term impact. Instead of relying solely on external aid that can be delayed or disrupted, it creates a stable, local source of food that can endure uncertainty. Ultimately, this reflects the kind of work I want to pursue: using engineering to create solutions that are not only innovative, but that make a tangible difference in people’s lives.

column22:
One of my favorite recent builds is MyStats, a deep-dive Spotify analytics dashboard that goes well beyond what Spotify Wrapped gives you.

The frontend is Next.js + TypeScript, backed by Postgres, but the interesting part is a Go microservice I wrote specifically to handle the heavy lifting. When a user imports their full Spotify history, the raw JSON Spotify returns can be enormous, we're talking billions of individual data points. The Go service processes the entire thing in under 2 seconds, generating a listening heatmap, a psychological listening profile, and granular breakdowns of top artists, albums, and tracks alongside low-level audio feature analysis (energy, valence, danceability, etc.).

The main challenge was making that data pipeline feel instant from the user's perspective. Go was the right call there, and it was a fun excuse to think hard about efficient JSON deserialization and memory management at scale.

https://github.com/sklyerx/my-stats
column23:
I've always wanted to build a mini self-hostable deployment platform, like a mini version of Vercel, kind of like an alternative that I can personally understand every layer of.

The core idea is a platform that takes a git push, detects the framework, compiles the project (nixpack, docker compose if it already exists, build script in deployment options), it isolates the Docker container, and serves it either via EC2 instances or Cloudflare Workers, depending on whether the workload is best for a long-running server (e.i: website, api, etc) or a lightweight serverless edge function. NGIX sits in front of the engine as the reverse proxy and handles routing, SSL termination, load balancing and deployment routing. Custom domain handling work would automatically by mutating DNS records through the Cloudflare DNS API and attaching SSL certificates on the fly

What makes this project stand out to me is that its essentially a systems programming problem disguised as a dev tool. I'm dealing with container orchestration, process isolation, networking, edge routing, load balancing, logs, build caches, etc. I'd want to build the CI pipeline runner from scratch too, parsing config files, spinning up ephermeral containers per job, and destroying them cleanly.

Most developers use platforms like Vercel or Railway without really knowing whats happening when they push a commit and see the changes live without downtime. When I understood how this works under the hood i wanted to practice with it and build a system and me and developers like me would use without having to face vercels payments or changes. and honestly, the result would be something genuinely useful for developers who want full control over their infra without the complexity of Kubernetes.

one of the coolest projects I’ve built was an Amazon Reviews ETL and sentiment analysis project. I took raw review data, cleaned and transformed it using Python and Pandas, then built a pipeline to organize the data into a more usable format for analysis. From there, I explored review patterns, sentiment trends, and product insights to turn messy text data into something meaningful.

what made it especially interesting to me was that it combined both data engineering and data analysis. It was not just about training a model, but about building the full workflow, such as, cleaning data, structuring it properly, engineering useful features, and then extracting insights. I liked that it felt closer to solving a real world problem rather than just completing a class assignment. It taught me a lot about working with messy data and showed me how much impact good data preparation has on the final results.
column23:
If I had all the time and resources in the world, I would want to build an AI powered education platform that gives every student the kind of personalized support you normally only get from a really good one on one tutor.

I think a lot of students are capable and hardworking, but they fall behind because learning is often delivered at one fixed pace and one fixed style, and is often a poor match for the professor’s style of teaching. In a classroom, it is hard for every student to get explanations tailored to exactly what they are confused about, why they are confused, and what kind of example would make the concept click for them. I would want to build a system that adapts to each student in real time by identifying gaps in understanding, changing the way concepts are explained, generating practice at the right difficulty, and helping students build confidence instead of just memorizing for tests.

What I would want to make different is that it would not just be another app that gives instant solutions. The platform would focus on teaching people how to think and how to learn. It would break down complex topics step by step, recognize patterns in mistakes, and guide students toward understanding in real time as they are learning. If a student struggles with probability, calculus, or programming, the system would detect whether the issue is a missing prerequisite, weak intuition, or repeated problem-solving mistakes, then respond accordingly. It would be less like an AI-powered search engine and more like an intelligent learning partner.

This project matters to me because education changes everything. Better access to personalized learning can improve confidence, career opportunities, and long term outcomes for people regardless of background. If the project is built well, a platform like this could help students who feel lost in large classes, people teaching themselves new skills, and anyone who needs support but cannot afford private tutoring.

One thing that always frustrates me is how much real-world data is not accessible. As non-affiliated developers or students, we’re often stuck using outdated Kaggle datasets, while the most valuable data in fields like healthcare or finance is locked behind their companies. I’ve seen so many promising ideas or hackathon prototypes that couldn’t grow further just because of missing data. Even as a research assistant I’ve had to reshape entire project ideas just based on what data I could actually access.
Therefore, with unlimited time and resources, I would want to build an open, privacy-safe data access and evaluation infrastructure that allows independent developers, students, and researchers to work on real-world problems without being blocked by data access. I imagine it as a controlled environment with high-quality synthesized datasets, possibly powered by large-scale simulations. There will be billions of AI agents modelling how people, systems, and markets behave. Instead of waiting years to collect real data or going through heavy access barriers, people could run experiments, test ideas, and iterate quickly in a realistic but safe setting!!
Of course data is a sensitive topic with real concerns around privacy, national security, and company confidentiality. But with unlimited resources, I believe it’s possible to design strong protection layers and architectures that balance accessibility with safety
It’s definitely way beyond what I can build right now, but it’s something I would love to be part of one day :)

column22:
Of all the projects I've worked on, Inner Machinations is my greatest source of pride, not just because it pushed me to make massive leaps in my technical skills, but because it was incredibly personally gratifying to work on.
For anyone who's taken a discrete math course, they've likely encountered a Deterministic Finite Automaton (or DFA for short), a state machine that underpins everything from regular expressions to compilers. As much as I loved learning about DFAs, I always rolled my eyes when it came time to describe them mathematically. Manually listing every possible state and transition quickly becomes tedious. So, in collaboration with my professor, I came up with the idea to create a desktop application that would let users construct DFAs graphically.
Having just learned about Svelte and Tauri, a framework for building desktop applications in Rust, I saw this as the perfect excuse to pick up some new technologies. One major challenge presented itself almost immediately: how do I actually go about making this? Before Inner Machinations, I had only worked on a couple of small web projects. During the early stages, "lost and discouraged" wouldn't do justice to how I felt. And yet, I persisted, which is part of why I'm so proud of it.
What started as a project fraught with frustration quickly stopped feeling like a "project" at all, and instead became something more like a hobby. Because the subject matter genuinely fascinated me, I kept at it on morning commutes, between classes, even while scarfing down dinner. By the end of summer, I had built not only a simulator for DFAs and NFAs, but a full-blown regular expression compiler as well.
The true cherry on top was the impact it had on my fellow students. My professor liked it so much that he integrated it as a teaching aid and offered me a TA position alongside it. Seeing the tool help students who had struggled with the same concepts has given me a sense of pride unlike anything I've ever experienced.

column22:
One of the coolest projects I built is VisionBuddy, an AI-powered indoor navigation assistant designed to help visually impaired and directionally challenged individuals navigate indoor spaces independently.

We (my team and I) built VisionBuddy during another hackathon, where we combined multimodal AI, voice technology, and spatial memory to create a real-time navigation prototype. The system uses Google Gemini’s vision capabilities to analyze live camera input and convert it into structured spatial reasoning. Instead of vague descriptions, the model outputs clear directions such as “Hazard at 2 o’clock, three meters ahead.” To make the system accessible and intuitive, we integrated ElevenLabs to convert the AI’s structured output into natural speech with multilingual support (currently supporting 9 languages in the prototype). We also used Snowflake to store “pinned locations” such as classrooms, exits, elevators, and safe zones, allowing VisionBuddy to remember important locations and provide smarter navigation when a user returns to the same environment.

The long-term vision for VisionBuddy is a wearable accessibility system, potentially integrated into smart glasses or a smartwatch, that provides hands-free spatial awareness and guidance in indoor environments where traditional navigation tools like GPS fail.

The project was built in 36 hours and was selected as a finalist (Top 15).

What I liked most about this project is that it felt close to a real-world system, since I followed the full process from design to testing.

One of my most impactful projects has been augmenting standard wheelchairs with various
assistive features. This project integrates pressure sensors, a back-facing camera, ultrasonic sensors, and real-time posture analysis to enhance user comfort and safety. My role involved designing UART drivers in C++ to transmit real-time ultrasonic sensor and GPS data from an Arduino to a Raspberry Pi 5, writing I2C drivers in Python to handle temperature and pressure sensor data, building a steel frame to house the components, and designing UI features on the mobile app in Android Studio using Java to provide real-time feedback. Through this project, I gained valuable hands-on experience in sensor integration and embedded system development.
column23: I enjoy playing music, and I want to make playing music as easy and accessible to anyone who wants to learn as I can, because the joy of creation should never be a privilege reserved for those with the "right" hands or the most expensive tutors. In my own experience, I've found that most music apps don't really adjust to my pace, which makes them feel more like metronomes than actual helpers. To solve this, I would develop an app that acts as an ultra-responsive digital coach using nothing more than the built-in microphone on a standard smartphone. Using real-time pitch detection algorithms, such as the YIN or pYIN methods used in tuning softwares, the app would listen to every note you play. The moment it registers a mistake, the music would pause, gently letting you know before automatically looping that specific section at a slower tempo. As the app detects your accuracy improving and your speed naturally picking up, it would use dynamic time-stretching to gradually increase the tempo back to full speed. This approach turns an everyday device into a patient teacher, ensuring that anyone with a phone can start making progress and finally get the hang of their favorite songs.

The coolest project I’ve built is a custom paint application that goes beyond basic drawing tools by combining creativity with interactive features. At its core, it functions like a standard drawing app, but I focused on adding unique elements that make the experience more engaging and dynamic.

One of the standout features is **text-to-voice integration**, where users can type text directly onto the canvas and have it spoken aloud. This adds an accessibility layer and also makes the app more fun and interactive. I also implemented **shiny outline effects**, allowing users to enhance their drawings with glowing borders that give artwork a more polished and visually striking look.

Another feature I’m proud of is the **enhanced copy-paste functionality**. Instead of just duplicating elements, users can manipulate, reposition, and reuse parts of their artwork seamlessly, making it easier to build complex designs quickly. I paid special attention to the user interface to keep it intuitive while still supporting these advanced features.

This project was especially meaningful to me because it challenged me to think beyond basic functionality and focus on user experience. I had to integrate multiple concepts—graphics rendering, event handling, and audio processing—into one cohesive application. It pushed me to problem-solve creatively and taught me how small features can significantly improve how users interact with a tool.

column22:
One of the coolest projects I’ve built is ArguLens, which is a real-time AI-powered debate platform that analyzes conversations just as they are happening. The idea came from noticing how hard it is to track the quality of arguments in fast-paced discussions, especially online, so I wanted to build something that could make debates more structured, transparent, and insightful.
ArguLens allows users to join live debate rooms where each message is analyzed in real time, while the system identifies logical fallacies, detects bias, extracts key claims, and generates a live rhetorical map showing how arguments connect and evolve. Then, at the end of the debate, it produces a summary and evaluates the strength of each side’s arguments.
What made this project especially challenging and fun was building it as a real-time system. I used WebSockets to handle live interactions, integrated AI models through AWS Bedrock, and structured the outputs using schema validation to ensure the analysis stayed consistent and reliable. A big focus for me was making complex AI outputs actually understandable and useful to users, not just technically impressive.
This project pushed me to think deeply about both system design and responsible AI, like how to analyze arguments without oversimplifying or introducing bias. It’s also one of the projects I’m most proud of because it combines technical complexity with a concept that feels genuinely useful!
GitHub: https://github.com/tiffany-mares/ArguLens

If I had unlimited time and resources, this is the kind of project I’d choose because it reflects what I enjoy most: building systems, working with real-time data, and creating technology that genuinely helps people!

column22:
The coolest project I built is PhotonDecode, a full-stack mobile app for processing and storing QR codes. I built an Expo React Native frontend and a Node.js backend running in a Docker container, with server-side QR validation using jsQR.

The technical challenge was real: real-world photos vary in lighting, angle, and distance, causing standard QR detection to fail. I implemented a multi-strategy QR detection pipeline, preprocessing images using eight approaches with Sharp (grayscale normalization, high contrast, inversion, and more) to maximize detection success. I also solved channel conversion issues (grayscale to RGBA) and built a robust upload pipeline that tolerates unreliable mobile networks.

This project gave me hands-on experience in designing intuitive cross-device UX, building resilient backend systems, integrating end-to-end tests, and containerizing for reproducible deployment, all while tackling real-world constraints.

GitHub Repo: https://github.com/lindan4/PhotonDecode

Blog post: https://lindanthillanayagam.substack.com/p/building-a-multi-strategy-qr-code

column22:
The coolest project I’ve built is a system designed to dramatically reduce the cost and latency of Large Language Model applications through semantic caching and intelligent request routing, a project that won GenAI Genesis 2026.

Instead of treating every user query as new, I built a pipeline that embeds incoming requests and matches them against a dynamically maintained set of semantic centroids. If a query is sufficiently similar to a previously seen intent, the system serves a cached, high-quality response template instead of calling the LLM again. This required designing efficient similarity search, tuning thresholding to balance accuracy vs. cost savings, and building a fallback routing mechanism to ensure response quality never degraded.

I implemented the backend using FastAPI, integrated Redis for low-latency caching, and used sentence-transformers for embeddings. The system was fully containerized with Docker and connected to a React frontend for real-time interaction and monitoring.

What makes this project especially meaningful to me is that it wasn’t just technically interesting, it had a measurable impact. In a competitive hackathon setting, it reduced token consumption by 55–75% while maintaining response integrity, effectively making LLM-powered systems significantly more scalable and cost-efficient.

More than anything, this project changed how I think about AI systems. Not just as models, but as end-to-end systems where architecture, optimization, and trade-offs matter just as much as model capability.
column23:
If I had unlimited time and resources, I would build a real-time, multi-modal diagnostic system to close the 'diagnostic gap' in oncology. This mission is personal: my brother recently underwent surgery for a neck tumor that was initially misdiagnosed as non-cancerous, only for us to receive a different reality post-operation. That 'wait-and-see' approach is a systemic failure I want to solve with engineering.

I envision a system that fuses pre-operative imaging (Ultrasound/MRI) with real-time optical biopsy data during a procedure. Technically, this would involve a transformer-based architecture capable of cross-modal attention, integrated with Bayesian neural networks to provide a quantified 'uncertainty score' for every prediction. Instead of a binary 'yes/no,' surgeons would have a probabilistic map of tissue health in real-time.

To make this viable, I would leverage my background in C++20 and SIMD acceleration to deploy these models directly onto medical hardware, ensuring sub-millisecond inference latency. For me, the ultimate project isn't just about building a better model; it’s about engineering the high-performance infrastructure required to move AI from a research paper into an operating room where it can actually save lives.

The coolest project I've made was a Unity game I created in high school for a close friend. Her two favourite games were Minecraft and Animal Crossing so I decided to design a game that fused the creativity of crafting with the decorative aspect of building your own world.

The core gameplay centered around combining the different items available to you to craft new items, which could then be used to decorate an overworld space. The biggest challenge I came across while developing the game was designing the crafting system. My first approach was hardcoding the combinations which became difficult to scale as I added more content. I ended up redesigning the system around structured item data and dynamic recipe mappings in C#. This shift allowed me to define crafting combinations as data rather than logic, making it way easier to expand the game with new items and recipes.

This project taught me how important it is that before jumping straight into building, to design systems with scalability in mind.

Let me talk about the project I made in my very first hackathon, it’s special to me because of a humourous aspect it has that my other, more recent, projects don’t really show off. I think what we made in those 48 hours was the funniest thing I've ever made. The theme was to create a scalable, AI-powered, enterprise-level solution to achieve one of the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (UN SDGs). Maybe you've heard of them- they seem to be a popular rubric used in hackathons by organizers. Our final submission was an AI healthcare diagnostic tool capable of understanding really diverse tones thanks to how we implemented Gemini's own natural language processing capabilities- so you could say something like "my stomach rlly hurts 🥺" and it'll give you a short but formal survey before showing you information you might need, like potential conditions, treatments, and specialists applicable. I kinda struggled near the deadline to layer our logo behind a few of the textboxes, and instead ended up keeping it at the bottom of the page, which honestly looked better than my initial idea, so I wasn't too disappointed.
Querying an LLM gave me such an unexpected feeling of humour. You can literally ask an LLM do anything and it'll reflect in the responses it returns- you can simply ask for a sassy, irritated tone in the response and you'll get one. Me and my team "asked" our app a lot of unhinged things after the event and got many many laughs!
One of the best parts of these hackathons is once the event is over, and the stress from the 2-3 nights is gone, you leave with an amazing project that you can now add to your resume, instead of waiting for the right opportunity to build a project and holding off on it. Hackathons make you show up, even if you *think* you're not ready, which is something I had to fight for a while at first. I'm really excited for my next hackathon at Hack Canada to pick the brains of even more people more experienced than I am C:
column23:
I really want to develop a solution for this at BearHacks: AI and its large water consumption can become an issue in the future.
Even if the stats regarding AI and its water consumption can be inflated quite large by social media, it is still well known that, when training models at least, it can take gallons of water. According to the Environmental and Energy Institute, a medium-sized data center can consume up to roughly 110 million gallons of water per year for cooling purposes, equivalent to the annual water usage of approximately 1,000 households. That’s appalling, but engineers take modern problems and make future solutions, and following my research, a strong solution for this would be to move data centers to climates where cooling can be less of a prominent issue. For instance, data centers should be constructed in places like the Arctic or beside bodies of water, limiting toxic emissions if produced. Appropriate measures should be taken to incentivize optimizing the energy consumption of AI, for example by increasing funding routed towards research and development of AI energy consumption reduction. Furthermore, another issue is the lack of access to physical memory dedicated to AI. Currently, RAM sticks cost hundreds of dollars, and this is due to the huge amount of RAM-based components required in AI engineering. Robust facilities need to be built in greater numbers, in tandem with duly-enforced policies to keep major tech powers in line, to separate the market for big-tech AI superpowers from that of ordinary consumers.

column22:
The coolest project I’ve built is Truth Bite, an AI-powered food scanner that helps people understand whether a product fits their health goals instead of giving generic nutrition advice. I built it because nutrition information is often confusing and contradictory, and I wanted to create something that helps users “see beyond the label” in a way that feels practical and personal. 

What makes it especially interesting is its Lens System. Instead of labeling foods as simply “good” or “bad,” the app evaluates ingredients differently depending on the user’s priorities, such as “Real Food,” “Focus,” or other health goals. It also translates confusing ingredient names into plain English and checks for personal conflicts like allergens or dietary restrictions, so the analysis feels tailored rather than one-size-fits-all. 

From a technical side, I built the frontend in React for a mobile-first experience and used Python on the backend to coordinate the AI workflow. The app uses a multimodal pipeline: Google Gemini extracts text from labels, Backboard IO evaluates the ingredients and generates a personalized explanation, and ElevenLabs turns that explanation into audio so users can listen instead of reading tiny labels in the store. One of the hardest parts was designing the logic to handle nuance, since an ingredient can be beneficial for one user and less ideal for another depending on their goals. 

What I’m most proud of is that the project combines AI, personalization, and user experience in a way that solves a real problem. It is not just technically impressive, but actually useful: it helps people make faster, more informed decisions about food while shopping. Building Truth Bite taught me a lot about prompt engineering, multimodal systems, and how important context is when designing intelligent products.

Recently, Paul Conyngham used ChatGPT and AlphaFold to produce a vaccine specific to his dog's tumour. Although we've known for a while now that cancer cures aren't a one-size-fits-all solution, I think it's absolutely mind-boggling that someone with essentially 0 medical knowledge was able to pull this off with only the help of AI tools. Although there has been a lot of public discourse and debate, I think we still need to acknowledge the fact that this, in and of itself, was an incredible feat. As such, I would like to take his multi-month process and see if there is a way to establish a more streamlined approach to his system. 

Given infinite time, I could ensure that all important facets were accounted for, and through the resources, I could ensure that anybody could access this process for free (and also hire an actual cancer specialist and make sure this system is sound scientifically 💀). More importantly, it could help alleviate the various side effects from destructive treatments such as chemotherapy, and even if it doesn’t fully cure cancer, the designed vaccine helps alleviate some of the negative symptoms associated with different cancer types.

column22:
LexiconAI — a Chrome extension that provides real-time AI-powered subtitles and live translation for any browser tab, built at MacHacks where it placed third.
LexiconAI captures audio from your active tab using Chrome's tabCapture API, streams it to Deepgram Nova-2 over a WebSocket for sub-second speech-to-text transcription, then sends finalized transcripts to a local FastAPI backend running Argos Translate for offline French to English neural machine translation. The translated subtitles are injected directly onto the webpage as a floating overlay, no switching tabs, no copy-pasting.
One of the trickier technical challenges was working around Chrome Manifest V3's ephemeral service workers, which Chrome can kill at any time. I solved this using an offscreen document to keep the long-running audio capture and WebSocket connection alive reliably.
The tech stack spans JavaScript (Chrome Extension MV3), Python (FastAPI + Argos Translate), and Deepgram's streaming API — all coordinated through Chrome's message passing system.
GitHub: https://github.com/nevathan321/LexiconAID

column22: One of the coolest projects I’ve worked on was during HardHAQ ’25, where I helped design and simulate a 1-qubit dispersive readout device. Before this, I didn’t have much experience with quantum hardware or RF simulation tools, so a big part of the project was learning quickly and figuring things out as we went. I used ANSYS HFSS to model the system and run simulations, which involved a lot of trial and error to get results that actually made sense. It wasn’t just following steps, we had to understand what the outputs meant and whether they were physically reasonable. That process helped me get more comfortable working in a technical area that was pretty unfamiliar at first. I also worked closely with a team of four, where we compared results and double checked each other’s simulations to make sure everything was consistent. Since small mistakes in setup could completely change the outcome, that collaboration was important for catching errors and improving the design. column23: If I had all the time and resources in the world, I’d want to build a wearable rehabilitation system that actually adapts to the person using it, instead of following a fixed program. The idea would be something like a smart brace or sleeve for joint recovery, such as for the knee or wrist, that combines sensors, embedded systems, and software to track movement, muscle activity, and progress in real time. In consideration of my background in mechatronics and biomedical engineering, it would include sensors like IMUs and possibly EMG to capture motion and muscle engagement, along with small actuators to provide feedback or assistance during exercises. The coding side would focus on processing that data and building models that can adjust the rehab plan dynamically, so instead of doing the same set of exercises every day, the system would respond to how well you’re actually recovering. What I find interesting is the gap between collecting data and actually using it in a meaningful way. A lot of devices can measure things, but they don’t really help guide the user beyond basic feedback. I’d want to build something that can interpret the data well enough to suggest changes, prevent overexertion, and keep people consistent with their rehab. I’d also want to make it practical to use, not just technically interesting. That means designing something comfortable, easy to wear, and simple to understand from the user’s perspective. With enough time and resources, I’d try to take it beyond a prototype and validate it with real users to see if it actually improves recovery outcomes. Overall, it's a project that spans my background in McMaster's mechatronics and biomedical engineering program in a way that feels useful.

One of the coolest things I’ve built was a small aluminum crucible setup that I made to melt down scrap aluminum and recast it into new shapes. I started the project because I was curious about how metal casting actually works outside of textbooks, and I wanted to see if I could build a simple system myself.

I put together a basic foundry using a heat-resistant container, insulation, and a burner to get the temperatures high enough to melt aluminum. The crucible itself had to be able to handle a lot of heat while still being practical to work with, so figuring out the materials and setup took some trial and error. Once it was working, I melted down scrap aluminum and poured it into molds to make small parts.

What I liked most about the project was how hands-on it was. It wasn’t just designing something on paper — I had to troubleshoot real problems like heat loss, keeping the setup stable, and making sure the pour worked smoothly. Seeing solid metal melt and then turn back into a new object you made yourself was pretty satisfying.

The project taught me a lot about materials, heat transfer, and practical problem solving, and it made me appreciate how much experimentation goes into even simple manufacturing processes.
column23:
If I had unlimited time and resources, I’d want to dive deeper into blacksmithing and researching different metal alloys. Being able to experiment with that hands-on would be really interesting to me.

I’d want to set up a workshop where I could forge tools, blades, and different mechanical parts while also experimenting with alloy compositions and heat treatments to see how they affect strength, toughness, and durability. It would be a mix of traditional blacksmithing techniques and more modern materials research. Part of the appeal is that it’s both creative and technical — you’re shaping metal with your hands, but there’s also a lot of science behind why certain metals behave the way they do.

I think it would be really rewarding to take something from the research side all the way to a physical object you forged yourself, especially if the alloy or process was something new you developed through experimentation.

column22:
The Cloud-Based Student GPA Management System is one of the most interesting projects I have developed with the serverless AWS technologies. The project was aimed at developing a scalable backend capable of supporting student records, coursework, and GPA computation without utilizing the conventional servers.

I developed the framework with the combination of five AWS services: Lambda, API Gateway, DynamoDB, S3, and IAM. The API Gateway was used to deal with REST endpoints, Lambda functions dealt with application logic, and student and course data were stored in DynamoDB. IAM was applied to implement secure access control, whereas S3 was applied to store the related data assets. The APIs facilitated CRUD operations on students, courses and GPA data.

The most interesting part of the project though was that I also automated the infrastructure with AWS CloudFormation, which means that the entire cloud environment could be deployed with code.

The project helped me to get learn and experience serverless architecture, infrastructure as code, and cloud-based backend development, which are common in cloud systems these days.

column22:
I think the coolest project I built would be my first big project I did in university, which was a self sorting pool table through the use of a gantry and claw. It was a project that let me see how fun it is to build a fully integrated system. Through that term, I came to love building mechanical systems and projects, and from highschool I loved coding and making simple software projects, but up until that point, I never made anything combining the two. As such, it has a soft spot in my heart. We first CADed the pool table and physical system. Next, we physically printed special components, cut the wooden pool table ourselves, and assembled the VEX pieces we were given. Finally, we wrote the code to control the VEX parts. It works by having every ball first funnel into a track that leads to a single tube. The claw then identifies each ball based on its colour, and then reorganizes it on the tube to be a proper order. After all this, it then moves each ball to the triangle before bringing the triangle out and closing the latch behind the triangle. This lets people play the next game immediately. It might not be the hardest project, but I found it the most fun and important to be so far. 

GitHub repo: https://github.com/Amit-Weis/bttrpie
Demo url: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=3O0Q1q2LcDg

I personally am frustrated with how our education system works currently with its AI restrictions and its overall marking scheme. I feel that even before AI became as big as it is, our educational system has always been lacking. I know many amazing passionate people who can't succeed because our school system fails them. Whether it is a teacher who ruins a student's passion, or just our systemic tests that focus on memorization over application, I think there is a lot that can be improved.

As such, if I could work on any project, I would love to work on a project that tries to fix at least some issue in the education system. I think that the biggest issue is that teachers can only teach certain courses, so either you get unpassionate teachers, or only a certain variety of classes that limit student creativity. I would like to create a physical robot to help teachers teach more diverse classes that they might not know about as much, for example a class focused on drone design, or basket weaving. This way, if a student wants to learn more about an interest they have, then this robot can teach a specialized class just to them while the teacher only needs to confirm the robot is teaching properly. It would use AI to create a detailed plan for a course specialized for the student based on the content in the web, and how the student has previously best learned materials. It would also be able to teach small group of peers in the same class while still trying to cater to each of their learning styles. This way, you can still spending time with classmates and build your social skills while having a more personalized learning experience. This way, education is more specialized which I think is a big issue right now. This way, each student would learn what interests them, and they can actually try things in high school before committing to them in university. I don't want to see more people losing their passion through our current system.

column22:
The coolest project I’ve built is a real-time network anomaly detection system that combines networking and machine learning. I designed an end-to-end streaming pipeline where live packets are captured, processed, and analyzed in real time using a PyTorch LSTM model.

What makes this project unique is the integration of low-level networking with AI. Instead of relying on generic datasets, I engineered domain-specific features like inter-arrival time, flow entropy, and port diversity to better represent real network behavior. This significantly improved detection performance, achieving over 92% accuracy and a strong boost in F1-score.

I also built the system to be scalable and realistic by incorporating a streaming architecture, which allowed continuous analysis instead of batch processing.

This project stands out to me because it reflects how I like to build things combining different domains to solve real-world problems in a practical and efficient way.

This idea came from a recent experience I had when I went to the hospital with my brother and felt extremely frustrated and anxious waiting for care. We built a mobile first UI where patients can track their treatment stage and help ground themselves with guided meditation and other grounding methods. 

We used React.js with a module component structure, built RESTful APIs with Node.js and Express. Synced everything with MongoDB and used Tailwind to build the UI. column22:
The coolest project I’ve built is SenseMap, a full-stack web app that helps autistic and sensory-sensitive people find comfortable public spaces before visiting.
SenseMap goes beyond typical maps by showing noise, lighting, and crowd levels through an interactive Mapbox map with real-time heatmaps. Users can leave sensory reviews, and we use AI (Google Gemini) to turn those into clear insight cards with summaries, best times to visit, and preparation tips. We also built a personalized match score that compares a user’s sensory preferences with each location.
I worked on UI/UX and frontend, focusing on creating a calm, low-stimulation interface inspired by tools like Notion and Linear. We built and deployed the entire platform in under 48 hours using React, Node.js, Supabase, and Prisma.
Link: https://youtu.be/ZgpSJP3a4nE?si=o3bT7lq0-Rv8vB5V

If I had unlimited time and money, I would work on a learning system that is low-cost and helps kids in remote areas of Asia and Africa learn how to code and understand basic AI without having to spend a fortune on infrastructure and internet access.
The idea is not to build something that is cutting-edge technology-wise; rather, it is to build something that is practical, rugged, and accessible. The idea is to build something simple and affordable that is basically a small tablet or microcomputer that has an application that is mostly offline. 
One of the most important aspects of the learning system is that it would be offline-friendly. There is a large number of areas in these countries that have poor internet connectivity. Therefore, the application would be designed to work offline most of the time. It would sync the information only when there is access to the internet.
Another important aspect of the learning system is that it would be contextual and inclusive. The application would be designed to work in multiple languages. The application would be designed to be simple to use for kids who may never have interacted with technology ( or even English) before.
In addition to learning how to code, the application would provide a window into the technology world for kids. It would show kids what is possible with technology. For example, they would learn how to build simple games using the application. They would also learn how artificial intelligence helps people in real life.

column21:
Clank. Clank. Clank.

That is the sound from my room at 2 a.m. when a project stops being a fun idea and turns into an obsession. Usually it means too many tabs open, a broken build, and me refusing to sleep because I know the idea can still become something better.

That is why I want to be at BearHacks.

I do not go to hackathons just to say I built something over a weekend. I go because they are one of the only places where obsession is useful. You get a room full of people who are willing to chase ambitious, slightly unreasonable ideas and force them into existence under ridiculous time pressure. That kind of environment brings out the best in me.

I am not the naturally “smart” kid. Nothing I build works on the first try. People only see the polished version, not the failed demos, rewrites, and sleepless nights behind it. What I do have is the stubbornness to keep pushing until something goes from barely working to genuinely exciting.

The projects I care about most make technology feel human. That can mean improving a rehab game’s computer vision system so it is more accurate and accessible, or making an AI trading system feel expressive and alive instead of cold and mechanical. Either way, I want to build things people connect with, not just use.

BearHacks feels like the kind of place where that mindset belongs. I want to build with people who are excited by hard problems, messy ideas, and the kind of demos that only come together because nobody was willing to quit. I want to leave with something chaotic, ambitious, and real.
column22:
“Who’s a good boy?”
That was the first thing our trading agent ever said out loud through a VTuber avatar I animated at 3 a.m. It was completely unserious, slightly cursed, and somehow the exact moment I realized we had made something memorable.
The coolest project I have built is VibeTrade, an emotionally expressive AI trading partner.
We started with a simple frustration. Most trading platforms are cold dashboards. They show numbers, but they do not help users stay calm when the market gets chaotic. Trading is emotional, so we wanted to build something that could reason, respond, and feel present.
VibeTrade became an agentic system that takes voice or text input, interprets user intent, analyzes market conditions, applies risk logic, and executes paper trades. It could pull live prices, check portfolio state, explain decisions in natural language, and respond through speech.
While my teammates focused more on the trading engine and backend, I went all in on the experience layer. I built the frontend, worked with 3D models and facial rigs, and integrated real-time voice so the agent could react through an animated avatar. I wanted it to feel alive, not like a chatbot taped onto a finance app.
The stack included Next.js, FastAPI, LangGraph, Supabase, Alpaca, Finnhub, and ElevenLabs. The hardest part was latency. The system had to go from input to reasoning to execution to response fast enough to feel real. Our biggest breakthrough was separating reasoning, data, and execution into cleaner layers, which made the whole thing more reliable.
VibeTrade taught me what kind of builder I am. I love making projects not just functional, but memorable.
Devpost: https://devpost.com/software/vibetrade
GitHub: https://github.com/Tankman61/VibeTrade
column23:
A manga panel already knows how to move. It just does not have the chance.

If I had unlimited time and resources, I would build a system that turns static manga panels into fully directed animated sequences synced to music. Not just image-to-video generation, but a real storytelling engine that understands motion, tension, pacing, and impact.

What fascinates me about manga is that a single panel can already feel louder than a full animation clip. It can imply speed, silence, force, or dread without moving at all. I would want to build a system that understands that language deeply enough to animate it with intention. A quiet stare before impact should hold. A beat drop should land exactly when the visual tension breaks.

The system would combine computer vision, generative video, music analysis, and creative controls. A user could upload panels, choose a song, and the system would analyze both. It would detect motion cues, emotional beats, and composition changes, then generate a sequence with camera movement, panel interpolation, timing variation, and stylized effects. Instead of randomly moving drawings, it would act more like a director.

I would want it to be more than a fan tool. It could become a creative platform for storyboard artists, animators, and editors to prototype scenes and explore pacing without needing a full studio pipeline.

I would choose this project because it combines everything I care about: AI, storytelling, music, visual systems, and the challenge of making technology feel alive.

If you want, I can make these even sharper and more admissions-maxed, like trimming filler and making each answer hit harder line by line.

column22:
For my program's end-of-term project, my team and I built an autonomous plant-watering robot. We came up with this project after a personal story that happened 1 year ago: my family left our indoor garden fully watered and returned 1 month later to find the plants dead. Water for a plant must be given gradually, and over-watering does not mean the same. The problem we were trying to solve is one many gardeners face after they leave for vacation. In an effort to fix this, our solution was to build a robot that starts at a docking location and, after a certain amount of time, travels to the plant and waters it a specific amount, depending on the plant type. To start, we used VEX components and electronics because they had a fully fledged brain, motors, and sensors, and they were easy to build with. Next, the watering system. We got creative and made our own peristaltic pump in SolidWorks, then 3D-printed it. This was our first hurdle, as finding the right dimensions to achieve the required pressure in the rubber tube required extensive calculations and theory. We tried 7 different fixtures before we landed on our final version. Our next hurdle was software-related. We planned to use randomized plant locations; as such, we wanted this robot to analyze the terrain and the plant locations first. This was where we had to expand past our current understanding of programming to Algorithms. In the end, we landed on Depth-First Search and implemented it in C++, which required a lot of debugging and trial and error. The engineering process required to create this project allowed our team to truly understand the boundaries robotics exceeds in the world and the capabilities it can achieve.

Github: https://github.com/Cool-1-2-3-4-5/1A_VEX_Project
Write-Up: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1ONGDm0cDVCJ64NIQCqLFiczNWocJWFD5HBowCO8DlkI/edit?usp=sharing

column23: If I had all the time and resources in the world, I would use them to solve my favourite board game, chess. I have been playing chess since I was in first grade. The thing that makes this game so fun is that it has infinite replay value. You can have different playstyles, whether you are someone who loves sacrificing pieces for the fun of it or a defensive, passive bot. Chess is a very simple game to understand, but the sheer number of possible games makes it super complex, exciting, and hard to calculate (the estimated number of possible chess games is a 1 followed by 120 zeros). There are two types of "solved" states for a game: weakly solved and strongly solved. A “weak solve” for chess could be proving that Black has a forced win at the start of the match, as White is trapped in a zugzwang (forced to move). A “strong solve” would be finding the best move for every single possible position and therefore solving the game. Currently, the sheer amount of storage and computational power needed renders this task near impossible, especially if there is no financial gain from doing this. Chess is already solved if there are seven or fewer pieces on the board, but the storage cost for this is 18 terabytes, which increases to over 2 petabytes if there are eight pieces. Using my unlimited resources and time, I would run a "supercomputer farm" and build the biggest data center in the world, which would eventually let me solve chess.

column22:
Most people care about climate change in the abstract, but they don’t have a clear, day-to-day picture of how their own habits add up, so it’s hard for them to change anything consistently. 

CarbonTrack solves this problem by turning “carbon footprint” into something visible, personal, and actionable. The app lets users record key lifestyle drivers (food, transportation, and home energy), and then produces a comprehensive carbon footprint view, plus a personalized reduction plan with practical steps. The app included a dashboard showing a weekly CO₂ estimate, a decomposition/history view, and recommendation lists tied to what users logged.

Behind the scenes, the build is a classic mobile + backend stack. React Native and TypeScript were utilized for the cross-platform UI. Python and Flask were used for the backend endpoints and services. MongoDB was used as a flexible document storage, and MongoDB Realm was used to integrate with the app and provide a local, on-device copy.

What made our app “sticky” was incorporating gamification. We added XP, badges and challenges, so that reducing carbon emission felt exciting and rewarding, rather than tedious and punishing. Having a leaderboard also added a competitive component, which would give users that extra push to achieve their carbon footprint goals. 

One of the obstacles we faced was with the carbon emission math. The strategies that we could have chosen when computing carbon emissions was quite arbitrary and complex. So, we focused on three of the biggest metrics (food, transport and energy). From here, it took quite a bit of researching to find a reliable source for approximately how much carbon was emitted for each consumption source (red meat, travelling by car, consumption of natural gas and electricity, etc.). These carefully made decision is what gave us confidence that our app was providing accurate and realistic feedback to users.

Link:  https://github.com/utmgdsc/CarbonTrack
