If we can ensure that future generations have a strong, thorough foundation of knowledge and are capable of thinking critically, we can prevent a lot of suffering and we can maximize human potential.

column22: The coolest hands-on project I've done recently was a practical web vulnerability assessment for my Web and Application Security class at Sheridan College. Instead of just coding a standard website, I set up a proxy environment using Burp Suite to actively intercept, inspect, and manipulate HTTP traffic between my browser and a test server. It was fascinating to look under the hood and use tools like the Repeater to modify data on the fly, uncovering how easily unvalidated inputs can be exploited. Finding those vulnerabilities completely changed my perspective on web development, and I want to bring that security-first mindset to a hackathon team.
column23:
If I had all the time and money in the world, I would create a hyper-realistic, procedural training sandbox for offensive cyber security. As I have learned through my current coursework, nothing beats hands-on training when it comes to learning a new skill. There is nothing quite like searching for vulnerabilities using a tool like Burp Suite to keep things engaging. However, most training environments are completely static, meaning that they generally consist of finding flags rather than dealing with the reality of the situation.

To overcome this, I would create a system where a completely new, misconfigured enterprise network environment is generated on the fly. A user could specify a desired environment, like a cloud-based hospital network or a complex web application like a financial system with a sophisticated SQL backend. The system would then generate the environment using Python automation and cloud computing, complete with background noise and logic flaws. This system would provide a training environment that is limitless and ever-changing, allowing students and professionals to sharpen their skills without relying on pre-taught tutorials.

column22:
The coolest project I’ve built is BlackBox Chess Engine Analytics. It started as a simple p5.js school project, but I kept building on it after submission because I got interested in how chess engines actually think.

Traditional engines rely on brute-force search and handcrafted evaluation, while Leela Chess Zero (Lc0) is a neural network that evaluates positions more intuitively, but as a black box. That gap is what pulled me in.

I integrated Lc0 and built a system to analyze its behaviour. I had an AI agent parse and rate games based on “learnability”—focusing on positions with clear tactics and ideas, not just perfect play, and the trained the engine on those games so the engine feels more human. Then I built data pipelines and visualizations like heatmaps to understand why moves are chosen, along with multithreading for real-time analysis.

Youtube demo: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7nbWr8TR6nA
GitHub: https://github.com/jay63683/BlackBox-Chess-a-XAI-leela-chess-GUI
column23:
If I had unlimited time and resources, I would build an AI tool that directly enhances how people think and learn. Most AI tools today optimize for output, which may be helpful in a sense of improving task delegation and automation, but does not improve the understanding of those who use it.

I would build a system that models how a user thinks in real time—what patterns they recognize, where they hesitate, and where they make mistakes and based on that, provide feedback that optimizes the way a user thinks, solves problems, and learns, allowing them to approach problems at a higher level.

This way, you could dramatically improve the quality and efficiency of the output a user produces using AI, instead of just giving them a tool to automate tasks. Instead of replacing thought, it would directly amplify it.

column22: Creating an autonomous robot that uses C++, Arduino, and motion sensors is by far the most interesting project I have done to date. The robot can sense its surroundings in real time and make decisions to navigate around obstacles. I created the logic to control the robot’s motors and make decisions regarding when to stop, turn or reroute based on input from the sensors. The tuning process was a major challenge since none of the early versions of the project reacted quickly enough or accurately enough to the sensor inputs. I debugged the system by isolating and testing all components, making sure that the sensors themselves were accurate, and refining the logic used to control the robot. This project stands out to me because it was the first time I was able to work on a project that brought together both hardware and software and required real-time problem solving, while also giving me hands-on experience in how autonomous systems work in general.

column22:
One of the coolest projects I have built is a WhatsApp chatbot powered by OpenAI’s GPT API. I wanted to explore how large language models could be integrated into everyday communication platforms to create a seamless, conversational assistant that feels natural and accessible.

The problem I set out to solve was accessibility and friction. Many AI tools require users to visit a separate website or app, but messaging platforms like WhatsApp are already deeply integrated into people’s daily routines. By embedding an AI assistant directly into WhatsApp, I aimed to reduce that barrier and create an experience that feels intuitive and immediate.

Technically, the project connects the WhatsApp Business API to a backend server that handles incoming messages, sends them to OpenAI’s API, and returns the generated responses in real time. The backend was responsible for managing authentication, formatting prompts, handling rate limits, and ensuring stable message delivery. One of the main challenges was managing asynchronous message handling and maintaining conversation context across multiple exchanges. My “aha” moment came when I structured the conversation history efficiently and implemented proper state management, which significantly improved response coherence and user experience.

Although I have not yet published the repository, I am currently refining the project and plan to document and open-source it soon.

column22:
As a kid, I always had an interest with video game history, whether it's reading about the Video Game Crash of 1983 or the Console Wars, but my coolest project was finally investigating the console that revolutionized the video game industry by building my own NES emulator: https://github.com/derzz/nex

Leveraging my CS background and a fascination with retro games history, I spent weeks implementing the CPU and PPU internals(the PPU is a separate chip that renders the graphics). Even though the NES is 41 years old, its design is a masterclass in efficiency. Debugging the interaction between the processor and the graphics unit to get a ROM to boot was a really fun challenge to overcome! It transformed my view of retro tech from just old games to seeing how the architecture lead to a boom of video games and creativity in 1985 and also lead me to get a deeper appreciation of clever tricks programmers used in the 1980s with limited resources to produce these games!
column23:
A a player who loves playing Super Smash Bros Ultimate(SSBU) and have attended many weeklies for this game, I’ve seen firsthand in tournaments how the bottleneck to win is high level practice. Current in-game AI relies on predictable algorithms rather than reads or adaptive neutral, and the high latency and toxic environment of online play often hinders rather than helps the player for tournament matches. In more competitive levels, Smash is more like a chess game, where you have to read your opponent and understand their habits to adapt and counteract their play style.

If I had unlimited resources, I would develop an AI SSBU coach/player that could run locally and would play like how a top player would play or based on certain paradigms such as rushdown or combo based. Unlike standard CPUs, this AI would be trained like how top players play, where it becomes important to understand your opponent's habits and reads instead of reacting to a move you make every frame, which is how the CPUs currently play, making them a bad method to improving your skills.

Furthermore, this project wouldn't just be a difficult opponent, it would also be a Real-Time Coach. By analyzing the player's habits and moves, this system can provide proper tips for neutral or provide help to execute a combo. My goal would be to provide pro-level coaching, giving every player, regardless of their local scene, the ability to train against a proper player without lag!

column23:
I would be interested in programming a project in Rust, due to its incredible memory-safety features and performance, which is often comparable, and sometimes faster than programs made using C. According to the Microsoft, 70% of the vulnerabilities are memory-related, and according to Google, 70% of Chrome vulnerabilities are also memory-related. Linus Torvalds, the creator of Linux, is understandably on-board for making Rust a mainstream programming language for the Linux kernel, as well. If organizations switched to Rust-based programming, that alone, without a doubt, would take care of a lot memory-related vulnerabilities in their software.

I currently have a number of projects on my GitHub that I actively maintain, but if it would be a life-long project that I'm really, really dedicated to, it would likely be a solid, reliable anti-virus/anti-malware product, due to the massive effort such a product would require to undertake. And of course, anti-virus the would be written in Rust :D

column22:
The coolest project I’ve built is AlaREM, an EEG-based smart alarm system designed to wake users at the optimal point in their sleep cycle to reduce fatigue and improve overall sleep quality .

The core idea was to move beyond traditional alarms, which wake you at a fixed time regardless of your sleep stage. Instead, AlaREM uses real-time neurological data to detect where you are in your sleep cycle and trigger the alarm when waking up will feel the most natural.

Technically, this was one of the most interdisciplinary projects I’ve worked on. I built a pipeline that processes EEG signals from a wearable device (a headband), performs real-time signal preprocessing on an STM32 microcontroller, and feeds that data into a machine learning model. I trained a neural network to classify sleep stages, which then drives the decision of when to wake the user .

On top of that, I developed a full-stack companion app (React + Firebase) where users can configure alarms and view sleep insights. One of the biggest challenges was dealing with noisy EEG data and ensuring the model was both accurate and fast enough for real-time use.

The biggest takeaway for me was learning how to connect hardware, machine learning, and user-facing software into one cohesive system. It pushed me beyond just building features into designing an end-to-end product that actually interacts with the real world.

GitHub: https://github.com/HashBukhtiar/AlaREM

What excites me about space is that it involves, in some way or another, everything I enjoy (software, data, embedded systems, large-scale infrastructure) and problems that actually matter. A project like this would combine real-time sensing, orbital modeling, distributed systems, computer vision, and machine learning in a way that feels both technically ambitious and globally meaningful.

The idea would be to build a system that continuously monitors objects in space, fuses data from telescopes and satellites, predicts trajectories with high precision, and identifies anomalies early. Beyond just detection, I’d want it to support decision-making, with things like estimating risk, simulating intervention strategies, and helping scientists and governments act faster with better information. In the long term, I think projects like this are important not just for safety, but for building the kind of space infrastructure humanity will need as we become more active beyond Earth.

More than anything, I like the idea of working on something bigger than a product or a company. It would be something where the technical work directly contributes to humanity’s long-term future and space has always represented that to me :)

My coolest project I've made thus far is a set of game macros for my favorite fighting game. I wont get into the specifics of the game itself, but the macro autonomizes many mundane tasks that people would normally cheat in, this is an alternative that I find more fun and interesting. Not only that but I have made some that do give a competitive advantage in order to push the idea that combat macros should be bannable. Specifically in super fast indicators un-reactable to the human eye, the script can instantly counter with high accuracy. 

The macros are made in Python and AutoHotkey for the most user-control for inputs at the hardware level. Automation and combat prediction macros utilize Computer Vision to detect when to activate and when to deactivate to save resources on the CPU. I have tried using various strategies to detect objects via pixel-grouped color detection and similarity and imaging comparison. 

I also took an outdated macro from an .exe file and compiled it. It was then optimized and reduced by 60% in size.

column22:
The 2D action game Wizard Hunt which I created through Processing stands as my most impressive project. The game features multiple levels where players must use projectile attacks to defeat enemy waves while facing progressively tougher challenges that increase throughout the game.

The project became fascinating because of my approach to creating its game structure. I created distinct classes to handle essential elements which included the player character, enemy units, attack methods, and projectile elements. I used ArrayList and HashMap data structures to handle all interactions between these core components. The system generates enemies according to the current level, and each enemy unit possesses unique attack patterns which include cooldown periods and projectile movement.

I created animation systems through frame arrays which I used to animate both player and enemy characters while implementing state-based behavior (idle, walking, attacking, hurt, death). The most difficult task involved establishing synchronization between player movements and animation sequences and collision detection which needed to track interactions between player attacks and multiple enemies.

The game experience analysis required me to enhance game feel through sound effects and background music and responsive controls which created active gameplay rather than basic operational gameplay.

The project taught me how to manage extensive codebases and develop object-oriented systems while understanding system connections between rendering and input handling and game mechanics during actual gameplay.

https://spaceguy20072.itch.io/wizard-hunt

The best project I have created so far is a deep fake audio detector tool called TruthTone++ that my team and I designed and implemented at the largest datathon in Canada, where we won the Best Audio Hack award.

We designed and implemented the tool within 32 hours to address the problem of voice fraud and misinformation. The tool can analyze an audio clip and detect whether it is genuine or a deep fake. However, we also focused on making the output interpretable. We added features to the tool, including a temporal heatmap of suspicious segments, a 3D audio fingerprint, and an AI component to provide an explanation for the output in plain language.

However, the thing I am proudest of is the fact that we were able to combine technical performance with product thinking. This is the part I enjoy the most while building.

column22:
The coolest project that I worked on is TruthTone++, an AI audio deepfake detection engine that was created by my team at CXC Waterloo 2026. We won the award for Best Audio AI Hack that year.

The purpose of TruthTone++ was to detect AI voice impersonation and authenticate whether an audio recording is genuine. We trained a CNN on mel spectrograms of audio, which we converted to 224x224 pixels greyscale images. We achieved ~99.7% accuracy on our training datasets and over 90% accuracy with previously unseen samples.

In addition to building a model capable of detecting AI-generated audio, we also built a complete system around the model. I was responsible for a majority of the backend design and development, including integrating a model-based pipeline for using the model, producing visualisations of how the model works (heatmaps), and audio fingerprints, as well as creating a Solana-based system for issuing NFTs that store a hash of the audio as proof of authenticity.

This project helped me to think past building models and move towards creating something that can be used, explained, and verified from start to finish. It has helped me gain experience in deploying ML systems and working with real-world data in a hackathon environment.

Links:
GitHub: https://github.com/beanscrack/Truth-Tone
Demo: https://youtu.be/M-fwg9Zm7v8
Devpost: https://devpost.com/software/truth-tone-js46ea
column23:
If I could have unlimited resources and time, I would develop a real-time authenticity layer for digital content to verify the authenticity of audio, video, or images to see if it is genuine or generated by AI.

As generative AI continues to evolve rapidly, it makes it increasingly difficult to tell the difference between real and synthetic content. This has serious implications for: misinformation, identity fraud/theft, and trust in online media. I want to create a solution that will detect deepfakes in the context of verification across the entire internet (an always-on verification layer).

The platform will include multiple models analyzing various modalities (audio, video, images) to produce a core "trust score" associated with the content. It could either be a browser extension/API integrated into existing platforms to allow users the ability to check the authenticity of what they are seeing/hearing instantly or have the ability to attach cryptographic proof/signatures as the verification for authentic content and any associated data.

I'm passionate about this idea because it addresses an increasingly relevant issue facing us in society today, while building on my interest in artificial intelligence/applied machine learning; therefore, it will push me further into finding solutions to both technical issues as well as product design issues.

column22:
During my internship at Magna International, I built a real-time logging system for autonomous delivery robots being tested on Toronto streets.

The team needed to track disengagements—when a human had to take over—and report them to the Ontario Ministry of Transportation. The existing process relied on operators remembering details later, which led to inaccurate data and wasted time.

I designed a lightweight web app that let teleoperators log events in ~5 seconds during operation, automatically capturing structured inputs and metadata. I iterated directly with users to make sure it worked in a fast, safety-critical environment.

This reduced logging time from minutes to seconds and significantly improved data accuracy, turning a manual, error-prone workflow into a reliable real-time system.

I’d create a platform that captures these failures in real time, structures them, and feeds them back into simulation and training pipelines so systems can learn from them at scale.

The goal isn’t just better performance, but building autonomous systems that improve transparently and earn real-world trust.

column22:
The coolest project that I've worked on is Spectra, which is an AI-powered system that monitors data in real-time and deploys on the blockchain. The goal is to create a system that continuously monitors incoming data, and releases funds when specific conditions are met (like a smart escrow), but uses AI to do so instead of static rules.

I built out the backend to process real-time input and evaluate that input with the machine learning model, then connected the output from the model to the Solana blockchain so that the model could trigger financial actions on-chain automatically. One of the biggest challenges was ensuring that the system was both reliable and cost-effective; every time there is an interaction on-chain, it incurs a cost. I had to be very deliberate in structuring how data flows between the AI model and the blockchain layer, as well as optimizing for latency and accuracy.

What made this project meaningful was being able to see the complete process work: getting real-time data into the system, having it evaluated by the model, and then executing on the financial decision autonomously. This project stretched me to pull together many different types of skills (machine learning, backend engineering, distributed systems) and ultimately won at a hackathon, which validated both the idea and the execution.
column23:
It aims to develop a system that not only provides you with recommendations about how to make choices regarding behaviors, habits, goals, energy levels, and context (example: time or location), but also understands the reasons you make those decisions in the first place, and helps “nudge” you towards taking actions that support your long-term goals!

It will technically incorporate reinforcement learning, models of behavior, and real-time data pipelines in order to do this. It is designed to operate seamlessly across multiple devices — including phones, web browsers, & wearables — and to maintain a feedback loop in which the system continually learns from both your behavioral choices and the outcomes of those choices. One of the most difficult, and therefore interesting, challenges of this project is balancing the need to optimize behavior with the need to give you, as the ultimate user of the system, full autonomy. Rather than using an automated decision-making system that “controls” you, the system’s goal would be to create a fully engaged collaborative partner with you that will help you remain true to yourself and achieve your goals!

What I find to be most exciting about this project is the impact it can bring. While many people struggle with maintaining consistent execution of their intentions, they do not necessarily lack in knowledge regarding how to do so. Therefore, by bridging the gap between intention and action, and doing so consistently, we could improve productivity, decrease healthcare costs, and even improve mental wellness, all at a very large scale!

column22: The last cool thing I built was at GenAIGenesis, I built Janus a local token compression proxy for coding agents in rust. Basically on cli coding agents like claude code you can change the base url to any website you like, so you can change it to a proxy server that still sends your request to the anthropic API. What Janus does is removes white spaces, removes redundant tool calls and utilizes a semantic cache so duplicate requests don't get sent to the API (i.e you don't get charged). Once all of the compression different compression techniques have been performed Janus sends it up to the Anthropic API and receives the response back. What makes Janus slightly more special is the way that it works with tool calls as we compare the byte sizes of files to make sure no changes have been made to the file, so when claude asks for redundant cat, ls, etc. commands Janus replaces it with the location of that output in the earlier prompt history. You can check out the devpost here, where I won the Google Sustainability Track solo https://devpost.com/software/janus-u9e3cl

column22:
One of the coolest projects I’ve built is a lab booking system designed to replace manual scheduling processes at my college.

The goal was to create a system where students could easily book lab sessions, while administrators could manage capacity, approvals, and scheduling without relying on spreadsheets or email chains.

I initially built a frontend booking interface using React, where users could select available time slots and submit their details. Later, I expanded the solution using Microsoft Power Apps and SharePoint to create a fully functional backend system. This allowed bookings to be stored, tracked, and managed efficiently, with proper data validation and structure.

What made this project interesting was solving real-world problems:

Preventing double bookings
Managing limited lab capacity
Creating a smooth user flow from selection → confirmation
Ensuring the system was simple enough for non-technical users

I also worked through challenges like fixing data connection issues between Power Apps and SharePoint, which taught me a lot about debugging and system integration.

This project stood out to me because it wasn’t just a demo — it solved an actual operational problem and could realistically be used in a real environment.
column23:
If I had unlimited time and resources, I would build a smart, cloud-based platform that helps small businesses and institutions automate their operations without needing deep technical knowledge.

Many small organizations still rely on manual processes for scheduling, inventory, customer management, and communication — not because better tools don’t exist, but because they’re often too complex, expensive, or disconnected.

The platform I envision would combine:

A simple interface for non-technical users
Automated workflows (bookings, notifications, approvals)
Scalable cloud infrastructure
AI-assisted insights for decision-making

For example, a small clinic, college lab, or local business could set up their entire booking and management system in minutes — without coding — while still benefiting from cloud scalability and reliability.

From a technical perspective, I’d want to design this using modern cloud architecture (microservices, APIs, serverless where possible), ensuring it can scale seamlessly as usage grows.

What excites me about this idea is not just the technology, but the accessibility — making powerful systems available to people who normally don’t have access to them.

column22:
Most recently, I participated in Hack the Globe, and built a platform that matches users with eligible energy subsidiary programs in Ontario. This was my first time collaborating on a hackathon with groupmates, which was a very interesting but worthwhile experience.

The project’s purpose is to provide users with energy subsidiary programs that they’re eligible with, but may not know of or know how to apply to. We do this with an eligibility check engine, that checks if the inputted user data meets the requirements for certain Ontario programs. We also implemented features for housing providers, where they can check their tenants and see who has applied to subsidies, and more.

For this project, I mainly worked on the frontend side of things. This includes the eligibility form, the results page, as well as the used UI elements that went with both. This was a cool project in my eyes, as building and tinkering with frontend elements was exciting to learn, and having an actual purpose for the project incentivized me to improve the design as much as improving the accessibility for the user.
column23:
Given unlimited time and resources to build a project, I would do something that relates to my favourite hobby, which is rock climbing. The project would be the ultimate tool for improvement. Essentially, it would be an app or some sort of software that would replace what both coaches and physiotherapists do. 

For example, it would have features such as a tool for analyzing your climbs to see what mistakes you’re doing that is stopping you from finishing the climbing or progressing in general, features for analyzing different grip types when doing strength training exercises to understand how to maximize the effectiveness of the exercise, a tool to analyze if a specific part of the body is potentially injured or strained, and more.

This would be my ultimate project. Without a coach, it can be difficult to see progress, or understand what is holding you back. However, with this tool, it could genuinely help climbers improve at a faster rate while seeing tangible results, and also avoid injuries by being more aware of their potential existence.

column22:
The coolest project I have built so far is an AI assisted data labeling platform that I worked on during two co-op terms at Vista Solutions, a large machine vision integration company. This was my first time building software that real engineers relied on daily and that could eventually be deployed for customers, which was both exciting and slightly terrifying :) .

The project came from a bottleneck in the machine learning workflow. Training vision models requires huge labeled datasets, but Vista’s existing software only allowed one person to label images at a time. This meant entire teams could be stuck waiting for labeling to finish before they could train or test anything.

I decided to tackle this by building a collaborative labeling platform inspired by Label Studio. The tool ran on a local web server and allowed multiple users to annotate datasets at the same time for classification, object detection, semantic segmentation and more. One of my favorite parts was integrating Meta’s SAM2 model and a custom machine learning backend so users could get AI generated annotation predictions and refine them instead of labeling everything from scratch. I also built training, evaluation, and inferencing features to complete the AI workflow. 

By improving the labeling workflow, we helped reduce iteration time and allowed teams to focus more on building and validating models instead of getting stuck on repetitive tasks. Before this, most of my projects were smaller things like games or personal apps. This experience showed me how fun it is to build systems that solve real problems at scale. It made me genuinely excited about machine vision, automation, and the challenge of designing software that helps other developers move faster.

For more details, feel free to watch the presentation I did at the end of my first co-op term: https://drive.google.com/file/d/154hsYPx7Q6QYsd1tncQOzkBBEg75_Kja/view?usp=sharing

(My part starts at 22:45)

column22:
Zenvoy: Never Lose a Receipt Again

Receipt management is broken, people snap photos, lose them, and can't track expenses. Zenvoy solves this with AI-powered intelligence. Simply snap a photo of any receipt, and Claude AI instantly extracts vendor, date, amount, and category automatically. The app stores everything securely on your device and syncs to the cloud, so your financial data is always accessible, whether you're online or offline. It's frictionless, private-first, and designed for real life.

The app is a fully functional React Native solution (iOS + Android via Expo) with search, analytics, PDF/CSV exports, Google authentication, and smart conflict resolution for cloud sync. It's perfect for freelancers, small business owners, and anyone drowning in receipts—with a clear monetization path through premium features like advanced analytics and unlimited storage.

The MVP is feature-complete and actively being refined with focus on error recovery and performance optimization.
column23: Essentially an advanced AI model as sophisticated as Claude, specifically trained on security patterns, vulnerabilities, and attack vectors. Its primary function would be to perform automated penetration testing on web applications, APIs, and AI systems to identify weaknesses, then generate detailed remediation guidance explaining the best ways to secure against those vulnerabilities. The system would be adaptive and intelligent, learning from each security test to refine its attack strategies and potentially discover novel vulnerabilities rather than just detecting known ones. While building this would require solving genuinely hard problems, including ethical frameworks to prevent weaponization, careful curation of training data, legally compliant testing methodologies, and sophisticated algorithms for adaptive learning.

The coolest project I've built is Drift Files, a full-stack personal cloud storage app where users can upload, manage, and download their own files securely.

It was my first time building something end-to-end and honestly it taught me more than any course has. I used React and Vite for the frontend, Node.js and Express for the backend, Supabase for file storage and auth (with Google and GitHub OAuth), and deployed the whole thing on Vercel. The biggest learning curve was understanding how all the pieces actually connect. Things like how auth tokens move between the client and server, how to route file uploads into cloud storage, and how to make sure each user only ever sees their own files.

Going in I only really knew frontend basics. Coming out I actually understand what full-stack development means in practice, not just in theory.

Live app: https://drift-files.vercel.app
GitHub: https://github.com/areejubaid/drift-files

column22: I built a custom 2D game engine from scratch in C++ called BodhiEngine. It includes over 15 core subsystems — a renderer, a 16-track audio mixer, input handling for keyboard/mouse/gamepad, sprite animation with sheet parsing, asset management with memory pooling, and TTF font rendering — all built on top of SDL3. What made it cool wasn't just the technical depth, but the design challenge: every subsystem had to follow clean architectural patterns (Singleton, Facade, Object Pool, Observer) so they could work together without becoming a tangled mess. It gave me a deep appreciation for what goes on under the hood of the tools we use every day, and it's the project I'm most proud of.

So I'm not saying I would do this... but I would probably not wish to make a project on anything that is not essential / highly beneficial for humanity due to concerns like this.

I would ultimately just wish to work on a project that provides support in some way, and solves a problem that is seriously worth solving. One of the coolest projects I’ve built is PathoQuery, which is a project I made with my team at my last hackathon. It is an AI-powered platform that visualizes the progression of diseases through the human body. Users can simply prompt the system with a known disease, like COVID-19 or Influenza, or input a set of symptoms and the app generates an interactive map on the human body, locating where the diseases are most prevalent, and provides a stage-by-stage visualization for how a disease progresses. It was 
built with watsonx.ai and is trained on journal articles published on PubMed to provide reliable and scientifically-evident information for viral infections.

In this project, I worked was mainly focused on the backend which was something I did in the past but I haven't really touched on it for a long time so this project was a nice remainder on Flask. The backend worked by using Flask to pass the data generated from the RAG model to the front-end using RESTful API to display the categorized information in our frontend.

Now PathoQuery is the coolest project for me is because it was the project from my first hackathon but also it was the first time I helped make a proper web application with a proper purpose. I never made a full full stack web application before as it was a new experience for me, where I got to learn a lot of new thing and improve my skills. It pushed me to think about how software can be used to solve meaningful problems, not just theoretical ones.

Overall, this project helped me improve my problem-solving skills and gave me experience working with data systems.
column23:
If I had unlimited time and resources, one project that I think would be interesting to work on is some sort of  “Perspective Swap” device. As for how it works, the device would allow two people to literally experience each other’s memories for some amount of time. It seems like a very interesting project to me as it connect to philosophy and the belief of right and wrong. It would allow for a better understanding of another person’s perspective on other topics and how they believe in that certain topic or subject based on the memories that the other person experienced.

The purpose of this project is to allow for a level of empathy that transcends simple conversation. There are many conflicts that exist between people, but it is not necessarily the fact that the person is unwilling to listen to the other person, but more the fact that the person is unable to understand the other person’s perspective or the experiences that the other person has had. The device could allow for a level of empathy between people that might otherwise be difficult to achieve.

The device could be used to help resolve conflicts, whether it be in a personal level, a professional level, or even a level of society as a whole. However a problem with this project would be privacy and consent. No one would want all the memories to be know so there are many questions this about this project, like can select memories be shown? What even counts as a memory? I think these are very interesting things to think about.

Now this is a futuristic idea, but there has been work on brain technology such as the Neurolink, which can be used to use your brain to control things without hand movement. The Neurolink would be a starting stage so maybe with infinite time and research this project could be possible.

column23: I had a discussion with my friend in Biomedical about biological experiments on rats. He mentioned that the reason we do scientific experiments with rats and not monkeys, despite primates being infinitely more related to humans than mice, is simply because of cost. The price of 3-4 rats will add up to roughly $1, while a monkey shipped in for experimentation can cost upwards of $2500 per ape. We then asked "what if we just used really bad criminals?", but that quickly fell through for obvious ethical reasons. If I had infinite resources and time, I would act to abolish this dilemma and create a simulated environment where we can emulate biological experimentation on whatever living organism we seem fit. This would save not only largely reduce costs for any scientific facility, but also create the opportunity for advanced scientific breakthroughs without the ethical dilemma of experimentation on real-life animals. This can be done at the cellular level, giving scientists the ability to monitor all biological processes simultaneously at all times for maximum information. It can also reduce experiments that would usually take hundreds of years to a fraction of the time. Overall, it would be a huge breakthrough for scientific research and allow exponentially greater discoveries.

column23:
If I had all the time and resources, I would build an AI-powered developer assistant integrated directly into web applications to help users debug, understand, and improve their code in real time.

Unlike existing tools, this system would understand the full context of an application (frontend, backend, and user interactions) and provide intelligent suggestions, detect bugs, and even simulate user flows to identify issues before they happen.

For example, if a user clicks a button and something breaks, the assistant could trace the issue across components, API calls, and state changes, and explain exactly what went wrong and how to fix it.

This project excites me because I’ve already worked on debugging complex UI and routing issues, and I’ve seen how difficult it can be to track problems across a full stack system. Building something that makes developers more efficient and confident would have a real impact.

column22:
It may be recency bias, but the coolest project I've built is actually my most recent one! It's called Violina, and it's web-app for violin/viola players that takes your webcam footage and analyzes your posture for correctness! It's not the most fancy project that I've built, but I'm most passionate about this one. It uses a machine learning model we trained with MediaPipe and OpenCV to analyze over six upper body joint coordinates in real time. By comparing live joint positions against collected baseline poses using linear regression, the app achieves 90%+ per-joint accuracy and provides immediate feedback overlays via a Flask REST API.

I also integrated a simple tuner and metronome functionality using the Web Audio API within a React frontend to handle pitch detection and timing cues. It was lowkey a massive technical challenge to sync live video processing with low-latency audio analysis, but we were quite satisfied with our final project.

The github repo is here: https://github.com/hans-hc/Violina

and the devpost is here, it has a video included: https://devpost.com/software/violina?ref_content=user-portfolio&ref_feature=in_progress

I would want to work on a small sword fighting game. The idea is more focused on being passive and patient, waiting for fights to come rather than starting them. Ideally it would have story choices like Undertale, where your actions can affect future events.

This idea stems from one of my favourite games, For Honor. where multiple unique cultures fight for resources. Since this game is focused on the multiplayer experience, slower playstyles have been phased out. Designing a defensive playstyle means allowing greater chances to punish the opponent harder, but it prevents this character from initiating the fight. Several years ago, the game had a problem where it was much easier to counter everything, so players would have 'staring contests'. Now the characters are reworked with more aggressive playstyles. 

A few other games that inspire this idea of mine are Dark Souls 3, Sekiro, and Katana Zero. Dark Souls 3 does have a counter (parry) system, however you cannot use this mechanic for every enemy in the game. The coolest project I’ve built is T-Care, a platform designed to improve access to campus support services, which ended up winning “Best Project for Improving Access to Campus Support Services” at a UofT x AWS hackathon.

The idea was simple: when students are dealing with mental health or accessibility issues, they don’t just need information, they need clear direction. T-Care acts like a “GPS for support.” It answers straightforward questions instantly, but for more complex situations, it guides users step-by-step to the exact place or service they need, even accounting for campus-specific shortcuts that typical maps miss.

Check it out on devpost: https://devpost.com/software/t-care-2lei6y
column23:
At the moment, it helps students to find their way around the campus in a more effective way by identifying shortcuts that even Google Maps do not offer. For instance, instead of taking the obvious route outside, it may guide you through a building in order to save time or avoid bad weather.

If time and money were no object, I would turn this project into a global system revealing people's actual movements through space rather than just the theoretical path on maps. This implies capturing the tacit knowledge people accumulate slowly such as which entrances are faster, which paths are less crowded, and which routes are more accessible.

What excites me most about this is that it transforms the small inefficiencies of daily life into something that can be solved. Everyone has been late or lost because the official route was not the best one. I would like to create something that simplifies human navigation on a large scale, making it more intuitive, more efficient, and more human-centered regardless of where you are.

This is one of my favourite projects; an AI geolocation app that takes an image input and estimates coordinates (without metadata) using features like the landscape, trash cans, bollards, and countless other things. It recognizes these using a RAG system, where thousands of geoguessr docs and 900k+ images are stored in a vector db to be queried when an image input is given. One of the coolest projects I've built is a website I designed and developed from scratch. The exciting part was the process of bringing something to life using nothing but my own ideas, creativity, and code. 

I started with a blank canvas and worked through every stage of the project myself. I sketched out the layout on a notepad, chose a colour palette, to writing the HTML structure, styling it with CSS, and adding interactivity with JavaScript. It was satisfying to watch a design that exist in my head slowly become a real, functioning webpage that anyone could visit and interact with. 

One of the challenges was making sure it was interactive on both a large desktop monitor to a small mobile screen. It pushed me to think critically about user experience and design principles, not just to code. 

I learnt that building a website is never just about coding but it is storytelling, deisgn thinking, and problem solving. All the button placement, font choices and sizes, and colour designs matter when it came to designing. That gave me a deeper understanding and appreciation of the art. One of the coolest projects I have built is an AI-powered emergency room kiosk designed to help with patient triage. I worked on this project with a team because we saw how overworked and overwhelmed the healthcare systems could be. We wanted to try and automate a part of the process in a meaningful way. 

The kiosk would ask the patient questions and summarize information in a way that the health care staff will be able to review. (As a disclaimer this was in no way going to replace nurses, our idea would always have a real human actively monitoring and on standby.) We used computer vision to analyze facial expressions and estimate levels of discomfort or distress, while also incorporating multilingual support to translate patient responses in real time. The idea was for patients not to deal with language barriers while already under stress. 

This project was incredibly rewarding since it was focused on making meaningful impact. It pushed me to think carefully about the edge cases and usability to prioritize patient comforts while still being effective.

Mixing hands-on development with deeper research into efficiency is something I find incredible interesting, and I am excited to see what I would be able to do to create more sustainable AI solutions.

column22:
Twin — an ambient AI companion that lives on your wrist. It records your conversations throughout the day, transcribes them in real time, diarizes your voice so it knows what you said versus what others said, and gives you instant debriefs. The main feature is a chat that has full context of your entire day, you never have to re-explain yourself to an AI again.
We built a React Native iOS app, a desktop app, a Fastify backend on Railway with BullMQ and Redis for async audio processing, PostgreSQL for storage, and wired a programmable smartwatch from Amazon via Bluetooth as a wearable prototype. The hardest parts were building cross-platform simultaneously, real time audio pipelines, speaker diarization, and navigating Xcode compliance and App Store submission all at once.

If I had all the time and resources in the world, I would like to work on optimizing traffic and ensuring that cities are walkable with support for bike lanes and emphasis on public transportation but at the same time seamless car traffic for those who do need it. 

No one likes traffic. But the impact of reducing traffic and creating more walkable cities extend beyond just saving time. It also brings people together because if people are walking to places instead of driving to places, then there are more people on the streets, helping bolster local atmosphere and bring business to local restaurants and stores. 

To do that, I would like to create an optimized traffic system that analyzes all traffic lights and all traffic in an area and optimizes it dynamically based on the level of traffic. For example, what if there are accidents or if there are more cars or there are events happening in the area. This project would extend beyond just optimizing traffic light times, also extending to proper urban planning, such as having roads that reduce the amount of left turns that need to happen, and replacing intersections with roundabout instead to prevent holding up traffic. At the same time, it would also prioritize people-first infrastructure instead of car-first infrastructure. For example, current GO train stations have giant parking lots before you actually get to the gauge station. But that shouldn't be the case. If we're walking on the street, we should directly be able to access the GO train station instead of having to walk through cars and a whole parking lot just to board a train.

The coolest project that I have built is my game price tracker! I love playing indie games on a site called itch.io, but as many games cost money and large sales are prevalent throughout the year, I try to buy games only when they are on sale. As such, I built this program to send me a macOS notification whenever a game on my wishlist reaches below my wanted price threshold. For example, if I know I am only going to purchase if the game is less than $2 or over 80% off, I input those values into the webpage and I will only be notified if at least one of these conditions is met for the particular wishlist game. The program runs a daily scrape in the background every morning at 10:40 AM, and sends the notification if conditions are fulfilled. I am really proud of how it runs in the background of my computer constantly, without needing the program to be opened. I enjoy how I essentially added a feature into my computer, building something that is genuinely useful to me. I check on the data once in a while and am satisfied to see it still scraping daily! 

Github repo with demo video: https://github.com/alice-zzz/itch.io-price-tracker
column23:
Given all the time and resources in the world, I would definitely like to build a project that solves a very large problem in the world. The first few that come to mind are war, hunger, healthcare, and the environment—and the topic of healthcare brings an idea to mind. In high school, I wrote an extended essay about using AI in healthcare, specifically using IBM Watson for Oncology as a case study. Although the technology ultimately failed in its time, I believe that if we truly assume all the time and resources in the world, it could be an incredible tool for cancer treatment.
The fundamental idea is as follows: cancer research is so incredibly quickly developing worldwide that there is no feasible way for practicing oncologists to keep up with the sheer volume of new information. The capability of cancer treatment is moving way faster than it can be deployed. In this project, AI would be able to do something it does best: synthesize, summarize, and draw patterns from insane amounts of data and use it to recommend personalized treatment plans for patients.
Cancer is one of the leading causes of death in the world, and because it is such a varied and unique disease of the body itself, its “cure” certainly has no blanket solution. All we can do is treat patients as well as possible, with better and better treatments as research and technology develops. As such, I would be honored to work on this project, as, if done correctly, getting the correct new treatments to the correct patients would be lifesaving.

column22:
Just one? This is hard.

One of the coolest things that I created was a finance based class summer hackathon for around 20 finance and non-finance students. I created a finance challenge for students where they had to invest their imaginary money in stocks and ETFs and I tracked their performance over time.
I created stock sheets where I mentioned the basic information about given stocks across different industries, the monthly returns for the stock over time with a graphical interface, printed it out and distributed it among all the participants and asked them to invest solely based on the information provided. 
I also created their portfolio sheets that helped in the calculation of returns. I also kept a bonus point given to students who would diversify and invest in more than 5 industries while keeping overall returns above a certain threshold.
Then I created a physical spinner with various economic shocks such as inflation spiking up, oil prices dropping, etc., was created. This ensure their investment planning were not random. 
The entire contest was purely built from scratch. 

I've also built and managed an online stock competition platform with customized requirements to support a similar experience. The goal was to make finance more interactive and closer to real world decision making rather than just theoretical learning. 
This project pushed me to think about user experience, engagement and how to design systems that people can actively participate in, which is why I found it very rewarding.

column22:
The coolest project I’ve built as of recent has to be DataFrame, which was built with a team at another hackathon. It's a visual workflow tool that combines an infinite canvas with executable Python code in nodes.

The idea was to change the way people interact with data and code by merging the flexibility of tools like Jupyter Notebook with a visual, node-based interface. With this project, users can drag, connect, and execute Python nodes directly on a canvas, making data workflows more interactive and intuitive. It was basically a super smart and advanced online whiteboard.

I worked on building the core system that allows users to create and connect nodes, as well as enabling in-browser Python execution using Pyodide (WebAssembly). This meant users could run real Python code instantly without any backend computation. On the backend, we used FastAPI with SQLModel and SQLite for persistence, and integrated Auth0 for authentication. We also incorporated AI features using Gemini to generate insights from user workflows. We also made a way to output data in various different ways like graphs and numerical values.

What made this project really cool was the combination of multiple complex systems: real-time UI interactions with the canvas, execution environments in the browser, and backend data management.

Here's our deployed project:
https://www.dataframe.space/
Devpost: https://devpost.com/software/dataframe-y41tbl

If I had all the time and resources in the world, I would build an AI-powered personal productivity and well-being system that helps users manage their daily lives in a more efficient, sustainable, intelligent and personalized way.

The idea is to create something that is able to mix AI and cloud technology well enough to actively assist with things like task management, financial tracking, and mental well-being. Instead of just displaying information, the system should be able to be trained to analyze user data like their schedules, spending habits, and patterns in their activity in order to provide smart insights for users to take action on. This idea kind of takes from my experience at the GenAI Genesis hackathon, where I worked on an AI financial assistant. That project introduced me to integrating retrieval-based AI to generate meaningful and smart insights, which I’d like to carry over and expand to help me make a more complete and scalable system.

For the technical side, this would probably want to be a cloud-based platform with a clean, responsive frontend and a robust backend handling data processing and integrations. I’d use machine learning or retrieval-based AI to provide smart and personalized insights, while ensuring the system remains reliable and secure. An important aspect of this would be to make the AI decisions transparent so users can understand and more importantly trust the AI's recommendations.

If working in a team, we would have to brainstorm for specific everyday problems, pick one or a few, and start rapidly prototyping implementations or solutions for it. column22:
Demo Video: https://youtu.be/RcgroYl9ebY?si=Q1vJw9dgWfVnHzYA

Recently, I completed another Hackathon where my team and I built Dear AI-ry, an AI-powered journalling assistant. We used it as a way to deal with mental stress and act as a way for the user to practice mindfulness. We created an AI agent that would constantly monitor journal entries, logging and classifying them and then subsequently generating suggestions and prompts personalized to the user. All this user information was stored in the agent's memory, which we implemented using backboard.io. We also created a working login/credential system, storing all user data in Firebase using vector embedding. This also allowed the agent to use these vectors to associate similar entries, making it highly personalized. Our final feature to note was integrating it with an Emailing API that allowed it to send daily reminders to journal, as well as checkins and prompts.

Overall, seeing this project come into fruition and have a real working prototype was incredibly rewarding, and the fact that this web app solves a real, prevalent problem was encouraging to me.

column22:
Built and shipped the full backend MVP for Arclo, an AI-driven growth and execution engine, as a first-year student. The system ingests live product and revenue data through a custom pipeline integrating GA4 and Stripe, normalizes it, and runs a rule-based diagnosis engine to identify funnel bottlenecks (e.g., activation drop-offs, revenue leaks, retention decay).

On top of this, I designed and implemented a GitHub-integrated execution layer that translates these diagnoses into scoped engineering actions. The system generates pull requests through an agent pipeline, with strict guardrails to constrain changes to relevant files, enforce minimal diffs, and maintain production safety. This effectively closes the loop from “problem detection” to “code-level execution.”

To ensure outcomes are measurable, I built post-merge tracking that links each change to downstream metrics (conversion, retention, revenue), enabling a feedback system that evaluates whether a fix actually improved performance. The overall architecture emphasizes deterministic signals and explainability over black-box AI, making it usable in real production environments rather than just as a demo.

In parallel, I co-built ERoute, an AI-powered emergency routing platform, and won the Google “Build with AI” track at Hack Canada 2026. ERoute optimizes hospital selection in real time by factoring in ER congestion, patient condition, and travel time, while also providing a simulation mode for government planners to model system-wide capacity changes. The project reframes emergency care logistics as a routing problem, similar to traffic optimization, with the goal of reducing wait times and improving patient outco

I want to participate in BearHacks 2026 because my first hackathon completely changed how I think about coding, building, and learning. In Fall 2024, I attended Hack the North, and what stood out most to me was not just the scale or the sponsors, but the people. At the time, I barely knew how to code, and I honestly felt out of my depth. I could contribute ideas, energy, and encouragement, but when it came to actually building, I felt intimidated and frustrated that I could not contribute as much as I wanted to.

That experience stuck with me in a good way. It showed me that hackathons are not just for people who already know everything. They are for people who want to explore, experiment, and grow quickly while building alongside others who are just as curious and driven. Since then, I have learned a lot more, gained confidence, and found supportive friends who are excited to build, break, and iterate with me. I have also been encouraged by a mentor at my current role at Scotiabank, who eagerly recommended BearHacks as a place of real innovation and as an experience he believes is genuinely valuable for anyone’s development. Hearing that from someone I respect made me even more excited to apply.

BearHacks feels like the perfect opportunity to do that. I want to challenge myself in a fast-paced environment, turn ideas into something real, meet inspiring people I can learn from, and hopefully contribute something valuable of my own as well. More than anything, I want to recapture that thrill, this time not as someone intimidated by the process, but as someone ready to embrace it.

github repo: https://github.com/AttaAB/Do_I_SuckOrAm_I_Unlucky

The coolest project I’ve built is a League of Legends analytics dashboard called “Do I Suck, or Am I Unlucky?” It started from a very honest question after I returned to the game and kept losing. Instead of defaulting to “my team sold,” I used the Riot API to build a full data pipeline and Streamlit dashboard that measures two things: how much impact I actually had in each game, and whether the game state at 10 minutes suggested I should have won at all.

To do that, I created a role-aware impact score using kill participation, damage share, CS per minute, and vision per minute, then trained a logistic regression model on early-game features like gold, XP, CS, and kill differences at 10 minutes to estimate win probability. The dashboard then compares actual wins to expected wins and labels games with categories like Clutch Win, Unlucky Loss, and Throw, which is both useful and humbling.

I love this project because it blends humour with real technical depth. It came from a personal frustration, but it pushed me to learn APIs, data engineering, modelling, and dashboard development in a way that felt creative and genuinely fun. It is probably the best example of how I like to build: start with a weird but interesting question, then turn it into something real, usable, and a little funny.
column23:
If I had all the time and resources in the world, I would build a creative tool that helps people turn the exact feeling in their head into something they can actually make. I have always been drawn to creative work. I went to a performing arts high school, and I love music, photography, cinematography, and art. One thing I have realized is that one of the hardest parts of any creative process is translation. You can know exactly what you want something to look, sound, or feel like, but still not have the technical skills, time, tools, or budget to bring it to life the way you imagined.

That gap between vision and execution can be incredibly frustrating. I feel it a lot with photography. I may know the mood, atmosphere, or graphic elements I want in an image, but I cannot always execute them perfectly, and even if someone else tried to help, they still would not fully see what I see in my head. So the project I would want to build is a kind of multimodal creative interpretation engine. This system could take rough sketches, voice notes, references, emotional descriptions, colours, sounds, or half-formed ideas and help turn them into editable drafts across mediums like photography, film, music, and design.

What would make it meaningful to me is that it would not replace the artist. It would help bridge the gap between imagination and execution while still preserving the struggle, experimentation, and personal touch that make creative work special. I think a tool like that could empower more people to express ideas they already carry within them but may not yet have the means to fully create. That is a problem I'd like to work on.

The most interesting project I created was a system that predicted product demand in a business. I wanted to find the most effective course of action to take regarding inventory management in order to avoid the situations when the company will run out of stock or waste money on extra items. I tried three mathematical models, the Naive forecast, 2-period moving average, and 5-period moving average. Most individuals believe that the more complicated a model the better it works. But, having analyzed the data I found that in this particular business, the Naive model was the most accurate. This project was an exciting one to work on since it proved that in the real world, you shouldn't just choose the "fanciest" technology. You have to let the data tell you what actually works. This project taught me  how to leverage data to be cost-effective and make better business decisions.

column23:
Given all time and money in the world, I would create a workspace, which would be so technologically advanced to to sense a person’s stress levels and automatically modify the surrounding to assist them. 
A majority of us work in rooms where the room remains unchanged throughout the day. But our energy and focus change constantly. Sometimes a room is excessively bright, too noisy, or simply stale, which makes it hard to be creative or stay calm when a project gets difficult.
I would like to design a room that would be able to "listen" to the person inside it. The room would automatically react to a simple sensor (heart rate monitor or typing speed) to dim or change the lighting, regulate the temperature and even the background sounds. 
Like when the system feels that you are becoming frustrated it may tone down the lights and play calming nature sounds. When it feels that you are in a high-focus state of flow, it would darken all distractions and turn the light into a clear, energizing blue.
This idea came into my mind when I was doing my internship. Whenever I used to go to office, after a few hours I would not feel motivated to work more, sitting at the same place every time, staring at same screen with the same background. It made be bored. This is why I wanted to make a smart workplace, where the room changes it's surrounding according to your mood and your vitals will reflect your mood. Your vitals can be obtained through a smart watch, bracelet or even a smart ring. 
Creating an environment capable of sensing stress, focus, and exhaustion, we move away from a world where technology suffers to be a distraction but is a shield. This project shows that given the right technology and a human-first mindset, it is possible to make spaces that not only hold us back during our working time, but also make us feel better and healthier and do our best thinking.

column22:
The coolest thing I've built is probably FalcoAI, a full-stack AI-powered code vulnerability scanner that analyzes code across 4+ languages, classifies issues by severity, and pinpoints the exact line where the problem is. It's got JWT auth, rate limiting, a credit system, and a REST API backed by PostgreSQL. It came from a real frustration: I wanted a tool that didn't just flag vulnerabilities but actually gave you enough context to understand why something is dangerous. You can check it out on my GitHub (Anomaly-E-E).

But honestly, the project I'm most excited about right now is something I am building now, and it came from a very real place, I got my headphones and my new iPhone charger stolen at the library. So I built a computer vision-based belongings guardian. You set up your laptop or tablet camera pointed at your stuff when you step away, and the screen goes black while running it. Under the hood it's running object detection, and the moment someone gets close to or touches your belongings, three things happen: your phone gets a notification, you get a screenshot from that exact moment, and the speaker blasts "KEEP IT BACK OR THIEF" out loud. That last part is very much intentional embarrass them into dropping it.

First time I ever worked with computer vision in a real application context, and getting the camera feed, the detection logic, the push notifications, and the audio alert all working together was genuinely challenging and still a log of bugs to fix. But nothing motivates you to finish a project like the anger of having your stuff stolen.

column22:
Still a WIP but it's basically a fine-tuned LLM that writes Python docstrings for me. 

I'm fine-tuning a 1.5B parameter model using LoRA, and results are really interesting. I first created a scraper that cloned about 10k repos from Github and extracted docstring, function pairs. I then created a training pipeline that fine-tuned Qwen 1.5B coder. 

Since Python docstrings are actually quite a straightforward problem, the smaller model punches well above its weight. From my testing, the model is actually better than 35B parameter models after fine-tuning with ~10% of the compute requirements. 

It's actually changed my philosophy about AI. I think we're trending towards much smaller, more specialized models orchestrated by agents instead of large, general chatbots. 

Here's the link: https://github.com/izzbe/tredoc

One of the coolest projects I’ve built is Periodic, a web app that translates menstrual cycle timing into personalized productivity and self-care guidance.

 https://walia-ada.github.io/Periodic/

The idea came from the knowledge that women work on different hormone cycles than men, and that affects their productivity. I wanted to create a tool that would allow women to very easily access this productivity information. 

I started with user research, including a small survey and empathy mapping. From there, I designed and built a dynamic web app using HTML, CSS, and JavaScript. The system calculates which phase of the menstrual cycle the user is likely in and generates a tailored result screen with guidance on productivity and self-care. A big part of the design focus was making the experience feel approachable rather than clinical, using a visual language inspired by handwritten notes. 

This project reflects the kind of work I enjoy most: building tools that are grounded in real user needs and designed to be both functional and helpful.

column22:
github.com/alextgu/deerhacks
depthfirstsocial.tech

At DeerHacks this past weekend, my team and I built Depth-First Social in about 30 hours.

These days, everyone builds with AI, meaning your LLM conversation history can serve as a detailed record of how you actually think and build. How you approach ambiguous problems, whether you architect before you code or go straight to shipping, how you communicate when you're stuck. We realized this is the most honest signal that has ever existed for finding a compatible teammate, and nobody has thought to use it before.

All existing matching platforms (LinkedIn, Tinder, Boardy, etc.) rely on self-reported data. People lie, or more often, they just don't know themselves that well. Your AI history captures something those platforms never could: the questions you ask at 2am, how you react when frustrated, what you genuinely care about versus what you perform caring about. That same signal extends way beyond hackathons, into finding the right friend, partner, or long-term collaborator.

So we built a pipeline that takes your Google Takeout export from Gemini, isolates workflow and sentiment, and runs them through Gemini Pro to extract a comprehensive 50-variable personality vector. Intellectual humility, execution bias, hotheadedness, vulnerability, contrarianism, and many more. Scored from what you actually said, not what you claim about yourself.

The matching algorithm uses context-specific weights, because what makes a great teammate is different from what makes a great partner. We modeled complementarity separately from similarity, and built clash penalties for combinations that look fine on average but are volatile in practice (i.e. both parties are averse to receiving feedback).

Stack: FastAPI, Next.js, Supabase, Snowflake for vector search, Auth0, and a Solana program for on-chain reputation and match escrow to create accountability after people connect.
column23:
One cool project idea I'd actually like to develop is a proper debugging and observability platform for ML training runs. Anyone who has trained models seriously knows how surprisingly blind the process is. You're largely watching loss curves and hoping, with limited ability to understand why a run is diverging, plateauing, or behaving unexpectedly.
The idea is a tool that continuously monitors the internals of a training run — gradient flow, weight update magnitudes, activation distributions, layer-wise learning dynamics — and instead of just surfacing raw metrics, actually interprets them. If your gradients are vanishing in early layers, it tells you that and suggests why. If your learning rate is too aggressive for the current loss landscape, it flags it before you've wasted 12 hours of compute.

The technical challenge I find compelling is building the diagnostic layer: mapping observable signals to likely root causes in a way that's actually reliable across architectures and domains. That requires both deep ML knowledge baked into the system and enough flexibility to handle the diversity of how people train models in practice.
The reason I care about this is that so much ML engineering time is wasted on trial-and-error that better tooling could eliminate. Mature fields have mature debuggers. ML doesn't yet, and I think that's a solvable problem worth solving.

column22:
The coolest project I’ve built is Bonsai, a B2B-focused language learning app that my team developed through Queen’s Technology & Media Association and presented at the Amazon Web Services Toronto office during our Winter Demo Day.

Bonsai is designed to help users prepare for real-world situations abroad, such as conferences or exchange programs. Instead of generic lessons, the app creates a personalized learning experience based on your target language, proficiency, and commitment level. It then generates scenario-based lessons that include vocabulary, listening content, and practical conversations. After each lesson, users complete quizzes with multiple formats such as multiple choice and pronunciation exercises. The most impactful part is the feedback loop, where the app identifies strengths and weaknesses and uses that data to continuously adapt future lessons.

As a developer on the team, I worked with new technologies like TypeScript and Go, and experimented with different large language models to power the adaptive learning experience. One of the biggest challenges was dealing with constantly evolving tools and figuring out how to integrate them effectively, but that ended up being the most valuable part of the experience. I learned how to quickly pick up unfamiliar technologies and make meaningful contributions in a fast-paced, team-driven environment.

What made this project especially meaningful was presenting it at AWS to a panel of industry judges. It pushed us to think beyond just building something functional and instead focus on scalability, real-world use cases, and clear communication of our product vision.

Overall, Bonsai stands out to me not just because of the final product, but because of how much I grew as a developer and teammate throughout the process.

https://github.com/Aqothy/bonsai

My first attempt at building a chess game in Java was a huge disaster. I had zero concept of clean code and literally created separate classes for every color and piece (e.g. WhitePawn, BlackKing). It was a mess of repeated logic, but that struggle was what made me fall in love with Object-Oriented Programming. Instead of just reading about design patterns from a textbook, I got to use them to save my game. Figuring out how to use inheritance and polymorphism to write smart logic was so fun, I got obsessed with implementing features like shared behaviour, en passant, castling, and checkmate detection in an architecturally sound way. Just the process of building something trash and the understanding why it was trash and fixing it was one of the most satisfying things I've done as a programmer. I later went on a spree building Space Invaders and Pacman clones just to apply the OOP concepts further. Github links:

The coolest project I've built is a single-threaded reverse proxy written in C, based on the reactor pattern using Linux's Epoll mechanism. It uses full asynchronous state handling, supports HTTPS and canonical name redirection for one host.

The most difficult thing to get right, was the design of the state machine. It took me nearly four months to get it right.
Secondly, it was really hard to add SSL support to the proxy, because of my design decisions, but at the end everything turned out great.

I learned a lot and am using the project to proxy my own website!
The website is served by another one of my projects: a multi-threaded HTTP server also written in C, but not as ambitious as the proxy albeit.

One time I was sitting in my cramped lecture and noticed that my professor kept having to pull the projector screen up so that she could make space for writing on the board. It was very repetitive and broke the engaging flow of the lecture because we'd have to wait at least 15 seconds every time (which added up).

What if she didn't have to pull the projector screen up at all? What if there was no need to make space because she could instead write directly on the projector screen? These were the questions I ended up distracting myself with during the entire lecture.

You obviously can't use a real pen because it wouldn't be erasable. So we'd have to use a smart pen. I knew this idea wasn't new, but it was very expensive. The current applications of smart pens on projector screens come as a very expensive set (thousands of $). There doesn't exist a pen she could directly connect to her older styled projector. Near the end of the lecture, I finally settled on a base idea! What if I were able to supply a much cheaper 3rd party pen, compatible with any projector by leveraging computer vision and custom software? This would come out to be much cheaper option for all teachers rather than buying a new projector.

I had the idea and some basic implementation steps planned out. The issue, however, is that I don't have a projector... I would need to run countless tests on a projector that I have personal access to for weeks. My university doesn't have extra projectors to hand out and the existing projectors are largely in use by professors. Concerns of privacy dim my chances even further so that leaves me with a resource bottleneck.

So if I had the time and resources, I would buy a projector and work toward creating an affordable bridge between older projector systems and modern teaching methods.

The coolest project I've made to date is PulmoScan, an AI-powered lung CT scan analysis tool that won the Build with AI track and was a finalist for the most technically complicated hack at Hack Canada 2026. 

Users would upload raw DICOM CT scans, and the system runs them through an image processing pipeline, classified them using an EfficientNet-B0 model fine-tuned on lung scan data, and then generated GradCAM heatmaps to highlight regions of concern, and renders the full scan in 3D using VTK.js so clinicians can interactively explore the results in a 3D object, instead of 260 CT scan slides which makes the monotonous job of looking through each one of them easier, and made it accessible through a clean web interface.

We will be moving on to the 2nd stage of the award, where we will be one of the few people pitching the ideas to an investor from Google, and we could get at least 10k in funding.

It was not only interesting making this but also navigating through each step and making a flowchart to make this was intellectually stimulating.
