column22:
The coolest project I’ve built is Campus Quest, a Web3-powered gamified campus ecosystem we created at DeerHacks 2026. It started with something I’d noticed at UTM for years: clubs host events, students show up once (usually for free food), and then there’s no lasting engagement. There’s no identity built from participation, no incentive to explore beyond your circle, and no connection between different communities. Campus life feels fragmented.

We asked ourselves: what if attending events felt like leveling up in a game?

Campus Quest turns campus into a living innovation map. Students open the app and see a 2D milestone path themed around UTM. As they move through it, they discover location-based micro-quests created by clubs. At real campus locations, they scan QR codes to unlock creative challenges — decoding ciphers, identifying sustainability inefficiencies, designing better walking routes, or solving club-specific problems. Completing challenges earns XP, avatar cosmetics, and Campus Coins powered by Solana. Over time, your character evolves visually and your participation becomes a persistent, on-chain innovation identity rather than just a memory.

In 36 hours, we built a fully functional blockchain reward system, a complete mobile app with maps and QR scanning, a wardrobe and avatar engine, and a persistent engagement model. More than anything, Campus Quest showed me that I love building systems that connect technical architecture with behavioral design.

column23:
If I had unlimited resources, I’d tackle personalized, real-time cognitive support for neurodiverse and aging populations. The problem is that human cognition varies drastically across individuals and contexts, and traditional tools often fail to adapt. People with ADHD, dementia, or age-related cognitive decline often face barriers that generic AI assistants or apps cannot solve.

I’d build an AI system capable of continuous, context-aware monitoring and intervention. Using multimodal sensing, brain-computer interfaces, wearable data, behavioral patterns, and environmental inputs, the AI could detect attention lapses, stress, or confusion and provide personalized support. For example, it could simplify tasks dynamically, suggest optimal cognitive strategies, or provide nudges to maintain focus and engagement.

The system would combine reinforcement learning for personalized adaptation, explainable AI for transparency, and federated learning to protect privacy. Cloud-scale computation would allow real-time analysis and feedback, while an accessible interface ensures inclusivity.

The goal would be to empower people to maximize their cognitive potential, reduce frustration, and enhance daily functioning, while also generating anonymized data to advance neuroscience research. Ultimately, it would turn AI into a collaborator that understands individuals deeply, not just a generic assistant.

"To build cool stuff" is what people who just discovered HTML say. I want to attend BearHacks to stress-test my own sanity.

I’m a "Full-Stack Goblin" currently building an enterprise AI startup focused on deep infrastructure (Model Context Protocol gateways, persistent memory loops, and RAG pipelines). I treat hackathons as a 36-hour crucible to test my most technically irresponsible theories. My goal isn't just to "build something cool"; I want to see if I can wire up a fully autonomous, multi-agent AI system that executes real-world backend functions without hallucinating and deleting a production database before judging begins.

After winning "Best Use of Gemini" at Hack the North '25 and placing 2nd at Hack404, I’ve developed a severe Pavlovian addiction to hackathon countdown timers. My workflow is pure "Panic-Driven Development," and I am looking for the perfect excuse to lock in at Sheridan's Hazel McCallion campus, consume a dangerous amount of caffeine, and ship a heavily over-engineered architecture at 4 AM.

(I'm from Waterloo). So I desperately need a 36-hour excuse to escape the gray weather and our terrifying geese population. I promise to repay your hospitality with buttery-smooth React frontends and backend code that miraculously compiles on the first try. We stay shipping.
column22:
The coolest thing I've ever built is VibeMovie, an AI-native video editor that won Best Use of Google Gemini at Hack the North 2025.

The origin story is just pure spite. I have a deep, personal vendetta against Adobe Premiere Pro. Manually dragging clips around a timeline feels less like being creative and more like digital bricklaying. I just wanted to bully an AI into doing the heavy lifting for me. We built a "Cursor for video editing" where you just chat with the UI (e.g. "Cut the silence", "Add a dramatic zoom").

The React frontend and Remotion programmatic rendering were fine to build. The absolute nightmare was the AI. We basically tried to teach a philosophy major (Gemini) to act as a strict logic compiler. In our V1, we asked it to trim a clip and it literally wrote us a poem about the passage of time instead of returning a JSON payload.

To fix this, I architected what I call a "Nanny Compiler". It is a ruthless backend validation layer that treats all LLM outputs as hostile, untrusted input. It forces Gemini into a strict schema, validates the output against actual video metadata, and auto-corrects hallucinations before they ever hit the render engine. We also crushed generation latency down to under 5 seconds using an LRU semantic cache. It actually worked, and it proved you can force AI chaos into deterministic, frame-perfect code.

Portfolio: https://moelshrief.wiki
column23:
If I had unlimited resources and compute, I would build the ultimate "Universal Bureaucracy Slayer".

Right now, the most miserable human experiences involve dealing with government portals, insurance claim denials, and telecom customer service phone trees. It is soul-crushing administrative bloat. I want to engineer a massively distributed, multi-modal agentic system designed specifically to navigate and weaponize bureaucracy for normal people.

I am talking about a system of agents hooked up to headless browsers that can autonomously ingest dense tax codes, parse 50-page insurance denial PDFs, sit on hold with Rogers or Bell for 4 hours using synthesized audio, and legally dispute arbitrary charges on your behalf.

The technical challenge here isn't just basic prompt engineering. It requires building a secure, persistent memory loop (a ReasoningBank) so the agent actually remembers state across a 3-week insurance dispute without losing context. It requires building ultra-secure Model Context Protocol (MCP) gateways so the agent can safely access your personal data to fill out forms without accidentally exfiltrating it. Most importantly, it needs a rigid "Action Boundary" layer to ensure the AI doesn't accidentally sell your car while it is just trying to renew your license plates.

Basically, wealthy people have personal assistants to handle the boring, friction-heavy paperwork of life. I want to use unlimited resources to democratize that level of operational leverage for everyone else. I want to build the infrastructure that completely abstracts away the concept of "doing paperwork".

One of the coolest projects I've built is a Medical Module on "How to Use an Epi-Pen" called "NEPI". One of the coolest projects I’ve built is a custom cybersecurity lab environment running on a bare-metal Kali Linux setup on my laptop. Instead of using a virtual machine, I installed Kali directly on hardware and configured both XFCE and i3 (tiling window manager). This allowed me to switch between a full GUI and a lightweight, keyboard-driven workflow depending on the task, helping me stay efficient when working with different security tools.

What made this project interesting was how I used it as a complete attack-and-defense lab rather than just a system setup. Within this environment, I performed web exploitation testing using Burp Suite by intercepting and modifying HTTP requests. I also analyzed network traffic using Wireshark and worked with Snort to understand how intrusion detection systems generate alerts. This project helped me move beyond just using tools and instead understand how offensive techniques and detection mechanisms connect in a real-world environment.

column23:
If I had unlimited time and resources, I would build a performance-centered operating system from the bottom up.
The idea came from the fact that no matter how much I tried to optimize game performance, the operating system itself was always the bottleneck I couldn't configure. Windows is built to do everything for everyone, and that comes at a cost. Background processes, unoptimized scheduling, and unnecessary services all compete for the same CPU cycles and memory that my games need. I kept hitting a ceiling that wasn't my hardware's fault, but the operating system itself.
My OS would be built around the core idea of being as fast as possible. In practice, that means a stripped-down kernel with no bloat, intelligent CPU and GPU prioritization that recognizes when a game is running and reallocates resources accordingly, and a scheduler designed specifically around the timing demands of real-time rendering and input processing — not general-purpose computing.
Taking an OS course gave me a real appreciation for how deep this problem goes. Scheduling algorithms, memory management, and interrupt handling are all things that I would need to do lots of research on.
It's an ambitious project, and I know it would take a long time, but that's what I'm most interested in. Especially with the current price of hardware, I believe that people should be getting more out of their machines.

column23:
Some context: Most (if not all) computer vision models work fundamentally in the same way that language models do. CV models are really good when it comes to learning how to talk about space, but not reason within it. This is a fundamental issue when it comes Computer Vision. The solution is to try to build a new kind of architecture, otherwise known as Large Spatial Models (as opposed to Large Language Models) that can natively reason about space and geometric characteristics without having to rely on the heuristics of language models.

If I had all the time and resources in the world, I'd use my background in mathematics (specifically in geometry and topology) to design and play around with new types of LSM architectures. Solving the problem of spatial reasoning would allow us to usher into a new era of technology like how the transformer architecture allowed us to do so a couple of years ago, with the advent of LLM's. I believe the development of LSM's is crucial for the bringing about of Artificial General Intelligence.

column21:
very hackathon I've done has pushed me past what I thought I could build in a weekend. At NinjaHacks I shipped a working FTC simulator under a tight deadline. At HackCanada, my team built Scorched, an urban heat island detection system using satellite imagery, ML models, and a live map interface in under 36 hours. Both times, the pressure is what made it real.
BearHacks is the next step. I want to walk in with a harder problem, a leaner team, and less hand-holding from familiarity. I'm at a point in my CS degree where I know enough to be dangerous but not enough to be comfortable, and that's exactly the right time to be in a room full of people building under pressure. column22:
Cities are getting hotter, and the people least able to cope live in the neighborhoods that absorb the most heat. Urban heat islands are a real, measurable problem, but most cities have no accessible tool to visualize where the risk is concentrated or how it's changing over time.
Scorched is what my team built at HackCanada 2026 to change that. It's an ML-powered urban heat island detection system for Toronto that pulls satellite imagery from Google Earth Engine, runs it through a SegFormer segmentation model and an XGBoost classifier, and overlays the results on a live Mapbox interface so anyone can see heat distribution across the city at a glance. The backend runs on FastAPI, the frontend on Next.js, and the whole thing was deployed on Vultr and integrated with the Gemini API for natural language insights.
We built it in under 36 hours. I owned the frontend, the Gemini integration, and the pitch. It's the project I'm most proud of because it wasn't just technically interesting, it was actually useful.

GitHub: https://github.com/geos1l/Scorched
Devpost: https://devpost.com/software/scorched
column23:
I'd build a Shariah-compliant financial infrastructure company. Not an app, but the actual rails that Islamic finance runs on.

The global Muslim population is 1.8 billion people, and the vast majority are underserved by conventional banking systems built on interest-based models that conflict with Islamic law. The existing "Islamic finance" solutions are either surface-level rebrands, geographically fragmented, or too slow and manual to compete with modern fintech. Nobody has built the deep infrastructure layer: the hardware-accelerated transaction systems, the real-time compliance engines, the global interoperability that would let Islamic finance actually scale.

That's the company I'd build. A financial technology firm where the Shariah compliance isn't a feature bolted on top, but the foundation the entire system is architected around. Low-latency settlement systems, embedded compliance at the hardware level, and open APIs that let Islamic banks and institutions worldwide plug in and operate at the speed of modern finance.

The reason it excites me beyond just the technical challenge is the impact. Billions of people deserve access to financial systems that align with their values, and building that at scale would be one of the most meaningful things I could do with a career in systems and hardware.

column22:
The coolest project I made was actually done a couple weeks ago, and I am really proud of it! I built an ESP32-based smart door lock system. I really enjoyed working on this project because it had a combination of both software and hardware components, which encompasses the whole of my Mechatronics degree so far. The system had push buttons to enter a password which were used to unlock the lock which was operated by a servo motor. I think the most fulfilling part was designing the logic around it, more specifically, incorporating a 6-state finite state machine. There was also a lot of troubleshooting on my part, specifically with wiring, power, and ensuring that the servo and controller worked in unison. I also created an app that can perform all of the user interface through your phone. This project not only made me like embedded systems even more but it also demonstrated to me how much I enjoy developing technology that can be used practically. 

To view the project demo: https://youtube.com/shorts/GK7am2XnWW0?si=Gb_LwtYIbQM6dq_Q 
To view the app demo: https://youtu.be/GrHbliP2Q14
column23: It would definitely be creating a global healthcare access platform that would combine AI, wearable health monitoring devices, remote diagnostics, and mobile clinics to make healthcare more accessible, especially in underserved communities. The first thing that comes to mind is that there already exist numerous apps that monitor specific aspects of your health which include calories, steps, sleep, diet, and medications. However, there is not a single platform that unites all of them in a real, impactful manner. The fact is that it is not only one factor that influences your health, it is a multitude of different variables. For example, when an individual is attempting to lose weight, it does not mean that just the quantity of calories the individual consumes will be the only factor affecting weight. There are other various elements such as sleep, age, lifestyle, activity levels, and even vitamins or medication that an individual may be taking. Creating a platform which considers the big picture and provides people with more personalized advice, in addition to linking them to remote medical assistance, would be highly effective. I see it as a personal mission of mine to offer a helping hand to underserved regions with mobile clinics so that more individuals could receive earlier assistance, and more informed care.

column21: Participating in BearHacks 2026 is about more than just the novelty of building, it’s about the intersection of technical rigor and real-world utility. As someone deeply immersed in low-level systems, from architecting C++20 inference runtimes to optimizing hardware thermal performance I see this hackathon as a high-pressure environment to bridge the gap between abstract engineering and solutions.
column22: One project stands out because it forced me to bridge the gap between high-level AI logic and the bare metal of distributed systems: building a Distributed Chess Engine modeled after AlphaZero from scratch. While it’s easy to call an API or use a pre-built library, I wanted to understand the underlying mechanics of how a machine actually "learns" a complex strategy through self-play. The core of the project was managing the massive computational overhead of Monte Carlo Tree Search integrated with a deep neural network. The bottleneck wasn't just the logic; it was data movement. Still in development not on github.
